title: A Constant-Gain Equation-Error Framework for Airliner Aerodynamic Monitoring Using QAR Data
authors: Ruiying Wen, Yuntao Dai, Hongyong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Monitoring in-service aerodynamic performance of airliners is critical for operational efficiency and safety, yet presents significant challenges when using operational Quick Access Recorder (QAR) data due to sensor noise, low excitation, and the absence of key model parameters like moments of inertia. These constraints render conventional state-propagation and recursive estimation methods unsuitable. To address these challenges, this paper proposes and validates the Constant-Gain Equation-Error Method (CG-EEM), a robust framework tailored for QAR data analysis. The CG-EEM employs a custom constant-gain estimator that avoids both the infeasibility of state-propagation filters and the premature convergence or instability issues of standard recursive algorithms. Extensive validation on a multi-fleet dataset of over 200 flights demonstrates that the framework produces highly consistent and physically meaningful aerodynamic parameters. Crucially, follow-up work has verified that this approach successfully resolves the fundamental thrust-drag ambiguity problem, ensuring the estimates are not just plausible, but physically unique and correct. This demonstrates that CG-EEM is a scalable and computationally efficient tool for reliable fleet-wide performance monitoring and early detection of airframe degradation.

title: A Wireless Microwave Monitoring System for Measuring the Pollutants of Triclosan and Glyphosate in Water
authors: Wen-Jing Wu, Hao Xie, Wen-Sheng Zhao, Wensong Wang
abstract: A wireless microwave monitoring system for measuring the pollutants of triclosan and glyphosate in water is proposed in this manuscript. The proposed wireless microwave monitoring system is a long-range, low-power internet of thighs (IoT) system, which can serve as a sensing node for monitoring water quality in a large-scale water area. The microwave sensing system and the LoRa-based wireless communication module constitute the real-time online wireless microwave monitoring network. The microwave sensing system is composed of the multiple stepped impedance transformer (MSIT) with an open-ended stub, radio frequency (RF) oscillator, and frequency demodulation circuit. To suppress the external interference, the microwave sensing system is designed as the differential configuration. The test channel of microwave sensing system outputs the pollutant concentration-dependent DC voltage, transmits this DC voltage to a laptop terminal via LoRa transceiver module, and the host computer system on the laptop terminal can display the concentrations of triclosan and glyphosate in distilled water by the trained BP-NN model. In measurement, a sensitivity of 1.33553 mV/(μg/mL) and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.001 μg/mL are calculated for water-triclosan mixture. In contrast, a sensitivity of 0.87638 mV/(μg/mL) and a LOD of 1.525 μg/mL are obtained for water-glyphosate mixture. The measured results demonstrate that the proposed wireless microwave monitoring system is an attractive role for industrial application.

title: A Piezoelectric MFC Enabled Active Micro-Force Sensor with High Range-to-Resolution Ratio for Cross-Scale Robotic Cell Micromanipulation
authors: Shengzheng Kang, Xiaolong Yang, Yao Li, Jiafeng Yao, Tao Li
abstract: During robotic cell manipulation tasks, micro-force sensors play an important role to provide real-time force feedback for precise interaction between the robotic manipulator and the environmental cells. In order to accommodate a larger cross-scale range of forces, this paper presents an active micro-force sensing strategy based on a macro-fiber composite (MFC) enabled piezoelectric cantilever operating in a closed-loop force-balancing mode. Unlike conventional passive piezoelectric sensors whose usable range is limited by structural nonlinearity and stiffness, the proposed sensor employs a symmetric MFC sandwich beam, in which one MFC layer performs force sensing while the other provides counteracting actuation under a robust model predictive control scheme. By actively maintaining near-zero tip deflection, the applied micro-force is inferred directly from the balancing actuation voltage rather than from the structural deflection. A distributed-parameter dynamic model of the composite beam is derived using modal superposition, enabling precise controller synthesis and state estimation via a Kalman observer. Calibration experiments demonstrate a sensing resolution of 10.12 μN over a measurement range of 21.53 mN, resulting in a range-to-resolution ratio of 2127.5, significantly exceeding that of existing piezoelectric micro-force sensors. After that, the effectiveness of the developed sensor is further validated in robotic manipulation of zebrafish embryos and onion cells. The results confirm that the active MFC enabled force sensor provides a practical route to wide-range, high-resolution force sensing for delicate biological micromanipulation tasks.

title: Oscillation Mechanism and Cooperative Optimization Control Strategy for MMC-HVDC and DC Voltage Divider
authors: Yinghong Hu, Yuan Zhao, Pengfei Liu, Fangfang Zhang, Yu Li
abstract: To address the DC voltage instability caused by a single-component fault in the resistor-capacitor voltage divider (RCVD) of the MMC-HVDC system, this paper presents the oscillation mechanism under the coupling effect of MMC-HVDC's constant DC voltage control and RCVD, and develops the dynamic regulation strategy for voltage division ratio (VDR) and the VDR limiting control for RCVD. First, the impedance analysis method is adopted to construct the DC-side impedance model of the MMC-HVDC system with RCVD. Subsequently, stability analysis and numerical simulations reveal the mechanism of DC voltage oscillation instability induced by the interaction between MMC-HVDC and RCVD. An electromagnetic transient model is then developed and validated through RTDS hardware-in-the-loop experiments and comparisons with on-site fault recordings. To mitigate the instability, a dynamic regulation strategy and a limiting control strategy for the VDR are proposed. The former stabilizes the VDR around its reference value through dynamic correction, while the latter constrains it within an acceptable range in real time. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed strategies effectively suppress DC busbar voltage divergence during RCVD faults, providing support for fault-tolerant control of MMC-HVDC systems.

title: PCS-UNETR: A Physically Constrained SwinUNETR Model for Robust Seismic First Arrival Picking
authors: Yang Li, Haiwei Guo, Xianfeng Gong, Yundong Guo, Duanshi Lv
abstract: To address the challenges of low accuracy, poor noise resistance, and inefficiency in seismic first arrival picking, this paper proposes Physical-Constraint SwinUNETR (PCS-UNETR). First, a SoftLabel dataset is constructed by converting seismic traces into single-band 2D images with Gaussian probability density labels to enrich gradient information. Second, a physical prior-constrained SwinUNETR model is designed. By incorporating an amplitude change rate penalty term into the loss function, the model is forced to identify arrivals on the rising edge of the first peak. Finally, morphological operations define a region of interest, where the pixel with the maximum predicted probability is selected as the final pick. Experiments on Sinopec Zhongyuan Oilfield's Puguang seismic data (10.24 million traces) demonstrate that PCS-UNETR effectively reduces outliers, significantly improves accuracy and noise resistance, and achieves full automation without manual intervention. The innovation lies in fusing physical priors with deep learning via a "global probability prediction + local morphological constraint" mechanism, offering a new approach for intelligent seismic processing with high application value.

title: Benchmarking Multimodal Large Language Models for Forensic Sciences: A Comprehensive Dataset and Evaluation Framework
authors: Ashmaan Sohail, Om M. Patel, Jack C. S. Venditti, Jihwan Choi, Addison J. Wu
abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated substantial progress in medical and legal domains in recent years; however, their capabilities from the lens of forensic science, a field at the intersection of complex medical reasoning and legal interpretation, remains largely unexplored. Forensic medicine uniquely depends on the accurate integration of often ambiguous text and visual information, yet systematic evaluations of MLLMs in this setting are lacking.We conducted a comprehensive benchmarking study of eleven state-of-the-art MLLMs, including proprietary (GPT-4o, Claude 4 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Flash) and open-source (Llama 4, Qwen 2.5-VL) models. Models were evaluated on 847 examination-style forensic questions drawn from various academic literature, case studies, and clinical assessments, covering nine forensic subdomains. Both text-only and image-based questions were included. Model performance was assessed using direct and chain-of-thought prompting, with automated scoring verified through manual revision. Performance improved consistently with newer model generations. Chain-of-thought prompting improved accuracy on text-based and multiple-choice tasks for most models, though this trend did not hold for image-based and open-ended questions. Visual reasoning and complex inference tasks revealed persistent limitations, with models underperforming in image interpretation and nuanced forensic scenarios. Model performance remained stable across forensic subdomains, suggesting topic type alone did not drive variability. These findings provide the first systematic baseline for MLLM performance in forensic science and inform pathways for their cautious integration into medico-legal workflows.

title: Preface: ICCBDCS 2025
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: The 9th International Conference on Cloud, Big Data and Communication Systems (ICCBDCS 2025) was convened at the University of Manchester, Manchester, United Kingdom, from 14 to 16 August 2025. The conference brought together researchers, practitioners, and educators to exchange developments and insights in the interrelated domains of cloud computing, big data, and communication systems.

title: Message from the Chairman
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: A very warm welcome to all participants of the 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering 2025 (ICCCE 2025), hosted by the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, International Islamic University Malaysia (IIUM).

title: LOOL: Low-Overhead, Optimization-Log-Guided Compiler Fuzzing
authors: Felix Berlakovich, Florian Schwarcz, Gergö Barany, Hanspeter Mössenböck, Stefan Brunthaler
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: The Influence of Financial Performance on Women Representation in Films: A Supervised Machine Learning Approach to Analyzing Bechdel Test Outcomes
authors: Juby Thomas, Sateesh Kumar T K, Vishnu Achutha Menon, Lijo P Thomas, Romy Mathew
abstract: This study investigates the relationship between financial indicators of movies and their likelihood of passing the Bechdel Test using a supervised machine learning approach. A logistic regression model was employed to analyze features including budget, domestic gross, international gross, return on investment (ROI), and domestic ratio. The dataset, sourced from secondary data, underwent preprocessing steps such as SMOTE for class balancing and standard feature scaling. Model training and evaluation were conducted using precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. Key findings reveal that while budget distribution shows no significant difference between movies passing or failing the Bechdel Test, ROI tends to be slightly higher for passing movies, suggesting potential financial advantages of increased female representation. International gross appears higher for passing movies, indicating broader global appeal. Feature importance analysis highlights budget and domestic gross as the most influential predictors. However, the model's performance metrics indicate moderate accuracy, with room for improvement in identifying movies likely to pass the test.

title: Empowering Women Through Next-Generation Connectivity: Analyzing the Impact of 5G Networks on Women's Economic and Social Development in Kerala
authors: Harisankar S, Akhil S, Suresh A
abstract: The introduction of 5G networks marks a significant leap in telecommunications, with the potential to drive economic growth, enhance social inclusion, and expand access to essential services. Despite Kerala's high literacy rates and progressive social indicators, disparities persist in access to next-generation technologies, particularly for women in rural and semi-rural areas. This study investigates the impact of 5G connectivity on women's economic and social empowerment in Kerala. Using data from 480 women, a logistic regression model was employed to identify key socioeconomic determinants of 5G accessibility. Multicollinearity checks and goodness-of-fit tests validated the model, while results highlight the critical roles of education, income, and digital literacy. Findings reveal that women in semi-rural areas, with higher education levels and incomes, are significantly more likely to access 5G. However, infrastructural gaps, high costs, and socio-cultural barriers hinder equitable access. The study underscores the need for targeted policy interventions, including investments in rural digital infrastructure, subsidized 5G devices, and tailored digital literacy programs. These measures can bridge the gender digital divide, fostering inclusive development and empowering women economically and socially. The implications extend beyond Kerala, providing a framework for leveraging 5G to achieve broader societal goals.

title: Empirical Insights into Female Gen Z's Perception and the Factors Influencing the Adoption of AI-Powered Customer Service Chatbots
authors: Akhil Krishna J, Athira R, Itty Daniel Skaria, Ananthi Rajayya
abstract: The customer service sector across global businesses, particularly e-commerce, has transformed by adopting AI-powered customer service chatbots. As Kerala is considered to be the most digitally literate state in India a sample of 150 respondents was taken to examine those variables impacting adoption of AI-powered customer service chatbots among Female Gen Z. In this research, by utilizing the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) framework many key drivers were identified and these insights will help business organizations design more effective chatbot solutions tailored to these demographics. The AI chatbot technology is designed to be responsive to the needs of women, providing them with an easily accessible and user-friendly product that aligns with their digital preferences. This paper identifies privacy and trust issues as key vulnerabilities for Female Gen Z, highlighting the need to address gender-based barriers in AI chatbot adoption. It offers solutions such as enhancing trust and security while promoting gender-sensitive technology design.

title: Empowering Rural Women Through Sustainable Cooking Solutions: A Human-Centric Approach to Clean Energy in Kalakrai, India
authors: Maitreyee Awasthi, Suraksha Rajagopalan, Gopikrishna M, Abhinav Jayakumar, Pavan Kumar, Vipina Valsan, Renjith Mohan
abstract: Globally, approximately 2.6 billion people face significant challenges in accessing clean cooking energy, leading to severe health issues and environmental degradation. This study, conducted under the Live-in-Labs® program, focuses on addressing these challenges in Kalakrai, a remote village in Pune, Maharashtra, India. The village's reliance on firewood, particularly by women who spend hours gathering it, contributes to health risks such as respiratory diseases and environmental issues like deforestation. Using Human-Centric Design (HCD) principles and Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA) tools such as resource mapping and problem tree analysis, this research proposes a sustainable solution-a community-based biogas plant. This intervention not only mitigates health and environmental risks but also reduces the burden on women, promoting gender equality by alleviating the disproportionate physical and health strain they endure. The project aligns with the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), specifically SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-Being), SDG 5 (Gender Equality), and SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy). The implementation of this biogas plant aims to improve the quality of life for rural women, foster gender equality, and create a more sustainable environment for the community.

title: A Low Profile S-Band Antenna for 1U CubeSat Communication Systems
authors: Ghaith Al Makdami, Omar Al Alawi, Jasim Al Maharbi, Hassan Al Lawati
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This paper presents the design and performance evaluation of a circularly polarized patch antenna intended for CubeSat applications. The initial design employs a square patch antenna with side truncations on a 3.2 mm FR4 substrate (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon_{r}= 4.4$</tex>) and is optimized to operate at 3.1 GHz. To enhance the bandwidth and polarization performance, parasitic patches were incorporated around the main radiator. A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 3$</tex> patch configuration demonstrated a wide impedance bandwidth of 57%, spanning from 2.9 GHz to 4.8 GHz, and achieved circular polarization within the 3.0-3.25 GHz range, with a 3-dB axial ratio bandwidth of 8%. The antenna gain at the center frequency was approximately 1.8 dBi. Further enhancement was achieved by increasing the array to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \times 5$</tex> configuration, which remained within the 1 U CubeSat form factor. This design extended the axial ratio bandwidth to 10.8% and improved the gain significantly to around 7 dBi. The results confirm the potential of using parasitic patch arrays to meet the compactness and performance requirements of small satellite communication systems

title: Minimax-Type Band-Pass Filters with Full-Band Center-Frequency Tuning and Narrow Transition-Band
authors: Tian-Bo Deng
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The paper investigates designing a recursive band-pass filter that has a full-band variable passband center frequency (CF), while its transition bands are extremely narrow. Furthermore, since the filter structure is recursive, the stability guarantee tops the overall design issues. This paper shows a stability-guarantee scheme using parameter transformations to meet the stability requirement. Based on this scheme, the minimax design criterion is adopted to design a bandpass filter with full-band CF tuning and sharpened transition bands. The minimax design suppresses the peak deviation of the filter's amplitude response and thus produces a minimax-type variable-CF bandpass filter with absolute stability guarantee. To validate this stability-guaranteed minimax design scheme, we conduct computer simulations to demonstrate that the minimax design can obtain a bandpass variable-CF filter with a full-band tunable passband CF and extremely narrow transition bands. Moreover, the variable-CF bandpass filter is not only stable, but also exhibits superior CF-tunable performance.

title: Satellite Reliability Analysis Using Mathematical Prediction Models: A Case Study of Satellite Anomalies
authors: Muhammad Imran Haris Ahmad Mahmood, Nadirah Abdul Rahim, Abdul Mutholib, Mahayaudin M. Mansor
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The importance of satellites in delivering critical services including communication systems, navigation, and earth observation heavily depends on system reliability. Satellite reliability is defined as the probability that a system is failurefree under specified conditions to ensure the satellites remain safe, efficient and long-lasting. Previous studies highlighted the impact of satellite anomalies as significant contributors to objective failures, cost inefficiencies, and satellite system degradation. However, these studies often lack a comprehensive analysis of reliability using structured mathematical models. Hence, this research addresses this gap by analyzing 87 satellite data from the Seradata database using different mathematical models, categorized into parametric and non-parametric models. The parametric models are Exponential, Weibull and Poisson, whereas the non-parametric models are Kaplan-Meir and Monte Carlo. A case study of satellite anomalies is conducted focusing on satellite communication subsystems of failed satellites due to anomalies. The detected anomalies vary from antenna, transponder, amplifier and battery subsystems. Then, the mathematical models are developed and compared to evaluate the study's objective in obtaining the best mathematical model based on the lowest Root Mean Square Error (RMSE). Based on the five mathematical models, the results reveal that the Exponential model exhibits the best overall performance with the lowest RMSE values for antenna, transponder, amplifier and battery of 0.22, 0.27, 0.30 and 0.30 respectively. The findings of this study demonstrate the advantages of having multiple mathematical model evaluations that enhance the selection of models in satellite reliability analysis for the development of satellite design and operation.

title: Visual Consistency in Formal Modeling: An Empirical Evaluation
authors: Yiliang Liang, Avinash Palliyil, Eunsuk Kang, Joshua Sunshine
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: A Dual-Band Antenna System Based on Decoupling Structure Reuse Strategy
authors: Bi-Tian Chai, Xin Geng, Qiang Xing, Wei Qin, Wen-Wen Yang, Jian-Xin Chen
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This article proposes a dual-band antenna system based on the reuse of decoupling structure for 5G Sub-6GHz applications. In this design, metal patches are added at adjacent edges on the surface of the dielectric resonator antenna (DRA) to effectively reduce the electric-field distribution within the coupled element, thereby achieving decoupling for the 1×2 Multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) DRAs array. Furthermore, the metal patches are reused as the electric dipole and combined with the metal vias introduced inside the DRA, work together to form a magneto-electric dipole (MED) antenna. Consequently, the antenna achieves dual-band operation: 1×2 DRAs operate in the 5G N79 band (covering 4.28-5.05 GHz), with in-band isolation better than 25 dB, while the MED antenna operates in the N78 band (covering 3.24-4.15 GHz), exhibiting a peak gain of 5.44 dBi. Owing to the polarization orthogonality between the 1×2 DRAs and MED antenna, independent control of the two antennas is achieved under tightly integrated physical structure conditions, significantly enhancing the overall design flexibility and application potential.

title: BiDepNet: A Bidirectional Enhancement Network for Depression Detection via Facial Expression Analysis
authors: Min Hu, Yongkang Lei, Xiaohua Wang, Lei He, Jiaoyun Yang, Ning An
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Research has shown that facial expressions can effectively infer the severity of depression. Recently, depression assessment methods based on images or facial landmarks have gained widespread attention. However, existing methods have not fully considered the complementary and synergistic effects between facial landmark features and images in extracting depression cues. Therefore, this article proposes a bidirectional depression detection network, aimed at comprehensively capturing facial depression cues. We employ a differential feature enhancement module to extract the differences between features and inject them into the corresponding features. Simultaneously, the common feature enhancement module utilizes facial landmark-based structured positional information as a guiding signal, directing the model to focus on depression-related information within the facial image features and fuse shared features. Furthermore, through adaptive feature fusion, we enhance the expressive power of depression-related features to improve the accuracy of assessment. We conducted extensive experiments on the AVEC2014 depression dataset and the RAF-DB facial expression dataset. The experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the current state-of-the-art methods, providing strong evidence of its effectiveness.

title: Privacy-Preserving Task Offloading Scheme Based on Blockchain Federated Learning in End-Edge-Cloud Computing Environments
authors: Shi Dong, Ruizhe Hou
abstract: With the continuous advancement of the Internet of Things (IoT) and mobile terminal technologies, the massive data generated by end devices poses new challenges for real-time processing in cloud computing. As a beneficial complement to cloud computing, edge computing has garnered significant attention due to its ability to provide low-latency services near the data source. However, user privacy data involved in the task offloading process faces leakage risks in open edge architectures, and existing solutions still lack synergy between privacy protection and computational efficiency. To address this, this paper proposes a privacy-preserving task offloading scheme for end-edge-cloud architectures based on blockchain federated learning. The main contributions are as follows: (1) constructing a collaborative computing architecture that integrates end devices, edge servers, and cloud centers; (2) proposing a privacy-preserving task offloading mechanism that integrates federated learning and blockchain technology, enabling distributed model training and secure auditing without sharing raw data; (3) validating the superiority of the proposed scheme in terms of energy consumption, latency, and privacy protection level through simulation experiments. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed scheme significantly enhances user privacy protection while maintaining efficient task processing, providing a reliable technical approach for privacy-sensitive applications in edge computing environments.

title: Smooth Collision Avoidance Decision-Making Method for Unmanned Surface Vehicles Under Marine Environmental Disturbances
authors: Yuqin Li, Defeng Wu, Zhenhong Fan, Guoqiang Li, Te Lian, Weichen Ruan
abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has been increasingly used for collision avoidance in unmanned surface vehicles (USVs). However, policies trained under dynamic and disturbed conditions often produce rapid and high amplitude control variations when responding to collision threats in practical multi-vessel encounter scenarios. Such aggressive action changes can accelerate actuator wear and shorten the service life of the control system. To address this issue, a smooth collision-avoidance decision-making method is developed by integrating Mamba sequence modeling, masked attention, and DRL. Firstly, a composite reward function is constructed by jointly accounting for the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, velocity-obstacle (VO)-based guidance, navigational safety, and action smoothness, which alleviates reward sparsity and balances safety with control stability. Secondly, Mamba sequence modeling and masked attention are embedded into the actor–critic framework to enable dynamic feature extraction and temporal dependency modeling in multi-target encounters, thereby improving robustness and adaptability in disturbed and uncertain environments. Extensive simulations were conducted against multiple DRL baselines. Under the strongest disturbance with 10 target ships, the proposed method achieved a success rate of 0.87, yielding a 20% improvement over SAC, while the mean per-step torque variation was reduced by 36.5% compared with SAC. In addition, real-field experiments were carried out to validate the learned policy, demonstrating its practical applicability and deployment potential in realistic marine environments.

title: Susceptibility Evaluation of Layout-Based 2:1 Multiplexer Topologies
authors: Ana Flávia D. Reis, Eduardo A. C. da Costa, Rafael B. Schvittz
abstract: Technology scaling has improved performance but also increased the susceptibility of integrated circuits to radiation-induced faults, such as Single Event Transients (SETs). Since physical layout influences SET generation and propagation, layout-level analysis is essential. This work evaluated the susceptibility to single-event transients, including area, robustness, power consumption, and performance of seven 2:1 multiplexer topologies. It also compares the proposed designs and a traditional design in a standard cell library. The evaluation is based on both physical layout characteristics and electrical simulations, utilizing the IHP 130 nm open-source PDK. Results show key trade-offs in selecting a multiplexer topology, since no topology proved superior in all aspects simultaneously. The proposed methodology enables a more accurate assessment of radiation susceptibility, overcoming the limitations of purely transistor-level approaches.

title: Comparison of Efficientnet B3 and Inception V3 Architecture Performance in Heart Disease Arrhythmia Image Classification
authors: J. Prayoga, Melinda Melinda, Teuku Yuliar Arif, Herlina Dimiati
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Early detection of cardiac arrhythmias is essential to prevent complications and improve patient outcomes. The ECG is a key diagnostic tool, but manual analysis requires expertise and is prone to error, necessitating automated classification. This study compares the performance of two deep learning models, Inception V3 and EfficientNet B3, for classifying arrhythmias using the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database, which contains 48 ECG recordings from 47 individuals aged 23–89. The ECG signals were denoised using a bandpass filter, features extracted with Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT), and then transformed into vector images for input to the models. Results show that Inception V3 outperformed EfficientNet B3, achieving 99.28% accuracy, 99.72% precision, 98.89% sensitivity, and a 99.30% F1-score, compared to EfficientNet B3's 98.12% accuracy, 98.06% precision, 98.33% sensitivity, and 98.20% F1-score. These findings highlight Inception V3's superior performance and its strong potential in supporting automated ECG-based arrhythmia diagnosis in clinical settings.

title: An Intelligent Movie Recommender System Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Nour Mohamed, Sam Ansari, Soliman Mahmoud, Khawla A. Alnajjar, Luke Topham, Abir Hussain
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: In the era of big data, personalized recommendation systems are pivotal in enhancing user engagement and driving revenue across digital platforms. However, conventional recommendation techniques often struggle with scalability, coldstart issues, and adapting to dynamic user preferences. This paper presents an intelligent deep reinforcement learning-based movie recommender (DRL-MR) system that leverages the synergy of actor-critic architectures and deep Q-networks within a Markov decision process (MDP) framework. Unlike traditional static models, DRL-MR continuously learns and adapts to user behavior through sequential interactions and real-time feedback. Evaluated on the widely used MovieLens dataset, the system demonstrates significant improvements in recall and ranking metrics, validating its capability to deliver adaptive, personalized recommendations. Despite challenges in precision due to the absence of contextual features, the proposed model establishes a strong foundation for scalable, real-time recommender systems. The results highlight the potential of the proposed architecture for deployment in dynamic, high-traffic environments such as online streaming and e-commerce platforms.

title: Integrating Large Language Models and Vector Databases for Podcast Semantic Search
authors: I-Ting Lin, Chia-Hao Chiu, Bo-Tang Liao, Bo-Jyun Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: With the rapid growth of podcast consumption, traditional keyword-based search methods face inherent limitations in handling unstructured audio data. Existing systems often rely on metadata or ASR transcripts, which are error-prone and fail to capture deeper semantics. To address these challenges, this study proposes a podcast semantic search system that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) with vector databases. Transcribed podcast text is transformed into semantic embeddings, enabling context-aware and personalized retrieval. The system follows a two-stage workflow: (1) offline transcription and embedding generation for vector indexing, and (2) online real-time semantic retrieval through similarity matching and LLM-based refinement. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Based on 238 valid survey responses and 100 controlled queries, the system significantly outperformed traditional search methods, achieving a mean relevance score of 5.62 versus 1.17 on a 1-7 scale <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(t=8.665, p<0.0001)$</tex>. It also reached a semantic score ratio of 82.62%, reflecting improved semantic precision and user satisfaction. These findings demonstrate the potential of combining LLMs with vector databases to advance podcast search and retrieval. By enhancing semantic relevance, adaptability, and user-centered reliability, the proposed system provides a robust framework for intelligent audio retrieval.

title: Reliability Assessment of Deep Neural Networks and Accelerators Across Design Stages
authors: Mohammad Hasan Ahmadilivani, Natalia Cherezova, Michael Glaß, Juan-David Guerrero-Balaguera, Maksim Jenihhin, Angeliki Kritikakou, Robert Limas Sierra, Leticia Bolzani Poelhs, Jaan Raik, Lucas Roquet, Fernando Fernandes dos Santos, Felipe Augusto da Silva, Matteo Sonza Reorda, Alessandro Veronesi, Ernesto Cristopher Villegas Castillo, Josie E. Rodriguez Condia
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is driving the introduction of advanced functionality and autonomy across several domains, including automotive, robotics, health care, and aerospace. Such advances are supported by the availability of algorithms and hardware accelerator platforms to efficiently implement them. However, transistor miniaturization raises reliability concerns during in-field operation and poses challenges in terms of computational intensity and performance for which conventional design and fault-characterization strategies are not well-suited. This work reviews three approaches for determining fault vulnerabilities and reliability challenges in hardware accelerators, devices, and software for Deep Neural Networks. The first strategy describes a cost-effective semi-analytical reliability assessment method for the design space exploration of reliability-performance trade-offs using reconfigurable systolic array engines. The second strategy analyzes and evaluates four abstraction levels for dot-product-unit-based accelerators and assesses the implications of selecting an affordable strategy based on the analysis needs. The third approach analyzes the critical role of correctly selecting representative inputs in large Vision Transformers (ViTs) for their effective reliability evaluation.

title: A Descriptive Mixed-Methods Approach to Activity Evaluation Using Natural Language Processing
authors: Mark Anthony A. Ozaeta, Zennia G. Zamora, Rodel D. Magin
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This study explores stakeholder perceptions of institutional activities at an educational institution through the application of advanced text mining and natural language processing (NLP) techniques. Recognizing the vital role that academic and co-curricular engagements play in holistic student development, the research aims to assess feedback on such activities using AI-driven analytical tools. By applying sentiment analysis, co-occurrence network analysis, and word cloud visualization to textual evaluations, the study provides insights into emotional tones, recurring themes, and key areas of interest. Results reveal a predominance of positive sentiments, emphasizing satisfaction with the quality, organization, and impact of activities. Negative and uncertain feedback, though less frequent, offered actionable suggestions for improvement. The triangulated analysis confirmed that integrating qualitative narratives with quantitative scores enhances the understanding of stakeholders' satisfaction and engagement. The findings support data-informed planning, continuous quality improvement, and the strategic use of stakeholders' feedback in institutional decision-making. This research underscores the importance of investing in AI and data-driven evaluation frameworks to create more inclusive, responsive, and engaging academic environments.

title: Design and Evaluation of Uniform and Nonuniform Planar Arrays Geometries Using Rectangular Patch Antennas
authors: Mullah Momin, Md Rafiqul Islam, Norun Abdul Malek, Othman Omran Khalifa, Khaizuran Abdullah, M. M. Hasan Mahfuz
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This paper presents the design, simulation, and performance analysis of patch antenna arrays using uniform and nonuniform planar geometries for 5 GHz applications. Array configurations of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 2,2 \times 2$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times 4$</tex> are designed and evaluated their performances through simulation. The study investigates the impact of uniform spacing versus customized spacing and feedline design in nonuniform arrays. CST Studio Suite is used to analyze key performance parameters such as return loss (S11), bandwidth, VSWR, radiation and total efficiency, gain, directivity, and side lobe level (SLL). Results show that nonuniform designs offer superior S11 and bandwidth in several configurations and highlight trade-offs in efficiency and SLL. The work contributes in the modular feedline optimization applied across different array geometries <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(1 \times 2,2 \times 2$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times 4$</tex>), enabling better impedance matching and bandwidth enhancement for compact <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 ~ G H z}$</tex> systems.

title: The Intersection of Fintech and Homebased Women Entrepreneurship: Opportunities and Challenges
authors: Vishwaja Rangannagari, Saraswati Raju Iyer
abstract: Women entrepreneurs with home-based businesses contribute to the growth of the economy and support innovations especially embracing the digital interfaces. The rapid expansion of virtual financial technology (fintech) in the entrepreneurial environment has brought about new opportunities for access to funds of home-based women entrepreneurs. This paper therefore examines the effects of fintech to women entrepreneurs in Andhra Pradesh, India with emphasis on the benefits and challenges brought about by the technology. In this study, both primary and secondary sources have been used. The study establishes that through the usage of fintech technologies such as mobile banking, digital wallets, and online payment solutions, women entrepreneurs have been able to bypass ancient impediments towards finance and market. A little over fifty percent have said that fintech has significantly improved their access to financial services. They also have said that they had both increase in sales through digital payment options and access to new markets/customers through the platforms. Risk management was another challenge identified with special emphasis on security risks such as fraudulence and data loss which are parts of online risks. The key issues which the respondents mentioned include transaction safety concerns and others stated that they find it challenging to understand the operations of the fintech platforms. Further, the research also concluded correlation between the level of women entrepreneurs' fintech usage and their digital literacy. That is why it is suggested that awareness campaigns should be organized and training programs are to be provided for women to overcome these challenges.

title: Women Versus Men: Comparative Analysis of Physical Activity and Mobile Phone Usage on Cognitive Performance
authors: Chaitanya Nutakki, Giovanni Naldi, Egidio D'Angelo, Shyam Diwakar
abstract: In this digital world, the critical interconnections of lifestyle risk factors for brain and mental health, and the relationship between smartphone usage, physical activity, and cognitive performance among men and women need to be explored. The aim of this study was to understand how the usage time of smartphones and involvement in physical activity can impact cognitive function among biological female and male individuals. To analyze the cognitive performance changes, we used a self-reported survey involving 221 participants aged between 18-90 from Kerala and Tamil Nadu using the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) and the activities of daily life questionnaire. The mean cognitive task scores of naming, language, delayed recall memory, attention, abstraction, orientation, and spatial memory were analyzed and significant changes between men and women were analyzed using statistical analysis. Overall cognitive score analysis suggested that women performed better than men in naming, language, recall memory, abstraction, and orientation, whereas men outperformed women in tasks related to attention and visuospatial skills. Involvement in physical activity showed augmented cognitive scores across men and women. Mobile phone usage between 30 and 60 minutes daily, can positively impact memory and learning, contributing to improved cognitive health. Our findings will help develop comprehensive strategies involving technology to assess cognitive health and potentially delay or prevent dementia.

title: List of Reviewers
authors: N/A

title: Integrated Stereo Vision and Compliance Control With an Underwater Manipulator for Hydraulic Structure Inspection and Maintenance
authors: Fei Wang, Haixin Liu, Lin Cao, Guangming Song, Aiguo Song, Roberto Meattini, Claudio Melchiorri
abstract: Hydraulic structure inspection and maintenance are essential for structural health and operational safety. To address the limitations of underwater intervention, this article introduces an integrated underwater manipulator system that supports both teleoperation and automatic operation modes. The automatic mode incorporates visual position estimation for locating structures, robust motion capability for accurate trajectory tracking, and compliance and force tracking for safe interaction. An uncertainty-based underwater disparity estimation network (UWNet) is developed to enhance position estimation accuracy. This network combines global and local features effectively and integrates uncertainty estimation and pseudolabel generation to adapt the pretrained UWNet, improving underwater disparity predictions. In addition, a robust controller is designed, comprising an inner-loop position controller based on modified unknown system dynamics estimator and supertwisting sliding mode control to mitigate hydrodynamic disturbances and model uncertainties. An outer-loop variable admittance controller with adaptive feedback compensation ensures compliant interaction and force tracking. Experimental results show that, with reliable disparity estimation from the adapted UWNet, the system achieves a trajectory tracking RMSE of 1.44 mm at 1.0 m/s flow and a mean force error of 0.63 N during thickness measurement, while enabling stable surface cleaning under varying flow conditions, thereby facilitating hydraulic structure inspection and maintenance.

title: LLM-based User-Customizable Economic MPC for Consumer Unmanned Surface Vehicle Path Planning
authors: Zhimin Liu, Yubin Jia, Panxiao Yong, Changyin Sun
abstract: Existing unmanned surface vehicle (USV) path planning methods based on model predictive control (MPC) frequently necessitate extensive manual parameter adjustments in response to changes in the control task, which limits their usability in consumer-grade unmanned electronic systems operated by non-expert users. When addressing the conflicting requirements of safety and economic efficiency in path planning, MPC typically prioritizes trajectory safety. Additionally, methods for adjusting controller performance based on personalized user requirements typically depend heavily on extensive user feedback or pretraining data. In this paper, an economic model predictive control (EMPC) approach assisted by a large language model (LLM) is proposed. Using an LLM agent, users can conveniently and efficiently update controller parameters via natural language to meet their personalized requirements. The LLM agent translates user instructions into mathematical expressions in real time, thereby enabling parameter adjustments for the EMPC controller and achieving performance consistent with user demands. Meanwhile, the EMPC approach balances obstacle avoidance safety with the USV’s operational energy consumption, effectively managing the trade-off between these two objectives. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed method, indicating its potential for real-time deployment in consumer unmanned surface vehicle electronic systems that require flexible user interaction and energy-efficient autonomous navigation.

title: Sensorless Sequential Model Predictive Direct Speed Control for Induction Motor Drives
authors: Emrah Zerdali, Sid Ahmed El Mehdi Ardjoun, Marco Rivera, Jacopo Riccio, Jakson Bonaldo, Patrick Wheeler
abstract: Advanced model predictive control schemes for induction motors (IMs), especially when integrated with sensorless control strategies, continue to attract considerable research interest in safety-critical and harsh-environment applications. To address both aspects, this article proposes a sensorless sequential model predictive direct speed control (MPDSC) method for IM drives. The MPDSC scheme enhances the dynamic response of the drive by eliminating the cascaded control structure and addressing the limitations of conventional linear controllers. To overcome the challenges associated with weighting factor selection in conventional MPDSC–and the resulting performance degradation due to fixed values–sequential control is incorporated into the MPDSC framework. Additionally, a sensorless control strategy is integrated to reduce the cost, physical size, and hardware complexity of the drive system. To this end, an extended Kalman filter is employed to simultaneously estimate the flux, speed, and load torque required for sensorless MPDSC operation. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through comparative simulations and experiments conducted under various operating conditions. The results show that the proposed approach outperforms the conventional one and maintains robust control performance across a wide range of scenarios.

title: Generic and Flexible Digital Model of DAB Converters With Various Topologies and Control for Efficient Optimal Design
authors: Chaochao Song, Nitheesh Rajendran, Ning Wang, Frede Blaabjerg, Pooya Davari
abstract: To evaluate the performance of dual-active-bridge (DAB) converters across various topologies and control schemes for optimal design, this article proposes a generic explicit digital model applicable two-level (2L)/three-level (3L) half-bridge (HB), full-bridge (FB), and three-phase (3P) topologies with a unified control structure. Voltage and current expressions for a single bridge leg considering both 2L and 3L topologies are developed in a generalized manner, and thus the model of DAB topologies with different number of legs can be constructed by superimposing the corresponding number of individual bridge leg models. Subsequently, the digital model, i.e., discrete-time model of DAB converters with different topologies and control configurations, is derived based on the circuit differential equations and a unified closed-loop control system by applying backward Euler method. Therefore, both steady-state and dynamic performance of DAB converters can be predicted in the proposed model. Owing to the analytical formulation of operating parameters and generic expressions for different topologies and control, the calculation speed of the proposed digital model can be accelerated compared to commercial simulation platforms such as MATLAB/Simulink and PLECS, and the design efficiency can be significantly enhanced, especially when it eliminates the need for frequent manual adjustments of topologies, modulation, and loss-thermal models of power devices. Simulation and experimental tests validate that the digital DAB model can achieve high accuracy under various topologies with fast execution speed.

title: Fabrication of VS/MXene heterostructure electrode-based micro-supercapacitor for on-chip energy source applications_supp1-3684739.docx
authors: Sushil Kumar Pandey
abstract: Vanadium disulfide, a transition metal dichalcogenide, has emerged as a highly promising electrode material for next-generation micro-supercapacitors. In this study, the growth optimization of vanadium disulfide single crystals and films was performed using a chemical vapor deposition system by tuning different parameters such as growth temperature, precursor amount, and duration. Vanadium disulfide single crystals edge length of up to ⁓330 μm were achieved at a growth temperature of 825°C, the largest crystals reported to date. Under similar conditions, textured thin films were also achieved by adjusting the argon carrier gas flow and precursor concentration. Atomic force microscopy confirmed a thickness of about 18 nm, indicating the formation of multilayer crystals. Density functional theory calculations revealed the metallic nature of the vanadium disulfide electrode adsorbed with sulfate ions, and a maximum quantum capacitance of 28 μF/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> at -0.5 V, indicating its suitability as an electrode for a supercapacitor. After material investigation, a vanadium disulfide electrode-based supercapacitor was fabricated using a metal mask-assisted technique. The prototype supercapacitor fabricated device demonstrates an areal capacitance of 41.16 mF/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, which further improved nearly sixfold to 250.3 mF/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> after incorporating an MXene layer above the vanadium disulfide electrode. The fabricated device has excellent capacitance retention of 88.9% during 1000 cycles of Galvanostatic Charge-Discharge at a current density of 0.5 mA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Our findings establish a link between synthesis conditions, material properties, and electrochemical performance, highlighting the potential for the commercialization of vanadium disulfide electrode-based micro-supercapacitors as on-chip energy sources.

title: Vehicle Dynamics Learning from Physics Priors with Model-Structured Neural Networks
authors: Aniello Mungiello, Felix Jahncke, Stefania Santini, Johannes Betz, Gastone Pietro Rosati Papini, Mattia Piccinini
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Modeling the vehicle dynamics near the handling limits is crucial for autonomous driving and racing. However, building accurate models remains challenging due to strong nonlinearities, limited data availability, and changing environmental conditions. Physics-based models offer interpretability but require costly parameter identification, while general-purpose neural networks typically need large datasets to generalize. This paper introduces a new model-structured neural network (MS-NN-full) that learns the coupled lateral-longitudinal vehicle dynamics by embedding physical knowledge into its internal architecture. MS-NN-full combines physics-inspired neuro-fuzzy models with data-driven components to capture the quasi-steady-state and transient behavior, as well as the mutual coupling between the lateral and longitudinal dynamics. Experimental results on a 1:10-scale autonomous vehicle show that MS-NN-full outperforms existing MS-NN baselines and general-purpose neural networks in accuracy and generalization, using less than two minutes of training data. The model also demonstrates rapid adaptation to new tire configurations and increased vehicle mass with minimal fine-tuning. We release our implementation and datasets to support further research in physics-guided learning for autonomous systems.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A

title: TIG-IM: a Two-Stage Inductive GNN with Multi-Task Learning for Influential Node Identification in Complex Networks_supp1-3681944.pdf
authors: Yonggang Li
abstract: Identifying influential nodes in complex networks poses a significant challenge, requiring a delicate balance among efficiency, accuracy, and redundancy elimination. To address this, we propose TIG-IM (A Two-stage Inductive GNN for Influence Maximization), a novel framework based on GNN. In Stage 1, we construct a multi-task learning-based global scorer. This scorer utilizes a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to simultaneously regress the SIR propagation score and our newly proposed 3D-Bridging Centrality. This multi-task design enables efficient identification of cross-community bridge nodes to generate a high-quality candidate pool. In Stage 2, we design a local selector that introduces a composite loss function with sparsity and diversity regularization. This selector performs a secondary re ranking on the candidate subgraph to produce a final seed set that is both structurally dispersed and possesses complementary propagation potential. Theoretical analysis proves that our scorer satisfies monotonicity and that the local loss can be interpreted as a MAP estimation. Extensive experiments on twelve real-world networks from diverse domains, including social, academic, and biological, demonstrate that TIG-IM significantly outperforms various state-of-the-art baselines in both spreading effectiveness and computational efficiency. Ablation studies further validate the synergistic value of our three core components: 3D-Bridging Centrality, sparsity penalty, and diversity-based re-ranking.

title: Asymptotic Information-Theoretic Optimality of Channel Model Substitution by Monte Carlo Sampling
authors: Xiuqin Wang, Shuping Dang, Haiqiang Chen, Zhongli Wang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Channel model substitution (CMS) has been widely applied as a powerful toolkit to enable closed-form communication performance analysis. Minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KLD) between an original channel model (OCM) and its distributional substitute (DS) can achieve information-theoretic optimality. However, for most CMS applications, the expression of KLD cannot be derived in closed form, leading to great challenges in minimizing such implicit objective functions. To tackle this analytical difficulty, we propose to use Monte Carlo sampling (MCS) to eliminate the integration of the objective function in these intractable KLD minimization problems. Through MCS, one can take the numerical estimate of KLD in a summative form as the objective function and proceed with the optimization to approach asymptotic optimality. By employing the generalized Newton-Raphson (N-R) method, the information-theoretic optima for a majority of CMS applications can be numerically obtained with guaranteed existence and uniqueness.

title: G2-SDF: Geometry-Guided Neural Signed Distance Field for Space Target Reconstruction
authors: Pengling Tang, Zifei Li, Yifan Chen, Dou Sun, Feng Wang, Ya-Qiu Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction of a space target using inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR) images often requires feature extraction and is susceptible to shadowing effect. The accuracy of feature extraction directly affects the reconstruction quality, whereas shadowing effect can result in incomplete reconstruction. To avoid feature extraction and address the problem caused by shadowing effect, we propose a 3D reconstruction method for space targets using forward differentiable rendering and backward optimization. The key point of the proposed method involves simulating the shadowing effect in rendered images and treating the observed ISAR image as the ground truth. To achieve it, a signed distance field (SDF) is used to represent the target model and shading images with shadowing effect are generated according to the physical principles of ISAR imaging. The rendering process is implemented using a neural network with the shading image as input. The results demonstrate effective shadowing effect transfer between the neural network input and output, ensuring that the rendered image retains realistic shadowing. During optimization, the loss function is constructed by comparing the rendered image with the observed ISAR image. The simulated shadowing effect relaxes the constraints on the invisible part of the target in ISAR images, enabling complete 3D model reconstruction. Experiments based on simulated images demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Code is provided at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Tangpl0066/G2-SDF</uri>.

title: Multi-View Images Suffice 3D Reasoning Through Chain-of-Thought Selection and Question-Guided Fusion
authors: Boqiang Xu, Jinlin Wu, Wei Zhang, Chenyang Su, Jian Liang, Zhenan Sun, Zhen Lei
abstract: 3D reasoning is crucial in areas like robotics and autonomous driving. Due to the high cost of 3D data acquisition, some recent methods attempt to enable LLMs to perform 3D reasoning through multi-view images, thereby transferring the powerful 2D reasoning capabilities of LLMs to 3D environments. However, these methods face challenges: either they use redundant views that contain many perspectives irrelevant to the question, or they rely on globally aggregated multi-view representations, losing the fine-grained vision-language correlations. To tackle these challenges, we propose 3DMulti-LLM, which mainly consists of three components: a COT selector, a question-guided fusion block, and pre-trained LLMs. Specifically, first, the COT selector leverages the powerful chain-of-thought reasoning capabilities of LLMs to identify question-related multi-view images. In this way, 3DMulti-LLM can eliminate a substantial amount of interference from unnecessary viewpoints. Then, we propose a question-guided fusion block for integrating multi-view features via question-guided interaction among various viewpoints. Finally, the pre-trained LLMs are utilized to reason in 3D scenes directly through multi-view features. Notably, our approach understands the 3D scene solely through multi-view images, without requiring the input of point cloud information or additional 3D feature extraction. Through our experiments, 3DMulti-LLM achieves impressive performance and surpasses existing 3D-input-free methods by +12.2% and +7.1% on ScanQA and 3DMV-VQA datasets, respectively.

title: DFLBN: A Dynamic Fuzzy Learnable Brain Network for Brain Age Estimation
authors: Weiping Ding, Jing Wang, Tianyi Zhou, Shu Jiang, Peng Ping, Jiaru Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Brain age estimation plays a crucial role in neuroscience and clinical research, significantly contributing to the diagnosis of neurodegenerative diseases and the assessment of brain health. Traditional approaches often struggle to effectively capture both the global dependencies and local feature patterns present in the complex and heterogeneous functional connectivity data across different brain regions. To overcome these limitations, we propose an innovative two-branch dynamic fuzzy learnable brain network (DFLBN). Prior to inputting into the dual branches, we apply feature preprocessing techniques, including positional embedding and reshaping. The global branch leverages a Transformer-based architecture to model global dependencies and incorporates a dynamic fuzzy fusion mechanism with Gaussian, triangular, and trapezoidal membership functions. This mechanism enhances the representation of boundary information, salient modes, and central regions within functional connectivity maps. The local branch, on the other hand, combines three convolutional filters with a learnable graph attention mechanism to extract fine-grained feature patterns and model the importance of inter-node relationships. Finally, a multilayer perceptron is employed for brain age regression. The experimental results show that the proposed method greatly outperforms the state-of-the-art methods and significantly improves the accuracy of brain age estimation on the SRPBS multi-disorder MRI dataset.

title: Wafer-Level Micromachined All-Glass Atomic Vapor Cells
authors: Zhenyu Hu, Qiaohui Yang, Taoyuan Zhu, Duo Pan, Zhiwei Li, Jingbiao Chen, Xianlong Wei
abstract: The atomic vapor cell is the core component of quantum sensing systems, enabling precise measurements through stable atomic reference transitions. While the optical path length of conventional wafer-level fabricated atomic vapor cells is typically less than 2 mm, this research presents wafer-level fabricated, all-glass micromachined atomic vapor cells with a significantly extended optical path length of 9 mm by ultrafast laser welding. An array of 24 such vapor cells with dimensions of 6 × 6 × 11.2 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> was successfully fabricated on a 2-inch wafer. The atomic vapor cells were characterized using a compact optical frequency standard system based on modulation transfer spectroscopy (MTS), achieving a frequency stability of 5 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-13</sup> at 1 s. These results highlight the advantages of ultrafast laser welding in overcoming the optical path length limitations of traditional wafer-level fabricated vapor cells, providing a scalable solution for compact optical frequency standards and miniaturized quantum sensors.

title: LLM-Enhanced Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning with Expert Workflow for Real-Time P2P Energy Trading
authors: Chengwei Lou, Zekai Jin, Wei Tang, Guangfei Geng, Jin Yang, Lu Zhang
abstract: Real-time peer-to-peer (P2P) electricity markets dynamically adapt to fluctuations in renewable energy and variations in demand, maximizing economic benefits through instantaneous price responses while enhancing grid flexibility. However, scaling expert guidance for massive personalized prosumers poses critical challenges, including diverse decision-making demands and a lack of customized modeling frameworks. This paper proposes an integrated large language model-multi-agent reinforcement learning (LLM-MARL) framework for real-time P2P energy trading to address challenges such as the limited technical capability of prosumers, the lack of expert experience, and security issues of distribution networks. LLMs are introduced as experts to generate personalized strategies, guiding MARL under the centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm through imitation. To handle the scalability issues inherent in large-scale P2P networks, a differential attention-based critic network is introduced to efficiently extract key interaction features and enhance convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that LLM-generated strategies effectively substitute human experts. The proposed imitative expert MARL algorithms achieve significantly lower economic costs and voltage violation rates on test sets compared to baseline algorithms, while maintaining robust stability. This paper provides an effective solution for the real-time decision-making of the P2P electricity market by bridging expert knowledge with agent learning.

title: Constraint-Adaptive Reinforcement Learning for Large-Scale Power System Planning
authors: Lin Chen, Yiran Liu, Zehan Qi, Zhaoyuan Wu, Yang Yu
abstract: High-penetration renewable integration and the spatial mismatch between energy supply and demand increase the complexity of multi-regional power system planning. Existing AI techniques in planning include renewable forecasting and demand prediction. These methods enhance the input fidelity of Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) models. However, they mainly focus on explicit and observable parameters. Multi regional planning also depends on latent and unobservable inter-regional responses. Such latent features are difficult to capture in the MILP pipeline. To address these limitations, we propose the Constraint-Adaptive Graph-Convolution (CAG-C) Reinforcement Learning (RL) model, which performs end-to end policy optimization. The planning problem is formulated as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process, where the agent iteratively adjusts regional power exchange strategies. A graph convolutional network encodes spatial and resource correlations, while a constraint-adaptive architecture transforms raw actions into feasible plans via region-wise scaling, power balance correction, and cost-optimal transmission allocation. This modular design decomposes the high-dimensional planning task into tractable subproblems, ensuring operational feasibility while enabling efficient learning. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed framework is both highly extensible and interpretable. As training progresses, the evolving solution trajectory reveals valuable insights into each region's functional role, offering a promising new paradigm for future power system planning.

title: HybridSphere: Enhancing Hybrid Meetings with Avatar-Based VR Environments_supp1-3679908.mp4
authors: Koji Momota
abstract: With recent advances in information and communication technologies, Hybrid meetings, where local attendees are physically present and remote participants join virtually, have become increasingly common. However, remote participants often experience reduced contextual awareness and a sense of isolation. To address these issues, we propose HybridSphere, a hybrid meeting system that reconstructs a shared virtual reality environment for remote participants. The system employs a 360-degree camera and pose estimation to generate real-time avatar representations of local attendees to allow remote users with head-mounted displays to experience the meeting as if all participants are in the same virtual space. We conducted a user study comparing HybridSphere to a baseline condition in which remote participants viewed an unmodified 360-degree video. Although the avatars were rated lower in perceived trustworthiness and likability due to limited visual fidelity, participants appreciated the seated-6DoF function. These results suggest that immersive and spatially flexible VR representations can enhance remote engagement in hybrid meetings.

title: Zero-Shot Open Circuit Fault Diagnosis of Converter Based on Prior-Knowledge Injected Dual Bayesian Network With Attention Mechanism
authors: Gen Qiu, Shang Yang, Jiusi Zhang, Fan Wu, Hao Luo, Jose I. Leon, Leopoldo G. Franquelo
abstract: Power electronic systems serve as core infrastructure for modern energy conversion and power control. Their reliability is crucial for the stable operation of key fields, such as industrial power systems, transportation, and aerospace. Open-circuit faults are difficult to detect, which can lead to serious consequences. While effective in data-rich environments, existing data-driven approaches’ reliance on extensive labeled data hinders their practical deployment. The reason is that acquiring comprehensive fault data in real-world settings is often prohibitively expensive. To address this, this article proposes a dual Bayesian zero-shot fault diagnosis approach based on prior-knowledge injected with attention mechanism (PKIDBAAM). This approach constructs a fault attribute space with clear physical meaning by analyzing the physical relationship between the Vienna rectifier topology and fault modes. It employs a multihead attention mechanism to dynamically fuse multidomain fault features from three-phase current signals. A dual Bayesian inference framework combining naive Bayes and integrated multiple Gaussian Bayesian algorithms is designed to achieve high accuracy recognition of inaccessible open-circuit faults. The verification results on a Vienna rectifier experimental platform show that it significantly outperforms conventional classical machine learning models, which provides a new solution for intelligent fault diagnosis in power electronic systems.

title: Influence of Dust Storm Cell Size on the Microwave Attenuation Prediction Method
authors: E.I. Eltahir, Md Rafiqul Islam, Elfatih A. A. Elsheikh, Mohamed Hadi Habaebi, Aisha Hassan A. Hashim, Othman Omran Khalifa, Mosab Hamdan
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This paper investigates how variations in dust cell size affect dust storm attenuation predictions based on two severe dust storm events measured on terrestrial microwave links in Khartoum, Sudan. All prediction models were used to estimate the specific attenuation in dB/km, and to obtain total attenuation, the specific attenuation is multiplied by the path length. This paper proposes a mathematically derived reduction factor, formulated from a two-dimensional exponential visibility model, to calculate the effective path length and correct for overestimated attenuation-especially for non-uniform and spatially limited dust cores. The June 6th and July 3rd, 2014 storms, differing in intensity and distribution pattern, were analyzed using the Goldhirsh exponential visibility decay model to extract minimum visibility (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{0}$</tex>) and characteristic radial distance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\mathrm{L}_{0}\right)$</tex>. Key storm parameters are: June <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6\left(\mathrm{V}_{0}=0.01362\right. \left.\text{km}, \mathrm{L}_{0}=6.4 \text{km}\right)$</tex> and July <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3\left(\mathrm{V}_{0}=0.02016 \text{km}, \mathrm{L}_{0}=7.3 \text{km}\right)$</tex>, which highlight different attenuation reduction factor behaviors. The reduction factor decreased with path length, reflecting the finite spatial extent of the dust cores, with the compact storm yielding lower values over longer paths compared to the broader storm. Two microwave links-operating at 21.2 GHz over 6.2 km and 14.5 GHz over 7.6 km -were analyzed. Results showed full core (100%) exposure for both links during the June 6 storm, while during the July 3 event, only the 6.2 km link experienced full exposure, with the 7.6 km link exposed to 88.4% of the dust core. The study highlights the critical roles of storm cell size and link length in determining the attenuation reduction factor, ultimately contributing to more accurate prediction models for designing reliable wireless communication systems in arid regions.

title: Photonic Crystal Based Biosensor for Early Detection of Breast Cancer Using Genetic Approach: A Review
authors: Sukeshini P. Balwir, Nikhil Deep Gupta
abstract: Breast cancer as being one of the most devastating & fatal form of cancer occurring in women, its early detection is currently the only keytowards theeffective treatment of this life threatening disease. Recent screening tests such as mammography, MRI biopsy, ultrasonography are expensive, less sensitive, time taking, uncomfortable & also require skilled professionals. This leads to the need of extremely sensitive andpoint of care technology. It has been found that the genetic biomarkers in the form of different MicroRNA concentration, which are upregulated or downregulated, are observed in the body fluids for certain cancerous condition and their accurate detection can help to timely diagnose the disease. The detection of these biomarkers using photonics techniques such as the one using photonic crystals can provide several advantages such as high sensitivity and specificity, non-/minimal invasion process, compact device design, etc. The present paper throws a light on and discusses the current breast cancer diagnostic techniques, their limitations, different possible genetic biomarkers for breast cancer detection, how nanophotonics can be used for the detection of these biomarkers & the recent technological advancement & challenges in the implementation of such different kind of biosensors.

title: Bridging the Gap: Exploring Gender Differences in eSanjeevani Adoption in Kerala
authors: Nayana Navitha, Parvathy Valsala, Vyshnavi Nair
abstract: As digital health and telemedicine become increasingly integral to healthcare delivery, addressing gender differences in access and utilization is crucial, particularly for women's health. This study examined gender differences and utilization of the eSanjeevani telemedicine service in Kerala, India. The study utilized a quantitative methodology using a self-prepared questionnaire. A quantitative survey of 338 respondents explored awareness, usage patterns, satisfaction levels, and the influence of gender and residence on various service-related variables. While overall awareness was high <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(58.9 \%)$</tex>, females exhibited lower usage frequency despite comparable awareness levels. Social media was the primary information source, but a significant portion could not recall how they learned about the service. Satisfaction was high among users, but a majority had never used eSanjeevani. Residence significantly impacted service perception and usage, with remote areas demonstrating lower utilization. Interaction analysis revealed that females in rural areas were the least frequent users. The study underscores the need for targeted awareness campaigns, improved accessibility in remote areas, and gender-sensitive eHealth service design to promote equitable utilization of telemedicine in Kerala.

title: Inventory Control Using a Drifted Poisson Process: Applicability to Actual Intermittent Demand Data
authors: Ryoya Koide, Yurika Ono, Aya Ishigaki
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: In recent years, numerous approaches for forecasting and inventory control in intermittent demand patterns have been proposed. However, most of these studies focus exclusively on point forecasts, with few incorporating stochastic forecasting methods while also considering inventory control. Levy processes are stochastic processes that can be formulated to incorporate discontinuous changes and have been previously employed in demand forecasting. However, most existing research has focused on prediction methods that consider only discontinuous changes, such as compound Poisson processes. No studies have formulated inventory control models that simultaneously account for both continuous and discontinuous components while also examining their applicability to real-world data. To address this gap, this study performs parameter estimation of jump processes on actual data and evaluates the model's utility by comparing the expected value of the formulated total cost with the total cost of inventory controlled using an ordering point method. The results demonstrate that the error becomes extremely small on short timescales, confirming that the expected total cost can be effectively utilized for decision-making in short-term inventory management.

title: FHE-MENNs: Opportunities and Pitfalls for Accelerating Fully Homomorphic Private Inference with Multi-Exit Neural Networks
authors: Lars Folkerts, Nektarios Georgios Tsoutsos
publish: ACM Journal on Emerging Technologies in Computing Systems
abstract: None

title: Adaptive Quantum-Based Least Mean Fourth Control for Multisource Isolated Three-Phase Microgrids: Experimental Analysis and Performance Evaluation
authors: Sombir Kundu, Ashutosh K. Giri, Dipankar Deb, Rajeev Kumar Singh
abstract: This article presents an adaptive quantum normalized least-mean fourth (AQ-LMF) filtering control strategy to improve power quality (PQ) in standalone microgrids (MGs). Integrated with a voltage source converter (VSC), the controller provides fast dynamic response, enhanced stability, DC-offset rejection, reduced steady-state error, and minimized oscillations, while ensuring frequency regulation, load balancing, and harmonic mitigation under highly distorted loads. The algorithm employs error-correlation energy and signal normalization to accelerate convergence, reduce computational burden, and maintain stability at higher step sizes. A solar photovoltaic (PV) system is interfaced to the DC link through a boost converter using an incremental conductance (INC) maximum power point tracking method, while an energy storage system (ESS) connected via a bidirectional DC–DC converter balances power among solar, wind, and load demand during generation variability and load fluctuations. The proposed estimator reaches its steady-state tolerance band within two update cycles, with subsequent iterations confined within noise and quantization limits.
