title: GRotate: Drum-Type Hydroponic System for Plant Growth using NDVI Monitoring
authors: Jasmin Kae Blancia, Marion Abbygail de Leon, Andrew Go-Alcantara, Dominic Rodrigo Pagulayan, Jan Kevin A. Galicia, Maria Leonora C. Guico, Jose Claro N. Monje
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Rotary hydroponic systems offer a compact, efficient solution for maximizing plant exposure to light and nutrients while minimizing space and resource use. This presents a design and evaluation of a rotary hydroponic system designed specifically for lettuce growth and equipped with environmental sensors with NDVI-based plant health monitoring. A NoIR camera and OpenCV-based image processing workflow were implemented to calculate the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), enabling noninvasive detection of plant stress. During a five-day trial, NDVI values declined from 0.52 to 0.32 in response to induced nutrient stress, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.94 between NDVI scores and visual health assessments. Plant growth parameters-including pH and Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)were monitored using an NPKPHCTH sensor connected to an ESP32 via Modbus RTU over RS485. The sensor system achieved 94.7 % accuracy for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex> and 97.86 % for TDS when validated against laboratory instruments. All data were transmitted wirelessly to the ThingSpeak cloud platform, where real-time and historical trends were visualized with an average upload latency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 9 2. 2 5}$</tex> milliseconds. The system demonstrated stable operation within a rotating environment and supports data-driven management of plant health and environmental conditions in hydroponic agriculture.

title: Validating Statistical Delay Test Generation Under Timing Variations via SAT-Based ATPG
authors: Hanieh Jafarzadeh, Jan Dennis Reimer, Hussam Amrouch, Sybille Hellebrand, Hans-Joachim Wunderlich
abstract: Process, voltage and temperature fluctuations induce timing variations which require statistical test generation methods for detecting Small Delay Faults (SDFs). Such a test set is called robust, if for each fault f of a randomly created circuit instance, it contains a pattern detecting <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$f$</tex> with its minimum theoretically detectable size. The paper at hand combines statistical delay test generation and deterministic satisfability-based test generation to validate the robustness of the test sets. The computing time for the SAT-based algorithm is drastically reduced as only the small set of faults is targeted not detected by the statistical approach. It is seen that the statistically generated test set has already an extremely high quality and only very few patterns need to be added by the SAT-based algorithm. Test set compaction starting with these deterministic patterns, provides an additional reduction of test data volumes while keeping the improved fault efficiency.

title: Generating Adversarial Examples of Time Series Data for Time Series Classification Using Differential Evolution
authors: Kensei Yamada, Keiichi Tamura
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: An adversarial example is a type of adversarial attack for deep-learning models involving the addition of tiny noises (perturbations) to the data to deceive the models. The key to attacks using adversarial examples is to increase their stealth. In our previous work, we attempted to validate adversarial examples for time series classification (TSC) problems by introducing partial perturbations to enhance stealth. We proposed partial fast gradient sign method and partial projected gradient descent, which add partial perturbations to time series data, and defense methods against them. However, they are for white-box attacks, where the model structure and parameters are known, and partial perturbations in black-box attacks have not been sufficiently studied. In this study, we propose novel blackbox attacks using differential evolution, one-element attack and few-elements attack, which are new threats that can generate robust adversarial examples by only adding perturbations to one element and few elements, respectively, in time series data. These methods apply the one-pixel attack for image data to time series data. The proposed methods impose constraints on values to limit their changes to small values, considering the characteristics of time series data. Experimental results showed that the proposed methods could generate adversarial examples with higher stealthiness than conventional methods.

title: Case Study: Neutron-Induced SEE Evaluation in a Mixed-Signal Programmable SoC
authors: Eduardo Lazzarotto Fabbris, Rafael Galhardo Vaz, Odair Lélis Gonçalez, Tiago R. Balen
abstract: This work presents results of a physical radiation test performed upon a mixed-signal system implemented in a commercial Programmable System-on-Chip (PSoC 6), totaling 209 hours of 14 MeV neutrons irradiation. Critical errors were observed in the Direct Memory Access (DMA) controller and timer peripherals, leading to functional deviations such as frequency shifting and execution hangs.

title: PowerCourt: Formally Judging Benign-Looking Circuits in Multi-Tenant FPGA Environments
authors: Philipp Schmitz, Mohamed Shelkamy Ali, Dino Mehmedagić, Hassan Nassar, Jörg Henkel, Dominik Stoffel, Wolfgang Kunz
abstract: In the era of Cloud Computing and High Performance Computing as a Service, multiple customers run their software and hardware on the same physical devices. This opens new opportunities for attackers, like Denial of Service (DoS) attacks on shared cloud Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FP-GAs). DoS attacks work by employing a malicious hardware design as a tenant on a shared FP-GA device, draining the FPGA's power budget. This paper aims at identifying potentially malicious circuits before they are deployed to the FPGA, using formal verification. Our methodology, PowerCourt, provides an accurate worst-case power estimation based on switching activity. It computes metadata about the design that enables service providers to classify designs as malicious. The method checks formal properties that verify whether a design can exceed a custom threshold and whether this threshold can be exceeded repeatedly. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach using various hardware designs. For example, for a trojan that enables excessive switching, we show that we can detect it offline and disable it during runtime.

title: Exploring AI-Driven Personalized Fashion: A Survey on Styling Preferences, Challenges, and Privacy Concerns
authors: Raja lekshmi A, Kamal Bijilani, Kaushik Jonnada
abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into fashion has unlocked new possibilities for personalized styling, offering tailored recommendations that consider individual body types, skin tones, and preferences. This study investigates the challenges faced by women in personal styling, including outfit selection, layering, modesty, and color matching, while addressing critical issues like societal pressures, algorithmic bias, and privacy concerns. Through a survey of 58 women aged 21-30, the findings reveal significant interest in AI-powered styling tools, with 72.4The proposed solution, StyleMe Mirror, aims to provide personalized recommendations by leveraging inclusive datasets, ethical AI practices, and privacy-first approaches. By integrating features like outfit visualization, skin tone analysis, and event specific suggestions, the tool empowers users to make confident styling decisions. Furthermore, its design promotes inclusivity, counteracts gender biases, and supports sustainable fashion practices by encouraging mindful consumption. The study underscores the potential of AI to revolutionize fashion by prioritizing inclusivity, cultural sensitivity, and user empowerment, setting a benchmark for equitable, user-centered fashion technologies. An initial design and prototype shows positive results.

authors: Nisha Devasia, Jamie Espinosa-Briones, Julie A. Kientz

authors: Pengcheng Zhang, Xiao Wang, Yanxiang Tong, Hai Dong, Yan Xiao, Shunhui Ji

title: Automated Construction of a Knowledge Graph of Nuclear Fusion Energy for Effective Elicitation and Retrieval of Information
authors: Andrea Loreti, Kesi Chen, Ruby George, Robert Firth, Adriano Agnello, Shinnosuke Tanaka
abstract: In this document, we discuss a multistep approach to automated construction of a knowledge graph (KG), for structuring and representing domain-specific knowledge from large document corpora. We apply our method to build the first KG of nuclear fusion energy, a highly specialized field characterized by vast scope and heterogeneity. This is an ideal benchmark to test the key features of our pipeline, including automatic named entity recognition (NER) and entity resolution. We show how pretrained large language models (LLMs) can be used to address these challenges and we evaluate their performance against Zipf’s law, which characterizes human natural language. In addition, we develop a knowledge-graph retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system that uses multiple prompts with LLMs to provide contextually relevant answers to natural-language queries, including complex multihop questions requiring reasoning across interconnected entities.

title: Lightweight Privacy-Preserving Mobile Sensing Framework via Additive Secret Sharing for Integrated Sensing Communication Computing Systems
authors: Haewon Byeon, Mohammed E. Seno, Mukesh Soni, Pritee Parwekar, Pushpendra Singh, Ankit Vidyarthi
abstract: The growing integration of sensing, communication, computation, and control in consumer devices raises new concerns regarding system efficiency and data privacy. To address communication overhead and privacy risks in mobile sensing, we propose LPMS, a lightweight privacy-preserving framework based on additive secret sharing. LPMS enables the secure exchange of data between sensing devices and edge-computing components while protecting sensitive information in Integrated Sensing Communication Computing Systems (ISC<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>). The framework introduces efficient secure neural network protocols that avoid heavy cryptographic primitives, thereby reducing computational and communication overhead. To further secure data transmission, a lightweight three-dimensional chaotic encryption mechanism is employed. The correctness and robustness of the proposed framework are validated through formal analysis under a semi-honest model, with practical limitations acknowledged. Experimental results on the KITTI dataset demonstrate that LPMS reduces computational overhead by 73.33% and communication overhead by 68.36% compared to PPFE. These results highlight the effectiveness of LPMS for privacy-preserving mobile sensing applications.

title: Rate-Adaptive Generalized Space Shift Keying Modulation Over Time-Varying Channels
authors: Ya You, Xue-Qin Jiang, Guoying Zhang, Jisheng Dai, Han Hai, Yuwen Cao
abstract: Existing space shift keying (SSK) schemes usually operate at fixed data rates, rendering them incapable of effectively adapting to time-varying channels. In this paper, a rate-adaptive generalized SSK (RA-GSSK) modulation scheme is proposed to adjust the data rate via acknowledgment (ACK) feedback. Specifically, the information sequence is mapped to a variable number of spatial vectors by a hash function and a random number generator (RNG). To enable more efficient control over the data rate, a puncturing transmission mechanism is developed based on the RA-GSSK scheme, which selectively skips certain spatial vectors during transmission. Additionally, a low-complexity pruning detection algorithm is introduced to reduce the detection complexity while maintaining reliability. Thorough theoretical analyses are conducted by deriving the union bounds for the frame error rate (FER) and bit error rate (BER) over Rayleigh fading channels. Monte Carlo simulations demonstrate that the proposed RA-GSSK achieves the dual benefits of reliability in the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regime and efficiency in the high SNR regime.

title: Evaluating the errors of Oceansat-3 winds over the Indian Ocean
authors: Jharna Borah, Kameshwari Nunna, Neeru Jaiswal, Krishna Kishore Osuri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Oceansat-3 scatterometer (O3SCAT) winds, operating in the Ku-band frequency, launched by the Indian Space Research Organization, are evaluated by collocating against OMNI, RAMA buoy, ship, ASCAT, and ERA5 reanalysis for different basins over the Indian Ocean (IO), viz., tropical-IO (TIO), north-IO (NIO), and south-IO (SIO), and different seasons during April 2023 to December 2024. O3SCAT shows an increasing bias in wind speed (WS) as wind strength increases, with overestimation at low WS and underestimation at medium and high WS. Whereas wind direction (WD) shows the opposite trend, with a decrease in bias as wind strengthens. Overall, the O3SCAT accuracies are comparable to the mission requirements of 1.8 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 20°. NIO exhibits the highest RMSD (up to 2.04 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 27.01°), followed by TIO (up to 1.99 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 27.01°), and SIO (up to 1.93 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> and 20.71°), with the Arabian Sea contributing to higher error than the Bay of Bengal. Seasonal verification over the TIO exhibits the highest error of WS (2.08 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>) during the monsoon season, and of WD during the post-monsoon season (41.88°) compared to OMNI buoy and ship data, with the highest seasonal variability. However, a consecutive triple collocation analysis shows the presence of inherent noise in OMNI buoy and ship datasets, though O3SCAT agrees well with RAMA buoy, ASCAT, and ERA5 datasets. Further analysis reveals a limitation of L2B 25 km O3SCAT data in capturing maximum winds of ocean vortices with a comparatively high RMSD of 6.51 ms<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup>. This study thus ensures the quality of O3SCAT-derived winds to the level required for assimilation in numerical models.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Driven Honeypots: Tactic-Based Defense for Industrial Control Systems
authors: Ying-Dar Lin, Rasul Mankaev, Didik Sudyana, Yuan-Cheng Lai, Ren-Hung Hwang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Machine Learning in Communications and Networking
abstract: Traditional honeypots in Industrial Control Systems (ICS) often fail to sustain attacker engagement, exposing their deception after a few predictable responses and limiting visibility to early reconnaissance. This weakness reduces their value for defenders seeking insight into later stages of the kill chain. To address this, we propose an adaptive honeypot that embeds reinforcement learning (RL) into an ICS honeypot, enabling it to shape responses in real time based on attacker behavior. Guided by the MITRE ATT&CK for ICS framework, each network interaction is mapped to a tactic, and the RL agent applies tabular Q-learning to select deception strategies such as delaying, modifying, or blocking ICS protocol messages to drive sessions deeper into the attack chain. We evaluated the system using scripted Metasploit-based attacks derived from MITRE Campaigns across three honeypot configurations: Static0 (default ICS honeypot), Static1 (modified Modbus handler to support uncommon but valid function codes), and Dynamic (RL-driven honeypot). Results show that while static honeypots stall at tactic levels 4 and 7, the RL-driven honeypot reaches the latest tactic within 700 attack–response cycles and more than doubles the average engagement time. Even in the first 400 sessions, a small portion of attacks already reach the final tactic, providing early threat intelligence. These findings show that an RL-enhanced honeypot can adapt autonomously, extract deeper threat intelligence, and give defenders broader visibility across ICS tactics.

title: A Hybrid FeedForward Neural Network-Genetic Algorithm Approach for Beam Selection Optimization in 5G Massive MIMO Systems
authors: Lujain Almomani, Mohammad Abbasi, Ayesha Wasim Qureshi, Kiyan Afsari, Mohd Fareq Abd Malek
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Massive Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) systems are crucial for 5G and beyond networks, but efficient beam selection with large antenna arrays remains a challenge. This paper investigates and compares several approaches for downlink beam selection in a simulated 5G Massive MIMO Line-of-Sight (LoS) scenario with a fixed 36-beam codebook. We evaluate a Feedforward Neural Network (FFNN) for direct prediction, a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for optimization, and propose a novel hybrid FFNN-GA model that leverages the FFNN for fast candidate selection followed by constrained GA refinement. Performance is benchmarked against exhaustive search and traditional machine learning models. Results show the FFNN achieves high accuracy (87.65%) with extremely low prediction latency (approx. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.5 \mathrm{e}-5 ~\mathrm{s} /$</tex> sample), while exhaustive search guarantees optimality fastest (approx. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.0007 ~\mathrm{s} / \text{sample}$</tex>) for this codebook size. The hybrid FFNN-GA model significantly improves accuracy to 98.00% with negligible SNR loss, demonstrating effective refinement. However, its runtime (approx. 0.0864 s/sample) remains dominated by the GA component and slower than exhaustive search in this configuration. The study highlights the accuracy-latency trade-offs, indicating exhaustive search is preferred for small codebooks, FFNNs excel for lowlatency prediction, and the hybrid approach offers near-optimal accuracy, with its speed advantage expected primarily in largerscale problems.

title: Exploring Interlinkages Between Assistive Technology and Individuals with Disabilities in India
authors: Chandrahash Patel, Priyanka Mallick, Hemant Kumar
abstract: Assistive technology (AT) has significantly improved the quality of life for individuals with disabilities in India. The global demand for AT products and solutions is significant, with the World Health Organization (WHO) emphasizing its role in enhancing functioning, independence, and autonomy. Adopting digital assistive technology (DAT) is essential in India for enhancing the independence and self-determination of those with disabilities. This study examines the status of assistive technology in India and its role in facilitating full inclusion and active participation of individuals with disabilities in all aspects of life. It also investigates how AT can help the specially-abled overcome social and professional obstacles to improve their mental and physical well-being. The article aims to enhance existing literature by providing a comprehensive overview of AT in India, proposing recommendations for further research and policy development to ensure a more egalitarian and inclusive society for individuals with disabilities.

title: Exploring the Role of Technology, Gender and their Relationship between Self-Efficacy & Academic Performance
authors: Asvitha Karthikeyan, V. Priya
abstract: The current study aims to explore the relationship between self-efficacy and academic performance among college students along with the influencing role of technology and gender in Coimbatore District, Tamil Nadu. The study was conducted with 300 samples of college students collected from 5 zones in Coimbatore based on the cluster method of probability sampling, the tools used in this study are Socio-Demographic Data; General Self Efficacy Scale; and Academic Performance Questionnaire. Correlation, regression, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex>-tests are conducted to analyze the data. Results revealed that technology usage has a significant association between both variables (self-efficacy & academic performance). The current findings point out a strong relationship between self-efficacy and academic performance of the students, and gender equality is observed in technology usage among both male and female students.

title: Sparing User Time With a Socially Aware Independent Metaverse Avatar
authors: Theofanis P. Raptis, Chiara Boldrini, Marco Conti, Andrea Passarella
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: The metaverse is redefining digital interactions by merging physical, virtual, and social dimensions, yet its effects on social networking remain largely unexplored. This work examines the role of independent avatars (autonomous digital entities capable of managing social interactions on behalf of users), to optimize social time allocation and reshape metaverse-based online social networks. We propose a novel computational model that integrates a quantitative and realistic representation of user social life, grounded in evolutionary anthropology, with a framework for avatar-mediated interactions. Our model quantifies the effectiveness of a partial replacement of in-person interactions with independent avatar interactions. Additionally, it accounts for social conflicts and specific socialization constraints. We leverage our model to explore the benefits and trade-offs of an avatar-augmented social life in the metaverse. Since the exact problem formulation leads to an NP-hard optimization problem when incorporating avatars into the social network, we tackle this challenge by introducing a heuristic solution. Through simulations, we compare avatar-mediated and non-avatar-mediated social networking, demonstrating the potential of independent avatars to enhance social connectivity and efficiency. Our findings provide a foundation for optimizing metaverse-based social interactions, as well as useful insights for future digital social network design.

title: Measurements of water quality and growth metrics in freshwater aquaculture: Dataset and technical challenges
authors: Qi-Song Colin Kiu, Chaiw-Yee Teoh, Ai-Lin Ooi, Wey-Lim Wong, Yik-Hou Chong
abstract: In aquaculture operation, it is essential to continuously monitor and maintain physical and chemical water parameters within optimal ranges while also measuring the weight and length of cultured species at defined experimental points. Although both water quality monitoring and growth measurement still rely predominantly on manual methods, their accuracy remains critical for successful cultivation and meaningful data analysis. In this paper, the ranges of water quality parameters, as well as weight and length measurements, are presented based on four aquaculture research experiments: Exp. I, involving the culture of red hybrid tilapia and native fish species in aquaponics and recirculating aquaculture systems (RAS); Exp. II and III involve culturing red hybrid tilapia and bagrid catfish, respectively, in flow-through systems; and Exp. IV, involving the culture of giant freshwater prawn in biofloc and RAS. The challenges and recommendations associated with manual measurements in these studies are subsequently discussed.

title: Multi-Fidelity Dynamic Line Rating Fusion for System Load Margin Enhancement with Large-Scale Offshore Wind Generations
authors: Tong Su, Junbo Zhao, Emil M. Constantinescu, Cosmin G. Petra
abstract: This paper proposes a multi-fidelity dynamic line rating (DLR) fusion method for power system load margin enhancement with large-scale offshore wind integration. First, multi-fidelity DLR data from diverse sensors are fused using multi-fidelity deep sigma point processes (MFDSPP), a probabilistic nonlinear learning model that integrates heterogeneous data sources to estimate the minimum line rating and quantify its uncertainty. Conformal prediction (CP), a distribution free uncertainty calibration technique that refines prediction intervals using empirical residuals, is developed to refine the uncertainty intervals of DLRs. Then, continuation power flow (CPF) analysis is used to calculate the system's maximum load margin for numerous N − k contingencies. Sampling these scenarios generates a large dataset to train the deep sigma point processes (DSPP) model, establishing a direct nonlinear relationship between operational data and load margin. Finally, DLR based line capacity constraints and DSPP-derived load-margin constraints are integrated into the real-time control framework to improve system stability. Case studies on the Northeast Power Coordinating Council (NPCC) system, incorporating real DLR sensor measurements, authentic N −k contingency datasets, and four real large-scale offshore wind farms, demonstrate that the proposed method effectively maintains a load margin greater than 10% of the base load, unlocks an additional 2,189.7 MWh of clean energy, reduces operating costs by approximately 2.34%, and achieves more than a 2,000× speedup compared with classical CPF-based sensitivity methods.

title: Hybrid machine learning pipeline for predicting and segmenting academic performance at a Mexican public university
authors: Rodolfo Alan Martínez Rodríguez, José Manuel Valencia-Moreno, Olivia Denisse Mejía Victoria, Alma Alejandra Soberano Serrano
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: Academic performance prediction remains a critical challenge in Latin American higher education, where institutional heterogeneity and limited data availability constrain the effectiveness of traditional analytical approaches. While supervised machine learning models often achieve high predictive accuracy, they typically lack interpretability and fail to capture latent student profiles required for actionable educational interventions. This study proposes a unified and interpretable hybrid framework that integrates predictive modeling and clustering within a single analytical pipeline. The approach simultaneously estimates academic risk and identifies student profiles using a real-world dataset of 386 first-year students from a Mexican public university. The results demonstrate that, while individual supervised models achieved strong predictive performance (AUC up to 0.84), the primary contribution of the proposed approach lies in its integrative capacity. The hybrid pipeline achieved competitive performance (AUC = 0.85; F1-score = 0.76) and enabled cross-paradigm validation between predictive and clustering outputs (κ = 0.70), providing evidence of structural consistency in student risk patterns. Beyond predictive accuracy, the framework links probabilistic predictions with behavioral profiles and anomaly detection, enabling the identification of heterogeneous at-risk groups and supporting targeted pedagogical interventions. These findings demonstrate the potential of hybrid learning analytics approaches to bridge the gap between predictive performance and actionable educational insights, offering a scalable and interpretable solution for early-warning systems in resource-constrained higher education environments.

title: Observer-Based Adaptive Practical Fixed-Time Event-Triggered Fault-Tolerant Control for Nonlinear Systems With Sensor Faults
authors: Lei Liang, Xia Huang, Zhen Wang
abstract: This article focuses on the problem of adaptive practical fixed-time tracking control for a class of strict-feedback uncertain nonlinear systems subject to sensor faults. To achieve the control objective of this article, a series of radial basis function neural networks and a nonlinear fault-tolerant observer are introduced to approximate unknown nonlinearities and reconstruct unmeasurable states, respectively. Moreover, an adaptive fault compensation coefficient is employed to mitigate the effects of sensor faults, while the dynamic surface control technique is applied to alleviate the “differential explosion phenomenon” arising from the backstepping method. In addition, the proposed controller design method avoids the singularity problem inherent in fixed-time control strategies. Finally, the designed event-triggered controller can guarantee that all the signals are uniformly ultimately bounded in the presence of sensor faults, the tracking error converges to a small neighborhood of zero, the convergence time is independent of the initial system states and no Zeno behavior occurs.

title: Visual and Text Prompt Segmentation: A Novel Multi-Model Framework for Remote Sensing
authors: Xing Zi, Kairui Jin, Xian Tao, Jun Li, Ali Braytee, Rajiv Ratn Shah, Karthick Thiyagarajan, Mukesh Prasad
abstract: Pixel-level segmentation is critical for remote sensing applications, yet traditional supervised methods suffer from high annotation costs. While foundational vision models like CLIP and the Segment Anything Model (SAM) offer zero-shot capabilities, they struggle with domain-specific challenges in aerial imagery, specifically: (1) scale variation causing attention drift, (2) lack of semantic discrimination leading to mask redundancy, and (3) poor adaptation to overhead perspectives. To bridge this gap without task-specific fine-tuning, VTPSeg is presented as a coarse-to-fine multi-model framework designed for high-precision off-line mapping. Unlike generic integrations, VTPSeg introduces a cohesive semantic-geometric synergy. Specifically, the Grounding DINO+ (GD+) module employs a novel synonym-based prompt strategy to maximize recall for overhead objects. The CLIP Filter++ module then utilizes a dual-prompt mechanism (visual attention circles and negative text constraints) to eliminate false positives caused by background clutter. Finally, these refined priors serve as precise point prompts for FastSAM, ensuring instance-level granularity. Validated on five diverse datasets (WHU, LoveDA, Inria, xBD, and iSAID), VTPSeg achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across five diverse datasets, demonstrating that strategic prompt engineering can effectively adapt frozen foundational models to complex remote sensing tasks.

title: Advanced Input Shaping Control Based on Reinforcement Learning for SCARA Robot
authors: Cong-Hung Nguyen, Kyoung-Su Park
abstract: In this study, a deep reinforcement learning-based input shaping technique (DRL-IST) is proposed to minimize residual vibration in industrial robot arms. The DRL-IST automatically tunes input shaper parameters to enhance vibration suppression following rapid point-to-point motions. The input-shaping design problem is formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) agent is employed to learn an optimal adaptation strategy for the shaper parameters. The proposed DRL-IST scheme is validated through simulations and experiments on a SCARA robot arm. Conventional two-impulse Zero-Vibration (ZV), three-impulse Zero-Vibration and Derivative (ZVD) shapers are implemented as benchmarks under various configurations. The results demonstrate that the DRL-IST significantly reduces residual vibrations and improves robustness against modeling uncertainties and external disturbances compared to conventional fixed-parameter input shaping, particularly in systems with configuration-dependent vibrations.

title: Byzantine-Resilient Distributed Economic Dispatch for Interconnected Microgrids: A Trusted Agent-Based Method
authors: Yuxuan Li, Dazhong Ma, Mingqi Xing
abstract: This paper investigates the economic dispatch problem in interconnected microgrids (IMGs) subject to Byzantine attacks. While IMG enhances resource allocation through collaborative dispatch to overcome the supply-demand limitations of individual microgrids, it renders the system vulnerable to malicious information injection by Byzantine agents. To address this, a resilient consensus-based Byzantine fault-tolerant algorithm, termed RC-T, is proposed. Distinct from existing studies that require prior knowledge of the maximum number of attackers, RC-T leverages trusted agents to establish dynamic decision boundaries. This mechanism effectively filters anomalous data and ensures high convergence precision, even under unknown, heterogeneous, and time-varying communication delays. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the RC-T algorithm converges to a neighborhood of the optimal dispatch solution, while simulation results further validate its effectiveness and superiority over benchmarks in handling both latencies and Byzantine attacks.

title: Doubly-Dispersive Continuous MIMO Systems: Channel Modeling and Beamforming Design
authors: Kuranage Roche Rayan Ranasinghe, Zhaolin Wang, Hyeon Seok Rou, Giuseppe Thadeu Freitas De Abreu, Emil Björnson
abstract: We address the modeling and optimal beamforming (BF) design for multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) continuous aperture array (CAPA) systems operating over doubly-dispersive (DD) channels. First, a comprehensive DD continuous MIMO (DDC MIMO) channel model that incorporates CAPAs at both the transmitter (TX) and receiver (RX) is derived, which is used to obtain explicit input-output (I/O) relations for various waveforms well suited to integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) and robust to DD channels, namely orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM), orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS), and affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM). Then, functional optimization problems are formulated for the design of TX and RX BF matrices that maximize received power, in which novel low-complexity, closed-form solutions are obtained via the calculus of variations (CoV) method, yielding expressions closely related to the classical matched filter commonly used in conventional MIMO systems. Simulation results confirm that the proposed TX/RX BF designs with CAPAs provide significant performance and computational complexity gains over conventional MIMO systems in DD channels.

title: An interval discrete wavelet neural network for rolling bearing fault diagnosis with uncertain information
authors: Xinyu Li, Shouping Guan, Xiuyuan Peng, Jianjun Wang
abstract: Aiming at the fault diagnosis problem with uncertain information, this paper proposes an interval multi-dimensional discrete wavelet neural network (IMDWNN). Based on the single-scale multi-dimensional radial wavelet frame, the network construction, parameter design and weight learning algorithm are presented in detail, which effectively avoids the curse of dimensionality and interval expansion problems caused by constructing a multi-scale frame via a tensor product. Comparative experiments between the proposed IMDWNN and five mainstream methods are conducted on a public dataset, and the results demonstrate that the IMDWNN achieves superior diagnostic performance. Furthermore, the IMDWNN is applied to the rolling bearing fault diagnosis of an X-ray orientation instrument with uncertain information, and its diagnostic results are analyzed and discussed in comparison with those of the point-valued multi-dimensional discrete wavelet neural network, confirming the effectiveness and practicability of the IMDWNN.

title: Rotation-Based Complex Permittivity Characterization of Low-Loss Slabs by Free-Space Reflection Measurements At Terahertz
authors: Bing Xue, Ugur Cem Hasar, Husain Ali
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: A rotation-based free-space method is presented for characterizing low-loss dielectric slabs at terahertz (THz) frequencies. By reflection measurements to measure the transmitted waves of a material under test (MUT) at different rotation angles in front of a back-shifted metal plate, both the complex permittivity and slab thickness are extracted without reference-plane calibration. Unlike conventional reflection-based approaches, the proposed method does not require reflection coefficients at the air–MUT interfaces. The method is analytically derived from free-space propagation theory and experimentally validated from 220 to 330 GHz using plexiglass and nylon samples. The results show good agreement with established transmission- and reflection-based techniques, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach when accurate thickness information is unavailable.

title: Wideband Single Conductive Layer Glass Picture Frame Electromagnetic Skin for 6G Upper-Mid Band Indoor Communication
authors: Minseok Chang, Jihwan Lee, Hyengcheul Choi, Changhyeong Lee, Hyosun Yang, Changhun Kim, Joongseop Kim, Jehoon Cho, Wonbin Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper is the first to present a wideband single conductive layer passive electromagnetic (EM) skin designed for upper-mid band indoor communication, employing a glass picture frame (GPF) structure that conceals the conductive patterns and enables unobtrusive deployment as a common indoor ornament, thereby enhancing decorative compatibility. The proposed EM skin effectively addresses inherent limitations of conventional microstrip-based passive EM skin designs, such as narrow bandwidth and low aesthetic value, by leveraging a modified slotted-circular GPF unit cell structure with a broad and linear phase response, low reflection loss, and good angular stability. The concept is verified through over-the-air (OTA) experiments in which the proposed GPF EM skin significantly improves propagation channel conditions across the 7–15 GHz frequency range in an indoor non-line-of-sight (NLOS) environment. Considering the practicality of the upper-mid band and the decorative appearance and simple structure of the GPF EM skin, it is expected to be effectively utilized in various indoor NLOS environments while being seamlessly integrated within building interiors.

title: Keynote Speaker: “Conjuga Molecule Design and Synthesis for Advanced Organic and Perovskite Photovoltaics”
authors: Chih-Ping Chen
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The rapid expansion of self-powered, integrated Internet of Things (loT) devices has fueled interest in advanced photovoltaic technologies capable of efficiently harvesting energy. Among these, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) and organic photovoltaics (OPVs) have emerged as frontrunners due to their high power conversion efficiency and solution-based fabrication, making them ideal candidates for addressing loT energy challenges. A critical factor in optimizing PSC and OPV performance lies in mitigating interfacial defects through engineered charge transport layers, including electron transport layers (ETLs), hole transport layers (HTLs), and interfacial layers (IFLs). In this keynote, I will present our recent advancements in the development of conjugated molecules for ETL, HTL and IFLs, enabling significant performance improvements. Our strategies include the design of efficient electronselective layers, perovskite bulk passivation techniques, and the implementation of bilayer HTL structures to enhance indoor PSC efficiency. Specifically, we demonstrate how organic semiconductors, sol-gel nickel oxide (NiOx), and self-assembled (SA) layers optimize energy alignment at interfaces, leading to enhanced carrier transport in PSCs. Under 3000K LED illumination at 1000 lux, our optimized C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">so.18</sub>FA<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">o.82</sub>Pb(I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">o.8</sub>Br<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">o.2</sub>)<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> devices achieved an impressive indoor power conversion efficiency exceeding 42%. F urthermore, we optimized bulk heterojunction (BHJ) blend layers—such as PM6:PY-IT, and PM6:Y6 and its derivatives—and their ternary counterparts to realize high-performance OPVs. Our studies demonstrate that optimized ternary blend morphologies enhance exciton dissociation, carrier transport, and overall power conversion efficiency. Additionally, we highlight improvements in OPV thermal and ambient stability, paving the way for more robust and reliable organic photovoltaic technologies. These advancements underscore the critical role of molecular design in improving passivation, electron transport, and interfacial properties-pushing the boundaries of next-generation PSCs and OPVs for energy-efficient loT applications.

title: LiFeChain: Lightweight Blockchain for Secure and Efficient Federated Lifelong Learning in IoT
authors: Handi Chen, Jing Deng, Xiuzhe Wu, Zhihan Jiang, Xinchen Zhang, Xianhao Chen, Edith C. H. Ngai, Jiangchuan Liu
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) devices constantly generate heterogeneous data streams, driving demand for continuous, decentralized intelligence. Federated Lifelong Learning (FLL) provides an ideal solution by incorporating federated learning and lifelong learning. However, the extended lifecycle of FLL in IoT systems increases their vulnerability to persistent attacks. This problem is exacerbated by the single point of failure. Furthermore, the single point of trust created by the central server hinders reliable auditing for long-term threats. Blockchain technology provides a tamper-proof foundation for trustworthy FLL. Nevertheless, directly applying blockchain to FLL significantly increases computational and retrieval costs with the expansion of the knowledge base, slowing down the training on resource-constrained IoT devices. To address these challenges, we propose LiFeChain, a lightweight blockchain for secure and efficient federated lifelong learning with minimal onchain disclosure and bidirectional verification. LiFeChain is the first blockchain tailored for FLL. It incorporates two complementary mechanisms: the Proof-of-Model-Correlation (PoMC) consensus on the server, which couples learning and unlearning mechanisms to mitigate negative transfer; and Segmented Zero-knowledge Arbitration (Seg-ZA) at the client, which detects and arbitrates abnormal committee behavior without compromising privacy. LiFeChain is a plug-and-play component that can be seamlessly integrated into existing FLL algorithms for IoT applications. To demonstrate its practicality and performance, we implement LiFeChain in representative FLL algorithms with Hyperledger Fabric under 6 representative attacks. Rigorous theoretical analysis and extensive evaluations demonstrate that LiFeChain effectively mitigates long-term attacks, maintains high scalability, and significantly reduces latency and storage overhead compared to state-of-the-art blockchain solutions.

title: Machine Learning-Enhanced Power Allocation for mMIMO-NOMA Systems in 6G Networks
authors: Mohamed Hassan, Khalid Hamid, Elmuntaser Hassan, Rashid A. Saeed, Mohammed S. Elbasheir, O. O. Khalifa
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: To enhance swift, wide communications with low latency, it is essential to address the challenges posed by emerging 6G technology. These concerns encompass the necessity to expand the frequency spectrum and augment the capacity while minimizing resource expenditures and delay. A novel strategy is suggested to enhance spectrum efficiency (SE), latency, and fairness by integrating dynamic reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) into downlink (DL) non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) power domain (PD) systems utilizing massive multiple-input, multiple-output (mMIMO) technology within the framework of 6G wireless networks. The system's scalability is evaluated to ensure optimal performance across various situations as the user count and SNR levels rise. Employed a distinctive optimization strategy to allocate power among users, effectively utilizing the water-filling logarithmic. The findings demonstrate that the incorporation of dynamic RIS in the mMIMO DL NOMA PD system markedly enhances SE, reduces latency, and improves fairness. The implementation of the proposed logarithmic method has demonstrated significant efficacy in power allocation, enhancing system capabilities while optimizing SE, latency, and fairness. The results provide essential insights for improving future wireless communication systems, and in accordance with the predicted equation, the Monte Carlo findings demonstrate that our work is precise and dependable.

title: HopeAI: A Chatbot Framework to Support Survivors of Sex and Labor Trafficking
authors: Tribhuvani Ayyagari, Ross Greer
abstract: Human trafficking is a pervasive crime that exploits vulnerable individuals for labor and sex, leaving lasting physical and psychological scars. Traditional efforts to combat trafficking, including prevention, rescue, and rehabilitation, often fall short due to limited resources and the complex needs of survivors. This paper presents HopeAI, a chatbot framework designed to provide immediate and continuous support for survivors of sex and labor trafficking. Leveraging OpenAI's assistant API and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) technology, HopeAI offers survivors information on nearby shelters, legal services, and therapeutic support, tailored to their specific needs. Additionally, the chatbot shares personal testimonies from other survivors, fostering a sense of community and hope. Our implementation focuses on resources available in Oakland, California, demonstrating the chatbot's potential to bridge the gap between rescue and rehabilitation, and providing a scalable solution applicable worldwide.

title: Gender Equality in a Digital Science Research Laboratory
authors: Carito Guziolowski, Oriane Thiery, Mounira Harzallah, Mira Rizkallah, Christine Chevallereau
abstract: The Laboratory of Digital Sciences of Nantes (LS2N) is composed of researchers working in the fields of Robotics, Computer Science, Computer Engineering, Industrial Engineering and Signal Processing corresponding to a total of around 500 people, of which 25% are women. In this laboratory, a Gender Equality and Diversity commission was created in 2019 and gathers today 34 people (65% women). This paper describes the efforts made by this commission since 2019 in order to evaluate, communicate about and improve the gender equality situation in the LS2N. The actions toward this end and discussed here include the proposition of a survey on gender inequalities early in 2020; the observation of women hiring process progress across eight years; the creation and maintenance of mentoring programs; and finally raising awareness among LS2N members through the organisation of seminars, conferences, listening groups and training. We also present the various challenges encountered and our current perspectives.

title: Enhancing the Scope of Social Work Interventions for Women Substance Users Through Technology and Digital Innovations in India
authors: Shobha Lal Kapoor, Parvathy Valsala, Arka Jyoti Sen
abstract: The field of technology-mediated interventions for mental health and substance use disorders is at a nascent stage within India and globally. There is a paucity of empirical studies in the literature that examine the effectiveness of internet-supported therapeutic interventions. In addition, there is a lack of conceptual clarity with respect to definition, terminology, modalities, methodology, and standards that must be adopted to deliver technology-based interventions in the area of physical and mental health. This paper aims to explore and review the types of digital interventions that can be used to recover substance users in India, with a specific focus on women substance users who have been victims of widespread discrimination and stigmatization. The aim of the paper is to provide a framework for mental health practitioners, researchers, and social workers to utilize and/or adapt digital tools available on the web/internet and the digital platforms developed by the Indian Government alongside traditional therapeutic interventions to empower women substance users through their addictive life cycle. The paper highlights the potential of web-based interventions, online counseling, and digital therapeutic tools in providing accessible, cost-effective, and personalized care that can be used in social work settings to provide sustainable support to female substance users. The discussions underscore the importance of leveraging digital health strategies to improve treatment outcomes and support the recovery journey of women substance users. For a developing country like India, community-level interventions delivered within an ecological framework and through the development of digital therapy tools can promptly target community-level volunteers, women drug users, their peers, families, partners, and social networks.

title: Advanced Uses of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) for Class Imbalance in Machine Learning
authors: Rusma Anieza Ruslan, Nureize Arabiy, Pei-Chun Lin
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Imbalanced datasets severely limit machine learning. As a result, models often struggle to accurately predict minority classes. This imbalance leads to bias toward the majority class, compromising the accuracy of minority class predictions. This study investigates the use of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to augment imbalanced datasets, specifically utilizing the Credit Card Fraud Detection dataset to improve classification performance. By generating synthetic samples for underrepresented classes, GANs enhance the training of algorithms such as Random Forest, Gradient Boosting and Decision Trees. A comparative analysis across multiple benchmark datasets shows that combining GAN-generated data and Gradient Boosting consistently achieves the highest accuracy among various tested algorithms. The Gradient Boosting model outperforms both Random Forest and Decision Tress, achieving an accuracy of 99.99% compared to their 99.98%. This finding demonstrates its effectiveness across different complexity levels. Results also indicate that integrating GANs improves class balance and enhances the model's ability to capture complex data patterns. This research highlights the effectiveness of using GANs for data augmentation in imbalanced settings and provides valuable insights for practitioners aiming to optimize classification tasks in realworld applications.

title: Research on Optimization of Multi-Modal Transportation System Network Considering Transfer Relationships
authors: Ruochen Shang, Chong Wu, Shiwei Guo
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: Baltimore's transportation system mainly relies on walking and buses, with researchers aiming to improve these by optimizing bus routes and sidewalk facilities. However, costs and traffic congestion remain concerns, especially after the collapse of the Francis Scott Key Bridge, which exacerbated congestion and affected travel time, freight costs, and supply chains. For Task1: To address these issues, researchers built a hypernetwork model for a multimodal transportation system considering transfer relationships. This model analyzes traveler behavior, defines feasible paths, and establishes cost functions, providing a comprehensive perspective for decision makers to identify bottlenecks, optimize resources, and promote sustainable development during bridge reconstruction, ensuring smoother transitions and minimizing disruptions. For Task2: The researchers conducted a cost analysis, studying travel time, emissions, and energy consumption for different modes of transportation. They employed a multimodal transportation network flow allocation model and sensitivity analysis to solve programming problems, aiding in quantifying the project's impact on stakeholders and providing a robust scientific basis for decision making. For Task3: Based on these analyses, the researchers proposed various measures to improve the transportation network, including setting up low-emission zones, promoting new travel modes, and reducing private car entry. They also suggested supportive measures such as strengthening parking facilities, implementing differentiated fees, optimizing traffic layout, and integrating public and shared travel options. Additionally, they proposed long-term development plans, policy guarantees, and encouraged public participation to create a green and efficient travel environment. Overall, this research report comprehensively evaluates the potential impact of optimizing bus routes and sidewalk facilities in Baltimore, proposing a range of measures and long-term plans to foster an efficient, environmentally friendly, and sustainable transportation system that caters to the city's current and future needs.

title: Improving Multi-Label Classification Accuracy for High School Mathematics Problems Using LLM Re-Ranking
authors: Shun Tanaka, Keiichi Tamura
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: Multi-label classification involves selecting an appropriate subset of labels from a predefined label set for a given data sample. In this study, we address a multi-label classification task for high school mathematics problems, where multiple curriculum-based math-unit labels are assigned to each problem. Manual labeling is challenging due to the required mathematical expertise and the considerable time investment. To mitigate these issues, we propose a novel classification approach for assigning multiple math-unit labels to high school mathematics problems using a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework. To incorporate background knowledge absent from the query representing a mathematics problem, we generate an example solution using a Large Language Model (LLM) finetuned on mathematical content. The similarities between the query and elements in the vector database are then computed using distributed representations. However, our previous method exhibited limitations in both accuracy and versatility. In this study we integrate re-ranking based on LLMs and perform a comprehensive evaluation using multiple LLM variants. Experimental results demonstrate an accuracy improvement of approximately 14% compared with existing methods.

title: Case Study: Integrating RISC-V Zicfiss Shadow Stack Support for Control-Flow Integrity on the CVA6 Processor
authors: Behnam Farnaghinejad, Ernesto Sanchez
abstract: Control-flow integrity (CFI) is a fundamental defense against control-flow hijacking attacks that corrupt return addresses, and hardware-supported shadow stacks have been adopted in several mainstream processor architectures to provide backward-edge protection. The RISC-V Zicfiss extension introduces analogous CFI support into the RISC-V ecosystem, yet its practical integration into RISC-V processors remains largely unexplored. This paper presents a case study on integrating Zicfiss shadow stack support into the CVA6 processor, an opensource application-class RISC-V core representative of systems executing rich operating systems and user applications. The extension's architectural behavior is implemented and validated across privilege levels, establishing a correct and specificationaligned baseline for enforcing backward-edge CFI. This work enables a systematic study of control-flow protection mechanisms in advanced RISC-V designs. It provides a foundation for future investigations into robustness and hardening against physical and microarchitectural attack vectors.

title: AI-Assisted Complementary Negative Capacitance Tunnel FETs: A Path Toward Superior Circuit Performance
authors: Sujaya Grace Ch, Anshul Awasthi, Surya Shankar Dan, Ramakant Yadav, Pooran Singh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: This article presents a novel AI-assisted design of ntype and p-type Negative Capacitance Gate-Overlap Tunnel FETs (NC-GOTFETs) using K-nearest neighbors (KNNs) regression, emphasizing its potential for high-speed and low-power VLSI applications. The optimized device geometry, which integrates a ferroelectric (FE) Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 (HZO) layer between the gate and dielectric to enhance device performance, enables complementary operation by modifying materials and doping profiles. A fully data-driven, non-parametric modeling technique based on K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) regression is used to precisely describe the ferroelectric characteristics of the HZO layer. Unlike traditional Landau–Khalatnikov models, this method directly utilizes experimental polarization–electric field (P–F) data of Zrdoped hafnia, ensuring a more realistic and physically consistent representation of ferroelectric properties. Device performance is evaluated in terms of Ion, Ioff, Ion/Ioff, sub-threshold swing (SS), gate-source capacitance Cgs, gatedrain capacitance Cgd , gate capacitance Cg ,and intrinsic delay (T). The NC-nGOTFET achieves an Ion of 261.7 μA, representing a (∼7.6×) improvement over the conventional nGOTFET, along with a reduced SS of 34.7 mV/dec and intrinsic delay of 1.294 ps/V compared to 59.1 mV/dec and 4.078 ps/V of the nGOTFET, respectively. Similarly, the NC-pGOTFET achieves an Ion of 212.5 μA, offering a (∼6.7×) improvement over the pGOTFET, along with reduced SS of 65.5 mV/dec and intrinsic delay of 1.688 ps/V compared to 129.8 mV/dec and 4.449 ps/V of the pGOTFET, respectively. At the 32 nm node, the proposed complementary NC-GOTFETs outperform conventional GOTFETs, MOSFETs, and recently reported NC-TFETs in the literature, demonstrating strong potential for superior circuit performance.

title: EXACT: An Explainable Anomaly Classification Tool
authors: Ted Boman, Theo Gould, Shahriar Hasan, Mir Riyanul Islam, Per Strandberg, Tiberiu Seceleanu
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is widely used in Industry 4.0 and Industry 5.0 applications for anomaly detection and predictive analytics. However, the opaque decision-making of most Machine Learning (ML) models limits interpretability and hinders effective decision-making and root cause analysis. Explainable AI (XAI) seeks to address these challenges; however, the combined processes of anomaly detection and explanation generation often require substantial domain expertise, limiting their accessibility to a broader audience. To address this gap, this paper presents EXACT (EXplainable Anomaly Classification Tool), a novel, modular software framework that enables practitioners and researchers to rapidly and easily perform explainable anomaly detection on time-series data, regardless of domain expertise. EXACT integrates time-series anomaly detection with XAI techniques into a single end-to-end workflow, including data handling, anomaly injection, parameter tuning, model training, anomaly detection, explanation generation, and visualization, making these features broadly accessible and user-friendly to the wider community. The effectiveness of EXACT is demonstrated through rigorous evaluations conducted on three public time-series datasets from distinct domains, using three anomaly detection models and three widely adopted XAI methods. Among these, XGBoost exhibits the most balanced predictive performance, achieving 99.96% accuracy, 82.61% F1-score, and 98.59% ROC-AUC on the Credit Card Fraud dataset, while Decision Trees demonstrate significantly lower training times. In terms of explainability, SHAP consistently delivers high-quality feature rankings, reaching an NDCG score of 0.98 on the Credit Card Fraud dataset, whereas LIME provides substantially faster explanation generation. These results demonstrate that EXACT effectively facilitates explainable anomaly detection in an efficient, user-friendly manner, while also providing a systematic analysis of trade-offs among predictive performance, interpretability, and computational efficiency. EXACT is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/TedBoman/EXACT), and the community is invited to contribute to its continued development and extension.

authors: N/A

title: Dynamic Event-Triggered Data-Driven Hierarchical Control for Networked MASs Against Byzantine Attacks
authors: Shitao Duan, Guangdeng Chen, Hui Ma, Hongyi Li, Tingwen Huang
abstract: A data-driven hierarchical control strategy is proposed to address the leader-following consensus problem for nonlinear networked multi-agent systems (MASs) with unknown dynamics under Byzantine attacks. The proposed hierarchical framework decouples the leader-following consensus control into leader state estimation and trajectory tracking, only using input/output data of systems. Specifically, a discrete-time distributed observer is first proposed to securely estimate the leader state utilizing the mean-subsequence-reduced-f algorithm, which effectively filters out malicious data from Byzantine agents in networked MASs. Then, a data-driven decentralized controller is developed to track the estimated leader state. In addition, we extend this strategy into a dynamic event-triggered data-driven hierarchical control algorithm, which not only securely achieves the leader-following consensus but also conserves network communication resources. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

title: Spectral-Efficient LoRa with Low Complexity Detection
authors: Alireza Maleki, Ebrahim Bedeer, Robert Barton
abstract: In this paper, we propose a spectral-efficient LoRa (SE-LoRa) modulation scheme with a low complexity successive interference cancellation (SIC)-based detector. The proposed communication scheme significantly improves the spectral efficiency of LoRa modulation, while achieving an acceptable error performance compared to conventional LoRa modulation, especially in higher spreading factor (SF) settings. We derive the joint maximum likelihood (ML) detection rule for the SE-LoRa transmission scheme that turns out to be of high computational complexity. To overcome this issue, and by exploiting the frequency-domain characteristics of the dechirped SE-LoRa signal, we propose a low complexity SIC-based detector with a computation complexity at the order of conventional LoRa detection. By computer simulations, we show that the proposed SE-LoRa with low complexity SIC-based detector can improve the spectral efficiency of LoRa modulation up to 445.45%, 1011.11%, and 1071.88% for SF values of 7, 9, and 11, respectively, while maintaining the error performance within less than 3 dB of conventional LoRa at symbol error rate (SER) of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> in Rician channel conditions.

title: Higher-Order Difference Phase Unwrapping for Microwave-based Vibration Measurements
authors: Jiahui Cao, Zhibo Yang, Yajie Guan, Shuming Wu, Asoke K. Nandi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Microwave interferometry is a promising technique for non-contact vibration measurement. However, due to the inherent drawback of phase wrapping, the measurable vibration amplitude is limited to a quarter wavelength (γ). To pursue high resolution and compact size, high-frequency microwave radars, e.g., millimeter-wave radars, are increasingly adopted. Yet, a higher carrier frequency further reduces the effective sensing range, which makes accurate measurement of large-amplitude vibrations challenging. To overcome this limitation, we propose a higher-order difference-based phase unwrapping (HDPU) method that significantly expands the applicability of microwave radar to vibration measurement. This method originates from an interesting finding: for a multi-sinusoidal signal with a proper high sampling rate (larger than π-times the Nyquist rate), there always exists a sufficiently large order such that the higher-order difference of the signal samples is limited to [−γ, γ]. This finding implies that the higher-order difference of the signal samples can be obtained from the wrapped phase. Considering the intrinsic connection between the multi-sinusoidal signal sample and its higher-order difference in the frequency-domain, HDPU enables robust vibration reconstruction by utilizing the spectral sparsity. By combining the higher-order difference and sparse reconstruction, HDPU allows microwave radar to measure vibrations with amplitudes exceeding a quarter wavelength without distortion. More importantly, it overcomes Itoh’s limitation and shows higher robustness to noise than unlimited sampling.

title: TechRxiv: Share Your Preprint Research with the World!
authors: N/A

title: Low-Level CDM Radiated ESD Event Detection and Classification Using a CMOS Logarithmic SoC
authors: Chang-Jiun Lai, Ming-Dou Ker
abstract: Low-level electrostatic discharge (ESD) events, including charged-device-model (CDM) and human-body-model (HBM) discharges, pose increasing reliability risks in advanced semiconductor manufacturing as automated handling, fine-pitch packaging, and 3-D integration, amplify the occurrence of short, low-energy transients. These weak events radiate broadband, nanosecond electromagnetic impulses that often fall below the sensitivity of conventional ESD monitors, thereby limiting the ability to identify them in real time on production equipment. This work presents a CMOS system-on-chip (SoC) detector designed for monitoring radiated low-level CDM and HBM ESD events. The SoC incorporates a gain-programmable logarithmic front end, a fast-recovery transient comparator (CMP), and a dual-domain discriminator that extracts both event magnitude and duration, along with a compact antenna interface that enables nonintrusive field sensing. Experimental evaluations demonstrate the reliable detection of CDM discharges down to ±25 V, and HBM discharges down to 100 V. These results indicate that the proposed SoC provides a practical and deployable solution for monitoring low-level CDM and HBM events in semiconductor fabs and advanced packaging environments.

title: Concurrent Dual-Band Continuous Class-F Power Amplifier: From Theory to Practice
authors: Sayedmohammad Daneshvar F., Filipe M. Barradas, Luís C. Nunes, Pedro M. Cabral, José C. Pedro
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article extends the continuous single-tone Class-B/F power amplifier (PA) theory to concurrent dual-band operation. Continuous load impedance design spaces, with constant efficiency and output power under concurrent two-tone excitation, are deduced and analyzed. Contrary to single-tone classes, in concurrent-band operation, the intermodulation frequencies play a central role in the performance, which increases the complexity of the analysis. The proposed method makes use of the notion of envelopes to simplify the problem. Using this notion, the waveform shaping can be applied at both the envelope and/or carrier levels, yielding different operation classes, which are analyzed in this manuscript. Under the proposed methodology, the baseband impedance is a consequence of the designed waveforms, rather than being set at the short-circuit. Similarly, terminations at other intermodulation frequencies also naturally arise under the framework used. The derived impedances for concurrent-band differ from those of traditional single tone, indicating that design methodologies based on frequency-by-frequency are suboptimal. As a proof of concept, a concurrent dual-band Class-F PA operating at 1.8 and 2.15 GHz was designed, implemented, and experimentally validated. The measured output power and efficiency for different tone asymmetries are consistent with the developed theory. Moreover, with the proposed design target impedances, there is no performance degradation under concurrent-band operation when the PA is excited with a modulated signal in each band.

title: Imaging Results from a Direct Conversion X-ray Detector with TlBr and CMOS Pixel Array
authors: J.F. Christian, M.S. Squillante, M.J. Breen, O. Maksimov, H. Kim, E. Weststrate, A. Kargar, V. Nagarkar, M. Dallimore, A. Pil-Ali, K. Karim, C. Scott, M.R. Squillante
abstract: This work explores the fabrication and characterization of X-ray imaging devices using a single-crystal TlBr sample (8 mm × 8 mm × 10 mm) and polycrystalline TlBr film (approximately 0.38-mm thick), both bonded to a Libra-1 Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (CMOS) readout chip connected to the BrillianSe 1M readout system. The BrillianSe 1M, designed for X-ray radiography by KA Imaging, features a 10 mm × 11 mm readout chip with a 7.8 μm pixel pitch and a 7.8 mm × 7.8 mm active area. The work below describes the characterization of test and imaging devices. The characterization includes current-voltage (IV) curves and impedance spectra, longevity measurements of the X-ray response and leakage current, and imaging characterization with light-emitting diodes (LED) and X-rays. The resistivity of 3x1010 Ω-cm was estimated for a polycrystalline film sample. Following five weeks, the leakage current decreased from 670 to 6.4 nA, while the X-ray photocurrent decreased from 28 to 4 nA. Both the single crystal and polycrystalline imaging devices resolved 0.3-mm-diameter holes in a Tungsten composite mask when irradiated with 90 kVp (100 μA tube current) X-rays. Effects at the interface can be seen with both LED and X-rays. The images provide critical information for optimizing the fabrication process and device longevity.

title: Pump Flow Compensation and Variable-Gain Sliding Mode Control for Mitigating Temperature-Induced Degradation in Servo Pump-Controlled Systems
authors: Zhiqiang He, Fugang Zhai, Zhuofan Yang, Yangtao Xing, Xiangdong Kong
abstract: Hydraulic-oil temperature variations are a nonnegligible factor in servo pump–controlled systems. As temperature rises, viscosity decreases, which induces pump-flow nonlinearities and weakens throttling authority, thereby degrading positioning accuracy and repeatability. To mitigate these effects, this article proposes a temperature-aware composite control strategy that integrates pump-side inner compensation loop with a position-based variable-gain sliding-mode outer robust loop. An inverse-amplification scheme based on online pump-efficiency estimation is coupled with variable-gain sliding mode control: the inner loop compensates pump-flow nonlinearities, while the outer loop suppresses multisource uncertainties and dynamic disturbances. Together, they provide a two-layer safeguard of “source-level compensation + robust outer-loop regulation.” Comparative tests across multiple operating conditions show that, relative to the baseline proportional-integral-derivative (PID), the proposed strategy improves both repeatability and positioning accuracy by more than 25%. Under nominal and high-speed conditions, it achieves accuracy improvements exceeding 70%. These results demonstrate markedly enhanced high-precision positioning robustness and temperature tolerance under elevated-temperature disturbances and long-term temperature drift.

title: A Novel Dynamic Sparse Convolution Residual Network for Incipient ITSC Fault Diagnosis of Electric Machines
authors: Han Tang, Guohai Liu, Xiangjin Song, Zhengmeng Liu, Qian Chen
abstract: Existing ITSC fault diagnosis methods for electric machines still face challenges in terms of incipient fault detection, noise robustness, cross-motor type applicability and multiple operating conditions. To this aim, this article proposes a novel dynamic sparse convolutional residual network fault diagnosis model for detecting incipient ITSC fault and estimating severity levels in electric machines. First, a novel dynamic sparse convolution paradigm is proposed in the diagnostic model to extract local features and long-range periodic dependencies from time-series signals. Second, an improved squeeze-and-excitation mechanism is introduced to adaptively enhance the representation of fault-related discriminative features. Extensive experiments carried out across three distinct electric machine platforms demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior diagnostic accuracy and noise robustness compared with the state-of-the-art models, while maintaining low computational cost. The proposed method also exhibits good generalization performance for unseen machines. Furthermore, visualization analysis is conducted to enhance the model’s interpretability.

title: HRIS-Assisted Communications: Trade-off between Combination and Separation
authors: Junhao Duan, Jia Hou, Limei Peng, Shuping Dang, Pin-Han Ho
abstract: This correspondence considers a double-HRIS-assisted system to investigate the impact of separating an intact HRIS into two sub-RISs on system performance under a fixed total number of RIS elements. Specifically, an achievable rate maximization problem is formulated by jointly optimizing the allocation of active and passive elements, the transmit power at BS, and the amplifying power for two HRISs under the overall power constraint, and an alternating optimization algorithm is proposed to solve the problem. Based on the obtained solution, we further compare the proposed double-HRIS scheme with the single-HRIS scheme and the active RIS (ARIS) and passive RIS (PRIS) coexisting schemes under the same total power budget, and analyze the conditions under which the double-HRIS outperforms the other configurations. The analysis shows that the distance between the transmitter and the user plays a key role in the performance comparison. Finally, simulation results verify the correctness of the analysis and further reveal that the superiority of the HRIS over the coexisting ARIS-PRIS scheme becomes more pronounced as the communication distance increases, while the double-HRIS exhibits greater potential than its single-HRIS counterpart in wide-area communications.

title: Analysis and Design of IRS-Assisted NOMA for Vehicular Visible Light Communication Systems
authors: Mingbo Niu, Ruihang Ji, Jian Yang, Xiaoqiong Huang
abstract: The rapid advancement of communication technologies has profoundly affected the development of intelligent transportation systems (ITS). The inherent unpredictability of vehicles and the complexity of their environments pose significant challenges to reliable communication within vehicle networks. Non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) is an innovative wireless communication technology with promising prospects, aimed at addressing challenges such as low latency, high accuracy, and reliability in communication system. Visible light communication (VLC) is introduced as an environmentally sustainable medium for information transmission in vehicle-to-vehicle communication systems, and NOMA is incorporated to enhance system performance. First, the NOMA-assisted line of sight (LOS) and non-line of sight (NLOS) VLC systems are modeled in detail, and an IRS-assisted NOMA scheme is subsequently developed for vehicular VLC systems. Second, the channel gain, channel capacity, ergodic rate (ER) of both near and far vehicles in IRS-assisted NOMA vehicular communication system are analyzed, along with their asymptotic performance. Additional, the bit error rate (BER) under perfect phase compensation and phase errors is also investigated. Finally, the theoretical analysis is validated by the numerical simulation results, and it is shown that the proposed VLC system improves communication efficiency.

title: Index Author
authors: N/A

title: Bayesian Calibration for High-dimensional Parameter Identification in Transformer Winding Diagnostics via FRA-derived Modal Features
authors: Yulu Fan, Shuhong Wang, Yanxin Ren, Youpeng Huangfu, Sicheng Zhao, Guoli Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Equivalent circuit models are significant for correlating frequency response analysis (FRA) signatures with mechanical conditions in power transformer windings. Nevertheless, accurate diagnosis is hindered by the high sensitivity of FRA signals to external interference and strong coupling among circuit parameters. This study presents an integrated approach combining subspace identification with the affine-invariant ensemble sampler (AIES) to overcome these challenges. The modal features of the passive system are determined by the roots of its characteristic equation, which depend on the circuit parameters. Subspace methods enable the extraction of these modal features directly from the measurements. The posterior distribution of the circuit parameters is estimated using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling. Specifically, the AIES algorithm is employed, notable for requiring only a single tuning parameter and exhibiting robustness to affine transformations. By integrating parameter scanning with physical mechanism analysis, the framework for identifying radial and axial winding deformations is established. Validation was performed using a specifically designed 10/0.4 kV, 200 kVA transformer. Quantitative evaluation confirmed less than 5% error against measured responses, with computation time below 150 seconds for the validation case. The proposed diagnostic criteria successfully linked parameter shifts to verified physical deformation.

title: Learning to Evade: Adaptive and Stealthy Attack on Reputation-Based Federated Learning
authors: Xining Liu, Erwu Liu, Wei Ni, Hanfu Zhang, Zhongxiang Wei, Ekram Hossain
abstract: Existing reputation-based defense mechanisms in Federated Learning (FL) operate predominantly under the premise of static adversarial strategies, and underestimate the adaptability of intelligent attackers and their threat. This reveals a novel dynamic attack framework that interpretes an attack process as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), which captures an adversary’s sequential decisionmaking under uncertainty and is implemented online using Monte Carlo tree search. Navigating an environment where the server’s trust evaluation is undisclosed, the POMDP-based attack adaptively alternates between malicious injection and benign participation to optimize the long-term trade-off between damage infliction and detection avoidance. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that this new attack can outmaneuver state-ofthe-art reputation defenses, achieving a superior efficacy-stealth trade-off. On MNIST under MAB-RFL with 20% attackers, the attack suppresses model accuracy to 26.64%, whereas static and adaptive baselines fail to prevent convergence (with accuracy of 92.71% and 89.82%, respectively). Awareness of the existence of this attack is important for the safe operation of FL.
