title: CONV_GBERT: Leveraging BERT-GPT Fusion and IMP-FGSM for Adversarially Robust Sentiment Classification
authors: Areeba Umair, Elio Masciari, Muhammad Habib Ullah
abstract: This paper introduces CONV_GBERT, a hybrid deep learning framework for robust sentiment classification. The model integrates the bidirectional contextual understanding of BERT, the generative capabilities of GPT, and the local feature extraction strength of convolutional neural networks. By concatenating embeddings from BERT and GPT and passing them through a convolutional layer, CONV_GBERT captures both global semantic meaning and fine-grained sentiment patterns in text. To further enhance robustness and generalization, we incorporate Improved Fast Gradient Sign Method (IMP-FGSM) for adversarial training. IMP-FGSM introduces controlled perturbations to the input embeddings and iteratively refines them using gradient-based updates. This technique improves the model’s resistance to adversarial attacks while preserving valid token boundaries. Experiments on benchmark sentiment datasets demonstrate that CONV_GBERT significantly outperforms baseline models. It achieves an accuracy of 84.89%, precision of 83.67%, recall of 82.88%, and an F1 score of 83.37%. These results highlight the potential of combining multiple transformer-based architectures and adversarial training to advance sentiment analysis in complex, noisy, and dynamic textual environments.

title: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters Information for Authors
authors: N/A

title: Analysis of Accuracy, Area, Latency, and Energy Consumption of Gas-sensing-based In-sensor Neuromorphic Computing
authors: Jonghyun Ko, Kangwook Choi, Jiseong Im, Donghee Kim, Hunhee Shin, Ryun-Han Koo, Sung-Ho Park, Jeseung Jeong, Gyuweon Jung, Jong-Ho Lee
abstract: The increasing demand for in-sensor neuromorphic computing motivates a system-level framework that jointly evaluates accuracy, area, latency, and energy. We prototype a platform that integrates a fabricated FET-based gas sensor with an embedded micro-heater, a floating-gated pMOS synapse array, and processing circuits, such as pulse-width modulator (PWM) and integrate-and-fire (I&F) neurons. Using transient responses to H2S and NO2 across temperature and concentration sweeps, we benchmark networks with 3–6 input sensors, three hidden layers (16–128 neurons), and two regression outputs. Accuracy improves with more sensors and larger hidden layers, saturating beyond 64 neurons. Quantization and circuit constraints add bounded error. Area is insensitive to sensor count but scales with hidden neurons, with the high-voltage switch dominating area. Latency is sensor-limited. Energy consumption is overwhelmingly set by the micro-heater (~99.97% of total), making overall consumption highly sensitive to sensor count while relatively insensitive to network size. These results identify trade-offs among accuracy, area, latency, and energy, pinpointing heater efficiency, minimal sensor selection, and high-voltage-switch optimization as the primary design-technology co-optimization (DTCO) keys for designing compact, fast, and energy-aware in-sensor neuromorphic platforms.

title: A Novel Approach with Channel Reduction Scheme for Depression Symptom Identification based on Resting-State EEG_supp1-3679027.docx
authors: Jianbang Xiang
abstract: Resting-state electroencephalography (EEG) offers a scalable approach to quantify affective neurophysiology. Using data from eighty-one young adults aged 18–25 years, this study proposes a systematic framework to address limitations in the integration of multidimensional EEG features and the insufficient characterization of their spatial distribution. We first extracted a comprehensive set of time-, frequency-, time–frequency, nonlinear, and connectivity features to capture the multiscale neural patterns associated with depression symptom. A statistical feature-selection procedure based on the two-sided Mann–Whitney U test was applied to identify robust discriminative EEG markers. Using the full 64-channel feature set, the constructed model achieved strong overall performance (AUC = 0.958), providing a reliable baseline for subsequent spatial analyses. The selected features were then mapped onto electrode locations to characterize their spatial distribution, and a progressive channel-reduction analysis was performed to determine the minimal electrode set needed to preserve predictive performance. The channel-reduction analysis indicates that using only 5, 8, or 12 electrodes still maintains high performance, with AUC values of 0.879, 0.918, and 0.948, respectively. This framework provides a reproducible foundation for developing low-density, wearable EEG systems for mental-health monitoring.

title: A Transformer-based probabilistic deep learning framework for uncertainty-aware long-range flood forecasting using satellite-derived mesoscale inputs_supp1-3681213.pdf
authors: JungMin Lee

title: Multi-Sensor Multi-Source Localization under Clutter and Uncertainty
authors: Matthias Ospel, Philipp Mikus
abstract: Multi-source localization from time difference of arrival measurements in sensor networks is challenging in the presence of clutter, timing uncertainty, missed detections, and an unknown number of sources. We propose a combinatorial framework that jointly addresses these challenges by evaluating feasible subsets of detections, referred to as coalitions. Each coalition is assigned an associated cost defined as the ℓ∞-norm of the time difference of arrival residuals. We retain coalitions whose residual lies below a user-specified pseudorange threshold, providing a physically interpretable control of coalition feasibility. Coalition selection is performed using a family of set packing algorithms, including greedy and binary integer linear programming formulations with cost-based and lexicographic objectives. The lexicographic variants maximize sensor consensus by prioritizing, from largest to smallest cardinality, the number of selected disjoint detection coalitions, and break ties by minimizing the sum of coalition costs. This provides a unified, geometrically consistent framework for joint data association based on maximized sensor consensus, clutter suppression, source counting, and localization. The methods are benchmarked using Monte Carlo simulations under varying levels of clutter, time jitter, and missed detections, as well as using an acoustic dataset recorded in an urban environment.

title: Dual 1-Bit Quantization for Conformer-CTC: A Decomposition-Based Mixed Precision Approach
authors: Jinsu Yeo, Youngmin Kim
abstract: While state-of-the-art Conformer-CTC models deliver remarkable accuracy, their substantial memory footprint hinders on-device deployment. To address this constraint, weight quantization serves as a standard compression strategy; however, applying naive 1-bit quantization to CTC architectures often causes severe performance degradation. To bridge this gap, we propose a resource-efficient fine-tuning framework tailored for large, pre-trained Conformers. We leverage Sign-Value-Independent Decomposition (SVID), which approximates weights using rank-1 vectors, offering superior parameter efficiency compared to conventional block-wise quantization. Crucially, we reinvest the memory budget saved by SVID into a sensitivity-guided mixed-precision scheme. By allocating the top 40% of layers exhibiting the highest quantization sensitivity to a Dual 1-bit (2-bit) representation, we achieve superior accuracy while maintaining a model size smaller than standard block-wise 1-bit baselines. Experimental results on LibriSpeech demonstrate that our 28MB model achieves 2.61% and 5.71% WER on test-clean and test-other respectively, significantly outperforming existing methods.

title: Inclusive Learning Model for Technology-Enhanced Vocational Education Through Culturally Responsive Pedagogy
authors: Sithara GS, Reshma Ramesh, Bhanu VR, Niksheeta Nayar, Srividya Sheshadri
abstract: Women in rural India, are the most underrepresented in vocational education and training despite the fact that a trained female workforce is crucial for economic success. Due to highly ingrained socio-cultural conventions, many women have difficulty accessing formal vocational training. Although technology-enhanced methods have increased accessibility, traditional models of vocational education have not sufficiently addressed the cultural settings and difficulties faced by female students. In order to address this gap, a Computerised Vocational Education and Training (CVET) model that adopted culturally responsive pedagogy was developed and implemented to provide vocational training to female learners across rural India. The present study focuses on an implementation of the CRP-based CVET model in one vocational training center located in Kerala, India administered under India's Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship. Qualitative analysis was used to investigate the lived experience of 10 female students who participated in training program, to understand their experience with technology enhanced vocational training. Central themes emerged through this process include the influence of the learner's culture on their engagement with the technology-enhanced vocational learning environment and the criticality of preparing trainers to adapt the delivery of technology-based teaching tools to the learner's needs. The study's findings highlight the importance of aligning CRP with the delivery of technology-enhanced teaching and learning. Additionally, the study offers recommendations for the further refinement and implementation of the CVET model, highlighting strategies to maximize its potential for promoting cultural responsiveness and inclusivity.

title: The Impact of Privacy and Security on E-Commerce Adoption: A Cross-Country Analysis
authors: Auliya Az-zahra, Rio Guntur Utomo, Farisya Setiadi
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This study investigates the influence of privacy and security perceptions on e-commerce adoption by integrating constructs from the Protection Motivation Theory and the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology. Using data from 211 respondents in Indonesia and Malaysia, the research applies Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling to examine the relationships among key variables. The findings reveal that privacy and security factors significantly impact e-commerce adoption in both countries, although with different patterns. In Indonesia, six out of ten hypotheses were supported. Perceived vulnerability (t-statistic 2.778), response efficacy (t-statistic 2.623), and perceptions of privacy and security (t-statistic 2.218) significantly influence behavioral intention. Furthermore, behavioral intention strongly affects actual use (t-statistic 16.790). In Malaysia, only five hypotheses were supported. Self-efficacy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex>-statistic 2.239) and effort expectancy (t-statistic 2.157) significantly influence behavioral intention, while perceived vulnerability shows a slightly stronger effect on privacy and security perceptions (t-statistic 2.866) compared to Indonesia. These results underscore how cultural and regulatory contexts shape privacy and security concerns in e-commerce adoption.

title: Bending Loss Phenomenon in Optical Signal Evaluation on Single-Mode Fiber
authors: Fatima Nur Ramadhani, Saktioto, Yan Soerbakti, Tengku Emrinaldi, Syamsudhuha, Rina Amelia, Okfalisa, Dedi Irawan, Nor Ain Husein, Muhammad Sufi Roslan, Kusherbayeva Maikul, Kushkimbayeva Bibara
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Bending loss is a major issue in optical fiber communication systems, where excessive curvature can disrupt signal transmission and significantly degrade optical signal quality. This study addresses the problem by investigating how bending affects power loss in single-mode fibers (SMFs), which are widely used in long-distance and high-speed networks. The objective is to analyze the variation of bending loss across five commercial SMF types-SMF-28, SMF-28e, SMF-28e+, SMF28e+LL, and SMF-28ULL-through numerical simulations using OptiFiber software. The simulations were performed at operational wavelengths of 1310 nm and 1550 nm, with bending radii ranging from 20 mm to 46 mm. Results show that bending loss increases as the radius decreases, with distinct sensitivity patterns observed in each fiber type. SMF-28 exhibited the highest loss at 1550 nm (up to 31.96 dB/km), while SMF-28e+ showed the most stable performance. The novelty of this study lies in its comparative evaluation of bending sensitivity across multiple SMFs under consistent simulation parameters. These findings provide valuable insights for selecting appropriate fiber types and designing resilient optical networks that minimize signal degradation caused by mechanical stress.

title: EdgeGuard: Blockchain-Enhanced Secure Data Circulation via Aggregatable Distributed Key Generation_supp1-3679770.pdf
authors: Yizhong Liu
abstract: Data has become a critical driver of innovation in artificial intelligence and the evolution of 6G technologies. The explosive growth of data volume accelerates the convergence of cloud and edge computing, while simultaneously posing heightened challenges to data security and privacy. The emerging cloud-edge-device collaborative paradigm enables dynamic and large-scale data circulation across heterogeneous entities, exposing systems to complex threats such as malicious edge nodes and eavesdropping over untrusted communication channels. In response to these issues, we propose EdgeGuard, a secure and decentralized framework for cloud-edge-device data circulation. EdgeGuard is specifically designed for highly dynamic environments and ensures robust data confidentiality, integrity, traceability, and resilience against both malicious external attackers and compromised edge servers. To underpin its cryptographic foundation, we develop two core primitives. Specifically, we introduce an Aggregatable Publicly Verifiable Secret Sharing (APVSS) scheme that enables efficient sharing of field elements while supporting aggregation and public verifiability. Furthermore, we construct AggDKG, a distributed key generation (DKG) protocol. AggDKG achieves public verifiability and bias resistance with an expected total communication cost of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mathcal {O}}(\kappa n^{3})$</tex-math></inline-formula> effectively overcoming the scalability limitations inherent in traditional complaint-based protocols. Collectively, these components form a comprehensive framework that strengthens secure and efficient data circulation in cloud-edge-device systems. Experimental data show that AggDKG delivers clear performance gains: across all tested scales, its total running time is only about 8%–65% of that of the DKG of Gurkan et al., and at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n=256$</tex-math></inline-formula>, it reduces per-node runtime by approximately 27% compared with the DKG of Gennaro et al. These results highlight EdgeGuard's superior scalability, lower latency, and stronger Byzantine resilience for secure large-scale deployments.

title: ZSS/Certificateless Signature-based Hierarchical Verification Scheme for IIoT Hot/Cold Data
authors: Yilin Yuan, Jianfang Lu, Xi Zeng, Bo Gao, Zichen Li
abstract: In edge-cloud collaborative IIoT systems, heterogeneous data generated by industrial devices are typically categorized into hot data and cold data according to their access frequency and timeliness requirements. However, existing data integrity verification schemes usually adopt a unified auditing mechanism, which fails to accommodate the distinct performance and security requirements of hot and cold data, and cannot effectively support dynamic data migration across edge and cloud layers. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a hierarchical and lightweight data integrity auditing scheme for edge-cloud collaborative IIoT environments. Specifically, for frequently accessed and latency-sensitive hot data stored at edge nodes, a lightweight auditing mechanism based on ZSS signatures is designed to enable efficient verification under resource constraints. For cold data stored in the cloud, a certificateless signature-based auditing model is introduced to eliminate key escrow risks while reducing certificate management overhead. Furthermore, to support the dynamic evolution of data states during the IIoT data lifecycle, a sampling-based data migration mechanism is proposed, which enables secure migration between edge and cloud layers without downloading the complete data, while preserving audit continuity and verifiability. Security analysis demonstrates that the proposed scheme ensures audit correctness and effectively resists forgery attacks. Extensive performance evaluation demonstrates that, compared with existing schemes, the proposed approach significantly reduces both computational and communication overhead, particularly under large-scale data blocks and high-frequency auditing scenarios.

title: Life-Cycle-Oriented Based Design and Optimization of LLC Resonant Converters for Light-EV Charging
authors: Meltem Lordoglu, Yusuf Behauddin Guney, Abdulsamed Lordoglu, Mehmet Onur Gulbahce
abstract: This paper presents a sustainability-oriented multi-objective design and optimization framework for a 4 kW LLC resonant converter intended for on-board charger applications. The proposed approach combines conventional electrical and thermal design procedures with life-cycle-based environmental modeling to achieve an optimal balance between efficiency, cost, volume, and carbon footprint. The methodology determines resonant parameters (Lr, Lm, and Cr) across a frequency range of 100–250 kHz and evaluates each configuration analytically, including magnetic component design, semiconductor loss estimation, and heatsink sizing. Environmental impacts are incorporated through cradle-to-gate and use-phase Global Warming Potential (GWPman and GWPusv) metrics for semiconductor, magnetic, and passive components. The optimization results reveal that SiC-based MOSFETs provide higher efficiency and lower operational emissions but at the expense of increased manufacturing footprint and cost, whereas Si-based diodes offer a more balanced trade-off between environmental and economic performance. A 4 kW prototype designed using the GWPuse-weighted optimal parameters achieved a peak efficiency of 98.1% within an input range of 325–375 V and an output range of 250–450 V, experimentally validating the proposed framework. Overall, the study demonstrates that integrating sustainability metrics into LLC converter design enables the development of high-performance, environmentally responsible, and cost-effective power conversion systems.

title: Uncooled Poisson Bolometer for High-Speed Event-Based Long-wave Thermal Imaging_supp1-3682677.pdf
authors: Zubin Jacob Zubin Jacob
abstract: Event-based vision provides high-speed, energy-efficient sensing for applications such as autonomous navigation and motion tracking. However, implementing this technology in the long-wave infrared remains a significant challenge. Traditional infrared sensors are limited by either slow thermal response times or the high power demands of cryogenic cooling. Here, we introduce the first event-based infrared detector operating in a Poisson-counting regime. This is realized with a spintronic Poisson bolometer capable of experimentally validated broadband detection from 3–14 μm across the MWIR and LWIR atmospheric transmission windows, with sensitivity estimated to extend into the SWIR regime down to 0.8 μm based on near-infrared transduction layer characterization. In this regime, infrared signals are detected through statistically resolvable changes in stochastic switching events. This approach enables room-temperature operation with high timing resolution. Our device achieves a maximum event rate of 1,250 Hz, exceeding the maximum detectable event rate of conventional uncooled microbolometers by a factor of 3.6. Power consumption is kept low at 0.2μW per pixel. These results demonstrate a pathway toward high-speed, energy-efficient, event-driven thermal imaging.

title: Adaptive Neural Boundary Control of a Rotating Flexible Beam with Input Dead-Zone and Output Constraint
authors: Baozhao Zhang, Dongsheng Xu, Xing Li, Yang Yu, Jinpeng Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper addresses the adaptive neural network-based boundary control problem for a rotating flexible beam system (RFBS) subject to unknown distributed and boundary disturbances, input dead-zone nonlinearity, and parametric uncertainties. The system is composed of a flexible beam with one end fixed to the center of a rotating disk, the other end is attached to a tip mass. The system is governed by coupled partial and ordinary differential equations (PDEs–ODEs), presenting significant challenges due to its infinite-dimensional nature. To achieve simultaneous vibration suppression and strict enforcement of boundary output constraints, a novel adaptive boundary control scheme is developed. The proposed controller incorporates a barrier Lyapunov function (BLF) to handle output constraints, radial basis function (RBF) neural networks to compensate for unknown system dynamics and dead-zone effects, and a disturbance estimator to reject boundary perturbations. By constructing a composite Lyapunov function candidate, the uniform ultimate boundedness (UUB) of the closed-loop system is proved via Lyapunov stability analysis. Numerical simulations are conducted to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed control approach.

title: Concurrent Dual-Band Quadrature Phase Shift Frequency-Selective Receiver
authors: Mohammadreza Majidimosleh, Somayeh Akhtari, Mohamed Helaoui, Fadhel M. Ghannouchi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A novel concurrent dual-band frequency-selective receiver architecture is proposed and analyzed. The core of the receiver is a quadrature passive mixer driven by a dual-band differential local oscillator (LO) signal, generated by the combination of two sinusoidal sources. This architecture eliminates the need for complex multiphase clock generation circuits typical of N-path filters, reducing system complexity and power consumption. A foundational theoretical analysis reveals that the conversion gain and linearity of the mixer are dependent on the relative phasing of the two LO signals, with distinct behaviors observed for different frequency ratios. The receiver’s architecture inherently supports digital image rejection, achieved through a reconfigurable summation of the baseband quadrature outputs, allowing for robust reception under both low-side and high-side injection (HSI) scenarios. To validate the theory, a prototype was fabricated using pHEMT switches. Measurement results demonstrate excellent agreement with the theoretical model. The system achieves a peak conversion gain of −12.0 dB, an average in-band IIP3 of 1 dBm, and an image rejection ratio (IRR) of 39.1 dB. Furthermore, the receiver’s capability to recover complex, concurrent wideband signals is demonstrated with an EVM of 1.24% for a 3-MHz LTE signal and 2.33% for a 5-MHz NR signal.

title: Novel Transform Based Optimization for Resource Allocation and Task Offloading in Communication Networks
authors: Yitong Wang, Jun Zhao, Liangxin Qian, Chang Liu
abstract: In wireless communication and edge computing networks, fractional programming (FP) and multiplicative programming (MP) are fundamental methodologies widely employed in solving non-convex optimization problems. Prior work introduced a remarkable method to solve non-convex functions with multiple ratios by deriving novel tight upper bounds, which we formalize as the UpperBound transform in this paper. However, the UpperBound transform faces critical limitations, particularly when directly extended to MP problems involving non-negative functions or discrete optimization variables. In this paper, we introduce a generalized transform termed the UP transform to overcome these limitations.We rigorously prove that the UP transform guarantees convergence to a Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) point for a broader class of MP problems, including scenarios where variables can be zero or discrete. We comprehensively illustrate the UP transform’s utility through two practical applications: partial task offloading in mobile edge computing, optimizing computation and energy efficiency; and user association coupled with resource allocation in heterogeneous networks, addressing mixed discrete-continuous optimization challenges. Comparative evaluations against conventional methods demonstrate superior convergence speed, efficiency, solution quality, and reduced computational complexity of the proposed UP transform based algorithms.

title: Compression and k -mer Based Approach for Anticancer Peptide Analysis
authors: Sarwan Ali, Tamkanat E Ali, Prakash Chourasia, Murray Patterson
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Anti-cancer peptide (ACP) sequence classification is crucial for cancer treatment development. Current neural network approaches achieve high accuracy but require substantial parameters and training data. Recent compression-based methods compress entire sequences, potentially missing fine grained neighboring information critical for classification. We propose a novel approach integrating Gzip compression with an incremental k-mer strategy. Unlike conventional methods, we compress individual k-mers and incrementally build subsequence compressions, preserving amino acid-level context. Using Normalized Compression Distance (NCD) and kernel-based embeddings, our parameter-free method achieves state-of-the-art performance on breast and lung cancer ACP datasets, outperforming deep neural networks and large language models without requiring custom features or pre-trained models. Our approach provides a practical, efficient alternative to computationally intensive methods, proving effective even in low-resource environments.

title: Dual-Mode Seamless Transition for Hybrid Distribution Transformer With Energy Storage
authors: Yanting Xue, Yibin Liu, Qidong Wen, Hao Deng, Deliang Liang, Lianchao Yu, Lishi Zhang
abstract: This article proposes a dual-mode seamless transition control scheme for the hybrid distribution transformer with energy storage (HDT-ES) system, explicitly addressing the actuation delays of mechanical circuit breakers. First, a phased transition strategy is developed based on real-time state monitoring. By leveraging the series converter for transient voltage compensation, it ensures continuous power supply reliability during the switching process. Second, a dual-mode control framework is established to coordinate the shunt and series converters. This framework ensures stable control authority handover between grid-connected and islanded modes. Third, a coordinated DC-link voltage control strategy is designed to manage the power balance between the converters and the energy storage unit during different operating modes. Simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness and stability of the proposed strategy under various load conditions.

title: On Inverse Log-Digamma Difference Function for Wireless Signal Processing
authors: Jia Ye, Shuping Dang, Peng Wang, Yihang Xu, Qianggang Wang, Shuaishuai Guo
abstract: The transcendental equation that represents the difference between the logarithmic and digamma functions has practical significance in wireless signal processing and wireless analytics, particularly in the applications of channel parameter estimation and channel model substitution (CMS). However, the inverse of such a crucial transcendental equation cannot be expressed in closed form using elementary or classical special functions. Therefore, in this letter, we formally define such an inverse as the inverse log-digamma difference (ILDD) function and establish it as an effective mapping inversion. We also prove the key analytical properties of the ILDD function, including its existence, uniqueness, differentiability, and monotonicity, and derive several tight bounds on it. Jointly applying the proven analytical properties and derived bounds, we further propose an accurate and robust approximation method for the ILDD function over the entire domain of definition. The effectiveness of all analytical results is verified by numerical computation via the Newton-Raphson (N-R) method and a case study of CMS.

title: Two-Stage Risk-Based Scheduling for Electricity-Biogas Rural Microgrids with Biomass Fermentation
authors: Bo Li, Zikun Liu, Hongxu Huang, Haiwang Zhong, Zhengmao Li, Wenfa Kang, Josep M. Guerrero
abstract: Electricity-biogas rural microgrids (EBRMs) possess significant economic potential and the ability to coordinate multiple energy sources, making them a promising paradigm for energy management in rural areas. This paper proposes an energy scheduling method for an EBRM that utilizes renewable and biomass resources, with the goal of economically meeting rural electricity demand. The biogas fermentation process is modeled as a nonlinear biochemical dynamic system, where biogas production is governed by differential equations involving fermentation temperature and total solids (TS) concentration. A practical biogas fermentation model, which considers the dynamics of temperature and TS, is developed and subsequently linearized using McCormick relaxation. To more effectively capture renewable energy uncertainties beyond the tail focus of Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR), a Gini-weighted CVaR (GWCVaR) model is introduced to quantify the risk of load shedding, formulating a stochastic mixed-integer programming problem. To address the mismatch between long-term biogas scheduling and day-ahead microgrid dispatch, a two-stage optimization strategy is proposed. Finally, simulation results demonstrate its effectiveness. The proposed method achieves a 26.2% reduction in operating cost compared to a baseline without biogas integration, while the integrated GWCVaR reduces load-shedding risk by over 85% with only a 3.4% cost penalty.

title: Interaction Mechanism Analysis of Rainstorm‑Induced Disruptive Incidents in Power and Transportation Systems
authors: Peixiao Fan, Siqi Bu, Fuzhang Wu, Shuangqi Li, Yuxin Wen, Jun Yang
abstract: Electric vehicles (EVs) intricately link distribution systems (DS) and transportation systems (TS) into coupled systems. Under extreme weather conditions (EWCs), especially rainstorm‑induced urban flooding, disruptive incidents such as power shortages, road blockages and traffic congestion induce complex interactions between two systems, amplifying negative impacts on EV users and system operations. However, the mechanisms underlying these interactions remain insufficiently comprehended. Therefore, this paper analyzes the complex interactions of disruptive incidents between systems, revealing the cascading effects within systems under EWCs. First, an improved power support capacity (PSC) calculation algorithm is developed to reveal how disruptive incidents in TS affect DS, by quantifying the impact of user behaviors on load distribution of DS, with the behaviors arising from user demands shaped by TS states and EWCs. Similarly, user decision-making mechanisms are proposed based on improved cumulative prospect theory and integrated into dynamic traffic equilibrium models, revealing how DS influence user decisions and, in turn, interact with TS under EWCs. Furthermore, the concept of “Disruption Containment Threshold” and the associated “Disruption Mitigation Gap” are proposed to quantify scenario-dependent capacity limits against cross-system disruptions. Finally, simulations show that the rainstorm-induced flooding scenario intensifies these interactions, causing large‑scale traffic congestion and insufficient PSC.

title: Strengthening EV Charging Infrastructure Against Mass-Scale Cyber Threats
authors: Neetesh Saxena, Samuel Ashman, Liana Cipcigan, Omer Rana
abstract: The rapid advancement of Electric Vehicles (EVs) has made charging stations a critical component of the overall EV charging infrastructure, but their increasing complexity also extends the attack surface, leaving them vulnerable to exploitation. This article identifies key security issues and challenges facing EV charging infrastructure, especially against mass scale cyber threats, explores gaps within existing solutions, and proposes an architecture and recommendations based on identified gaps. By offering targeted recommendations and insights, we provide a guideline to strengthen EV charging systems and their infrastructure against emerging cyber threats.

title: Wearable Fractal Antenna for 1.575 GHz of Global Positioning System and 2.4 GHz of Wireless LAN Applications
authors: Mohd Fareq Abd Malek, Nornikman Hassan, Abeer ElKhouly, Ayesha Wasim Qureshi, Hock Chuan Lim, Majid Munawar, Muhammad Zuhair Bolqiah Edris
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Tracking and monitoring systems have seen rapid development across multiple sectors, including military and law enforcement, to enhance operational capabilities. This research focuses on the design of a wearable flexible textile antenna with a fractal shape that can simultaneously operate at Global Positioning System (GPS) and Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) frequencies. The primary objective is to develop a single antenna capable of covering both frequencies, thereby improving system integration in wearable applications. A new fractal-shaped antenna was designed and simulated using CST electromagnetic simulation software, achieving optimal reflection loss (S11) and surface current results at the target frequencies. For GPS (1.575 GHz) and WLAN (2.4 GHz), the CST simulation showed return losses of - <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 1. 4 8 ~ d B}$</tex> and - <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 4. 6 7}$</tex> dB, respectively. Subsequent measurements yielded return losses of - 14.47 dB for GPS and - 11.01 dB for WLAN, demonstrating the antenna's effectiveness for dual-frequency operation within the desired ranges.

title: LPCG: A Capture Activity-Aware Technique for Low-Power Scan Testing
authors: Rohit Badjatya, Jaydev Bapat, Makoto Ikeda, Masahiro Fujita, Virendra Singh
abstract: As semiconductor technology advances to deepsubmicron nodes, power consumption during manufacturing test has become a critical design constraint. In particular, high switching activity during the capture phase of at-speed transition delay testing induces IR-drop and ground bounce, causing false failures and significant yield loss. This paper introduces Low-Power Capture Grouping (LPCG), a fine-grained scan-cell partitioning technique that directly mitigates capture power. LPCG uses a novel Capture Activity-Aware (CAA) algorithm to partition scan cells into segments by analysing fault-essential capture behavior across the full test set. Each segment is controlled by an in-chain Segment Control Bit (SCB) that enables per-pattern clock-gating. By activating only the minimal set of segments required to detect a given fault, LPCG substantially reduces simultaneous switching during capture. Compared to the state-of-the-art method, experiments using at-speed transition delay fault (TDF) patterns on ISCAS'89 benchmarks show LPCG achieves an average capture power reduction of 43.4%. This is achieved at the cost of an average 7.8% increase in test data volume and similar area overhead, demonstrating a practical trade-off for power-constrained VLSI testing.

title: Convolutional Neural Networks for Energy Estimation in High-Energy Calorimetry
authors: Diogo Alves Cardinot, Bernardo Sotto-Maior Peralva, Gustavo Barbosa Libotte, Luciano de Manhães Andrade Filho
abstract: Particle accelerators are complex facilities that collide particles at extreme high speed, aiming at discovering new physics. In this context, high-energy calorimeter systems plays a crucial role as it provides the particle energy quantity that is an important information for the potential new discoveries. This work evaluates the performance of the Optimal Filter (OF) method and several Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures in reconstructing the amplitude of simulated signals that represent the response pulses produced by calorimeters in high-energy physics experiments. The comparison is conducted using quantitative metrics — including RMS, MAE, MedAE, and Coefficient of Determination. The results show that different CNN architectures exhibit varying performance depending on the occupancy rate but generally outperform the commonly used linear method, providing more accurate reconstructions. Although the proposed requires a large execution time due to the training process across multiple folds, these findings indicate that CNNs are promising alternatives for calorimeter energy reconstruction, particularly in high-occupancy conditions as the ones expected for high-luminosity experiments.

title: Assessing the Limitations of Activation Clipping for Fault Mitigation in Vision and Language Transformers
authors: Lucas Roquet, Fernando Fernandes dos Santos, Luigi Carro, Angeliki Kritikakou
abstract: Deep Neural Network (DNN) activation clipping is a well-established method for mitigating hardware-induced faults during inference. Clipping constrains activations to predefined ranges and filtering corrupted values, including NaN and Inf. However, high variability in activation distributions, both across and within layers of a model, makes it challenging to define fixed ranges that reliably capture corrupted values beyond extreme cases. In this study, we show that activation clipping alone is insufficient to protect vision and language Transformers from inference faults, and that inputs exhibit markedly different levels of fault sensitivity depending on the model's confidence. Our experiments show that the missclassification rate can reach 10.28% even with per-layer clipping is employed.

title: A Textile Antenna Embedded in Traditional Attire for Body-Centric Communication
authors: Hassan Al Lawati, Zia Nadir
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This paper presents the design, simulation, and analysis of a wearable U-shaped textile antenna tailored for integration into traditional attire, with the aim of enabling reliable body-centric communication. The antenna is fabricated on a cotton substrate, selected for its softness, breathability, and cultural appropriateness in the context of traditional garments. The cotton material exhibits a dielectric constant of 1.6, a loss tangent of 0.04, and a thickness of 0.25 mm, making it suitable for wearable applications. The antenna was modeled and optimized using Ansys High Frequency Structure Simulator (HFSS) to resonate at the 2.45 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) band. Simulation results confirm a strong resonance at 2.46 GHz, with an operational bandwidth of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 6 \%}$</tex>. The far-field radiation pattern reveals a boresight-directed main lobe with minor back lobes, attributed to substrate and ground plane constraints. The compact and low-profile design supports seamless integration into clothing without affecting performance or wearer comfort. This study validates the potential of incorporating textile antennas into culturally traditional garments, paving the way for discreet and user-friendly wearable communication technologies

title: A Conceptual Framework for Enhancing Sustainability of Halal SMEs in Malaysia Using Blockchain Technology
authors: Imanina Binti Mohd Zainuldin, Han-Foon Neo, Chuan-Chin Teo, Rajiv Dharma Mangruwa
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The Halal industry, encompassing a wide array of products and services, is pivotal to the economic growth of Muslim-majority countries like Indonesia and Malaysia. These nations are at the forefront of the global Halal market, driven by their substantial Muslim populations and commitment to upholding Halal standards. Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) are crucial contributors to this industry, offering diverse Halal products ranging from food and beverages to pharmaceuticals and cosmetics. However, ensuring the sustainability of Halal products in terms of integrity and traceability remains a significant challenge for these SMEs. Blockchain technology has emerged as a revolutionary tool in addressing traceability issues across various industries, including the Halal sector. By providing a decentralized and immutable ledger, blockchain can enhance transparency, traceability, and trust in Halal supply chains. This study aims to utilize the application of blockchain technology in enhancing the traceability of Halal products for SMEs in Malaysia. This study proposes a blockchain-based supply chain management system to enhance sustainability. By leveraging blockchain's core attributes, including immutability, decentralization, and automation, the proposed system enables real-time tracking and certification validation. Key stakeholders such as suppliers, manufacturers, and certification agencies benefit from a more efficient and trustworthy system. The findings of this study can provide valuable insights for policymakers, industry stakeholders, and SMEs, paving the way for a more robust and transparent Halal market.

title: Generative AI for Analyzing Participatory Rural Appraisal Data: An Exploratory Case Study in Gender Research
authors: Srividya Sheshadri, Unnikrishnan Radhakrishnan, Aswathi Padmavilochanan, Christopher Coley, Rao R. Bhavani
abstract: This study explores the novel application of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) in analyzing unstructured visual data generated through Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA), specifically focusing on women's empowerment research in rural communities. Using the “Ideal Village” PRA activity as a case study, we evaluate three state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) — GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Gemini 1.5 Pro — in their ability to interpret hand-drawn artifacts containing multilingual content from various Indian states. Through comparative analysis, we assess the models' performance across critical dimensions including visual interpretation, language translation, and data classification. Our findings reveal significant challenges in AI's current capabilities to process such unstructured data, particularly in handling multilingual content, maintaining contextual accuracy, and avoiding hallucinations. While the models showed promise in basic visual interpretation, they struggled with nuanced cultural contexts and consistent classification of empowerment-related elements. This study contributes to both AI and gender research by highlighting the potential and limitations of AI in analyzing participatory research data, while emphasizing the need for human oversight and improved contextual understanding. Our findings suggest future directions for developing more inclusive AI models that can better serve community-based participatory research, particularly in gender studies and rural development contexts.

title: Empowering Social Interventions: An Adaptive Monitoring Solution with Integrated NLP Analytics
authors: Shyam Prasad Nedumaran, Aswathi Padmavilochanan, Spiridon Bakiras
abstract: This paper presents an Information Management System (IMS) with a comprehensive data architecture for monitoring and managing geographically dispersed social interventions. The IMS features customizable program metrics, Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), and parameters to adapt to diverse programs. The system uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) models, specifically BERT and RoBERTa, to analyze qualitative data such as feedback and reports gathered from the grassroots level. The analysis aligns these insights with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and highlights intersections between overlapping sub-domains, such as education and gender roles. These intersections are demonstrated through a detailed case study of a Women's Empowerment intervention, where women provided feedback on the challenges they faced while participating in the program. The IMS facilitates remote project monitoring, enabling managers to oversee multiple projects across hard-to-reach locations, thereby improving operational efficiency and program outcomes. Built for scalability, the system can be extended to support a wider range of social interventions in the future.

title: Enhancing Access to Health Information for Adolescent Girls in Bangladesh Through a Culturally Responsive AI Chatbot
authors: Shamim Ahamed, Marzia Zaman, Md Abdullah Al Mashud, Nafisa Khan, Farhana Sarker, Khondaker A. Mamun
abstract: The advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) is leading to new possibilities in healthcare. This paper introduces a chatbot to support adolescent girls in Bangladesh who face cultural and social barriers in accessing sexual, reproductive, and mental health (SRMH) information. With smartphones and digital platforms becoming widespread, conversational agents can provide personalized health education and access to information but many existing tools fail to address the needs of linguistically diverse populations like Bengali speakers. Our research fills this gap by using language models and utilizing methods such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combined with keyword-based filters and LLM-based classifiers. Our chatbot caters to the cultural and language needs of Bengalispeaking female adolescents, especially focused on SRMH. Leveraging a clinically validated dataset, our chatbot achieves an overall accuracy of 72.5%, which is a 46.5% improvement over the baseline RAG method. Our findings show how LLMs can be used in healthcare education for teenage girls and highlight the need for model improvement and larger datasets. It's a step forward in using AI to promote women's health literacy in areas where information is scarce.

title: AI's Influence on Gender Representation and Societal Norms in Emerging Technologies
authors: Ramya G R, Keshav S, Vimal Harihar S K
abstract: This paper explores the impact of artificial intelligence (AI) on gender representation and social norms. As AI increasingly mediates interactions with media, social networks, and workplaces, its influence on gender identities and the reinforcement of societal norms becomes more pronounced. This study analyzes AI systems on social media platforms, content recommendation engines, and autonomous decision making systems to understand how gender roles and expectations are portrayed, reinforced, or challenged. The findings highlight both the opportunities and risks posed by AI in shaping a more equitable or biased gender landscape. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of AI's role in promoting inclusivity or perpetuating stereotypes, and proposes a framework for building more gender-inclusive AI systems.

title: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters Information for Authors
authors: N/A

title: COOPMamba: Efficient Vehicle-to-Vehicle Cooperative Perception based on 3-D Point Clouds
authors: Peng Zhang, Xinju Chen, Yunji Liang, Xiaokai Yan, Zhiwen Yu
abstract: Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) cooperative perception promises enhanced environmental awareness but still suffers from two critical gaps: (i) existing intermediate-fusion models typically rely on single-branch feature aggregation, which struggles to simultaneously capture localized discriminative cues and long-range contextual structures, and (ii) attention-based fusion modules, like Transfomers, incur high computational and communication overhead, limiting real-time deployment. To address these limitations, we propose COOPMamba in this paper, a lightweight yet expressive collaborative-perception framework that introduces a dual-branch decomposition of salient and global information and a CMamba cross-branch state-space interaction block. The proposed CMamba block performs direction-aware, long-range feature propagation, enabling more robust multi-vehicle feature alignment and mitigating missed detections caused by incomplete observations. Extensive experiments on V2XSet and OPV2V demonstrate the effectiveness of our design: COOPMamba achieves 89.1% AP@0.5 and 77.1% AP@0.7 on V2XSet, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches by 2.5%–6.4%, while maintaining the lowest MACs and inference latency among existing fusion methods. The results confirm that our cross-branch state-space modeling substantially improves collaborative 3D object detection under both ideal and noisy real-world conditions. The source code is available at https://github.com/npunancy/coopmamba.

title: Three-wavelength laser interferometric absolute measurement technique for static/dynamic composite signals
authors: Jingshan Jia, Shuyang Duan, Peixuan Li
abstract: A three-wavelength laser interferometric absolute measurement technique is proposed. In contrast to the White-light interferometry, this technique can achieve absolute measurement using only three fixed-wavelength lasers. Across the 0-1.1 mm cavity length range of a Fabry–Perot interferometer (FPI), the theoretical demodulation error for the static cavity length remains below 0.1%, even when the cavity length variation is much greater than 100 μm. The experiments have demonstrated the demodulation of static/dynamic composite signals by this technique when the cavity length is 0-1.1 mm. Demodulation errors of the static component for static/dynamic composite signals are < 0.75% in experiments. The maximum cavity length variation measured in experiments is ~100 μm. High accuracy demodulation of the dynamic component is experimentally demonstrated, and is unaffected even by significant changes in the static cavity length. The proposed technique is a passive demodulation method and is therefore suitable for detecting high frequency signals. A 20 kHz signal is successfully demodulated in an experiment. The proposed technique provides a simple and reliable solution for high-speed absolute demodulation of static/dynamic composite signals, demonstrating promising potential for engineering applications.

title: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
authors: N/A

title: Free-Running EPI Motion Tracking in MRI via Gradient-Coupled Coil Signal
authors: Enamul H. Bhuiyan, Muntafa M. Mahi, Mushifikur R. Navin, Muhammad E. H. Chowdhury
abstract: Objective: To develop a comprehensive, hardware free, and real-time motion tracking method for prospective correction of motion artifacts in EPI-based brain MRI, which remains highly sensitive to rapid motion and geometric distortion. Methods: We propose a gradient-induced voltage–based motion tracking framework that leverages time-varying magnetic flux detected by small surface coils to estimate six degrees of freedom of rigid-body head motion. An analytical equation describing gradient-induced voltage was derived from electromagnetic theory and validated through second-order modeling and high fidelity numerical simulations. A lightweight nonlinear regression model maps induced voltages to estimates of head pose. Simulation-based analyses evaluated sensitivity to coil placement variability, gradient noise, and non-ideal trajectories. Results: The proposed forward model demonstrated stability under moderate coil misalignment and realistic noise conditions without requiring sequence modification or additional scanner hardware. In free-running EPI experiments with human subjects, the predicted motion estimates correlated strongly with image-based measurements, supporting the real-time feasibility of the method. In human experiments, predicted motion closely matched SPM8 estimates, with mean deviations ≈ 1mm in translation and ≈ 1° in rotation across all six degrees of freedom, even for large movements (≈ 20° rotation and any translations within the gradient linearity range). Conclusion: The framework offers accurate and computationally efficient head motion estimation, making it suitable for integration into prospective motion correction pipelines. Significance: This work advances MRI motion correction by enabling rapid, scanner hardware modification-free, and sequence independent tracking using only gradient-induced coil signals, thereby enhancing the reliability of neuroimaging under naturalistic motion conditions.

title: A Modular Coupled Inductor-Based Regenerative Cascaded Multicell Converter for Medium Voltage Drive
authors: Satyam Jha, Shambhu Sau
abstract: Cascaded multicell converter (CMC) is proved to be an effective solution for medium-voltage (MV) high-power variable-speed drives. To enable this converter with regeneration capability, the diode bridge rectifier is replaced by an active front-end rectifier (AFER) in each cell. However, this configuration requires large filter inductors and overrated transformers due to switching harmonics, produced by AFER. This article proposes a coupled-inductor-based modular regenerative CMC employing three-phase AFER cells. The modular design enhances scalability, while the coupled inductor (CI) effectively attenuates switching harmonics, thereby reducing transformer volt-ampere (VA) rating. In addition, the CI ensures equal current sharing among cells, reducing both control complexity and the number of current sensors required. The three-phase AFER eliminates subharmonic currents in motor and grid currents and reduces the DC-link capacitor requirement of each cell. The proposed topology is experimentally validated on a developed laboratory prototype, and the results confirm the effectiveness of the converter.

title: Case Study: Exploring nMR Implementations for Energy Monitoring in Telecom Base Stations Under Non-Ionizing Radiation
authors: Elián Nevado, Jimmy Tarrillo
abstract: At elevated altitudes, increased neutron flux raises the risk of radiation-induced soft errors, compromising critical systems. This work evaluates coarse-grained N-modular redundancy (3, 5, and 7MR) with Hamming-protected instruction memory in a PicoBlaze soft-core for telecom power monitoring. Runtime fault injection on an Artix-7 FPGA assesses reliability, logic utilization, and power consumption. Results show significant gains up to 5 MR, while 7 MR yields marginal improvements relative to its hardware and energy cost, confirming diminishing returns in redundancy scaling. These results highlight reliabil-ity-cost trade-offs in resource-constrained FPGA applications.

title: Impact of Alpha-Induced Single Events on the Inference Confidence of Quantized Neural Networks on Cortex-M
authors: Leonardo R. Gobatto, Nathan Guimarães, Fabio Benevenuti, Antonio Carlos S. Beck, Fernanda L. Kastensmidt, Jose Rodrigo Azambuja
abstract: This paper investigates the impact of alpha radiation-induced single events on neural network (NN) inferences running on Cortex-M processors. Our methodology utilizes alpha particle irradiation experiments to assess how these single events modify the output confidence of the NN results. Using image2col-based operations, we experimented with three distinct NN topologies combined with both <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4}$</tex>-bit and 8-bit quantization levels. Results indicate a reliability trade-off between the two precision levels: while the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4}$</tex>-bit implementation demonstrated a higher Mean Executions Between Failures due to its smaller memory footprint, it resulted in higher error magnitudes when faults occurred. Additionally, the experiments showed that the topology with the lowest spatial resolution in its final layers was more sensitive to error propagation, exhibiting larger deviations in output confidence. These experiments also reiterate the inherent resilience of NNs, where single-event upsets primarily degrade inference confidence rather than causing incorrect classifications.

title: On the Study of Development of Broadband Pyramidal Microwave Absorber Using Date Palm Leaf Waste: A Sustainable Approach
authors: Abdollah Hassan, Aparna Krishna, Abdelattif Alotaibi, Anas Labib, Nasser Shaibani, Mohamed Ouda, Jamal Kurdi
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The growing demand for sustainable materials and the increasing impact of electromagnetic interference (EMI) have accelerated research into eco-friendly alternatives for microwave absorption and shielding applications. This study investigates the potential of agricultural waste as a low-cost, renewable filler material for developing dielectric composites suitable for microwave absorbers used in anechoic chambers. The date palm leaf, an abundant agricultural residue in Qatar, is incorporated into polyester resin at varying concentrations to fabricate DPL/Polyester composite samples (DPL1, DPL2, and DPL3). The dielectric properties of these composite samples are analyzed using a dielectric assessment kit. Results showed that increasing DPL content decreased the real permittivity while increasing dielectric loss and conductivity, indicating enhanced electromagnetic energy dissipation. Among the samples, DPL3 exhibited the most favorable dielectric properties for electromagnetic wave absorption. Later, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 3$</tex> array of pyramidal microwave absorbers (PMA) was simulated in HFSS using the measured dielectric parameters. The proposed model demonstrated excellent performance, achieving over 99% EM wave absorption across a broad frequency range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 - 2 0 ~ G H z}$</tex>.

title: Evaluating the Usability, Performance, and Cost-Efficiency of Deploying ML Models on Cloud Computing Platforms
authors: Hongyu Wang, Jeong Yang, Young Lee
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: With enhanced computing capabilities and accessibility to cloud resources, cloud computing providers offer Machine Learning (ML) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) services. Their primary purpose is to provide efficiency, scalability, and adaptability in modern software development and IT operations while reducing overall costs and operational complexity. However, prospective customers often question which ML and AI service will best suit their organizational and business needs. This study compared and analyzed the usability, performance, and costefficiency of deploying ML models across cloud platforms. Specifically, it examined BigQuery ML and Vertex AI in Google Cloud, Redshift and SageMaker in AWS, and Azure Machine Learning. Using MIMIC-IV datasets of hospitalized patients, regression models were deployed on each platform to predict mortality and disease progression. The results of the analysis revealed that Google's BigQuery ML offers good usability with great documentation and a moderate learning curve, which makes it suitable for SQL-savvy users and large-scale data analytics tasks. Google's Vertex AI, while more flexible, incurs relatively higher costs. The costs of AWS SageMaker are unpredictable due to its parallel training processes, while Azure ML, although requiring complex authorization settings, is accessible to novice users. These findings provide valuable insights for selecting an appropriate AI-ML service for deploying similar ML models when making informed decisions for cloud-based ML deployments.

title: Gendering Digital Inclusion: A Study on Kudumbashree Women's Access, Awareness and Empowerment
authors: Kochukrishna Kurup V S, Malavika RS, Ashwin CG
abstract: This study examines digital inclusion among Kudumbashree women in southern Kerala, where the focus is on access, awareness, and empowerment. The research uses quantitative approach where survey was conducted with 80 participants from the southern districts of Kerala, which are Thiruvananthapuram and Kollam districts. The result of the study shows the different dimensions of digital usage among Kudumbashree women such as Digital Inclusion and Access, Digital Literacy and Skills, Digital Awareness and Usage, Perceived Benefits and Challenges and General Opinions about Digital Usage The result of this study shows high digital inclusion <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(67.5 \%)$</tex> and awareness (62.5 %), but the digital literacy is low (55 %). There was a significant difference found between digital inclusion and access and perceived benefits and challenges (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p=0.037$</tex>) and between digital literacy and skills and digital awareness and usage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{p}={0.000})$</tex>. These findings highlight a gap in the literacy and skills on digital technologies among the participants.

title: Social Dynamics of Energy Transition: Gender and Cultural Implications in Rural India
authors: Raihana Aliyu Mustapha, Aryadevi Remanidevi Devidas, Aji Abba
abstract: Energy transitions in rural India present an opportunity to combat energy poverty and promote socio-economic advancement. The success of these initiatives is significantly affected by the social dynamics of the communities they serve, including gender roles and cultural norms. This review examines the gendered and cultural implications of rural energy transitions in India, emphasising the challenges encountered by women in obtaining and managing energy resources. The review identifies three key challenges: the marginalisation of women in energy decision-making, cultural opposition to innovative technology, and the digital gap that restricts women's access to renewable energy alternatives. Although current initiatives like the National Energy Policy and Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana have enhanced energy access, they are predominantly gender-neutral and do not address the specific needs of women. The results indicate that gender-sensitive policies and culturally inclusive strategies are essential for achieving equitable energy transitions. Recommendations encompass the inclusion of women in energy decision-making, addressing the digital gap, and offering technical training to enhance women's capabilities in energy management. The analysis emphasises the necessity for more research on the long-term effects of gender-sensitive energy policy and community-driven energy initiatives in rural India.

title: Hierarchical Single-Event Effect Avoidance and Handling Methods for TT&C Links of GEO All-Electric Propulsion Orbit-Injection Satellites
authors: Haixu Wang, Xin Wang, JiangLei Gong, Yu Zhang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 15th International Conference on Communication and Network Security
abstract: None

title: A Survey of Model Inversion Attacks: An Interface-Prior Perspective
authors: Haoyu Liu, Yuwen Chen, Zhen Yang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 15th International Conference on Communication and Network Security
abstract: None

title: A Deep Neural Network Optimization Framework Based on Optimal Transport Bridge Feature Selection and Sparse Representation
authors: Guipeng Lan, Shuai Xiao, Jiabao Wen, Jiachen Yang, Wen Lu, Baihua Li, Qinggang Meng, Xinbo Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: The performance of deep neural networks (DNNs) in accomplishing tasks heavily relies on feature selection and sparse representation of high-dimensional data. Previous work has treated feature selection and sparse representation as separate mechanisms for improving DNNs performance, focusing on identifying and leveraging informative features to enhance task-specific outcomes. However, few studies have established a connection between feature selection and sparse representation. To address this gap, this article proposes an optimization framework termed informative sparse transport (IST), which integrates feature selection and sparse coding into a unified multiobjective optimization framework. Using optimal transport as a bridge, the IST framework harmonizes the relationship between feature selection and sparse representation, offering an informational advantage. In the IST framework, feature selection aims to identify an optimal subset of features to maximize mutual information or minimize redundancy, while sparse representation seeks to approximate data with the fewest possible features. Although these objectives differ, they are fundamentally complementary, as both emphasize extracting task-relevant information while eliminating redundancy. By unifying feature selection and sparse representation, the IST framework effectively mitigates challenges posed by high-dimensional data, delivering a robust solution for enhanced feature extraction and representation. We validate the IST framework on generative and classification tasks, demonstrating IST framework improves model performance through the complementary synergy of feature selection and sparse representation.

title: Confidence Matters: Balanced Pseudo-Label Selection for Semi-Supervised Few-Shot Learning
authors: Kunlei Jing, Hebo Ma, Chen Zhang, Ziyang Shang, Zhiyuan Zha, Bihan Wen
abstract: Semi-Supervised Few-Shot Learning (SSFSL) aims to classify categories given a few support samples and a number of unlabeled samples per class. Most existing methods focus on promoting the pseudo-labeling of unlabeled samples to boost SSFSL performance via more samples with confident pseudo-labels, which is inevitably limited by the scarcity of given support samples for pseudo-labeling training. We realize that confidence selection of pseudo-labeled samples is a promising alternative, for which both task-free generality and task-specific adaptability are crucial to guide accurate and fine-grained selection. To this end, we propose a two-step BalAnced pSeudo-label SelectiOn (BASSO) method that explores certainty entropy to characterize the confidence of pseudo-labels and learns the entropy distributions to guide selection. Specifically, step one rehearses the pseudo-labeling on large task-free samples to model the entropies of high-confidence and low-confidence pseudo-labels as prior general distributions. Step two devises a generator fed with task-specific prototypes to learn the adaptive distributions for each task. We further tailor balancing factors to balance the two types of distributions in guiding the selection during training episodes, ensuring both accurate and fine-grained selection. We perform confidence selection by casting the certainty entropies of pseudo-labeled samples into the balanced distributions and comparing their probabilities subject to high-confidence and low-confidence pseudo-labels, respectively. The comparison deviations denoting confidence levels are utilized to reweight the selected samples for later self-training. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks demonstrate that BASSO effectively balances selection accuracy and amount, showcasing competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods. Our code will be available at https://github.com/Klein-JING/BASSO.

title: NeuroHBM: A 3D-Integrated Neuromorphic Processing-in-Memory Architecture Using HBM
authors: Kyoyun Lee, Byeonghyeon Kim, Juyoung Oh, Jin Hee Cho, Dongsuk Jeon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Artificial Intelligence
abstract: The advancement of artificial intelligence and high-performance computing is reshaping industries and redefining technological standards. However, as machine learning models exponentially increase in complexity and data demands reach unprecedented levels, efficient and high-speed memory systems have become more critical than ever. While high bandwidth memory (HBM) serves as a bridging technology by providing both high throughput and large capacity, it cannot fully address the fundamental challenges posed by enormous data transfer volumes. In this work, we propose an efficient processing-inmemory architecture aimed at realizing neuromorphic computing on HBMs. By leveraging the residual space in the throughsilicon- via (TSV) area on the logic die, our design deploys leaky integrate-and-fire neurons and employs sparse interconnections for effective neuromorphic processing. This approach not only significantly reduces data transfer overhead and power consumption but also is fully compatible with state-of-the-art commercial HBMs. Experiments show that the proposed design achieves a high energy efficiency of 0.39 pJ per synaptic operation, significantly outperforming conventional GPU-based processing, without incurring any area overhead in HBM. These results highlight the potential for incorporating processing capabilities within HBM with minimal costs, paving the way for highly efficient neuromorphic computing.

title: Memristor-Based Directional Forgetting Neural Network Circuit With Emotion Memory and Its Application in Intelligent Robots
authors: Junwei Sun, Huiyan Liu, Yingcong Wang, Yanfeng Wang
abstract: Most neural networks based on memristor only consider the relationship between emotion and memory, but ignore the relationship between emotion and directional forgetting. This article presents a neural network circuit based on memristors, which can achieve targeted forgetting with self-correlation and emotional memory. The designed circuit is mainly composed of memory module, self-correlation module, amygdala module, emotion module, and association neuron module. Memory module can dynamically adjust the time for memory formation and the time for memory decay according to the requirements of the task. Association between learning materials and the characteristics of the individual can be achieved through the self-correlation module. The learning material signals are transformed into positive learning material signal and negative learning material signal through the amygdala module. Emotion module and the association neuron module are used to determine whether the emotionality of the learning materials is consistent with one's own emotionality. The feasibility of the function of the proposed circuit is verified through Simulation Program with integrated circuit emphasis. This circuit provides more references for the further development of brain-like intelligence.

title: Certification of Linear Inclusions for Nonlinear Systems
authors: Yehia Abdelsalam, Sebastian Engell
abstract: In this letter, we propose a novel method to certify whether a given set of vertex linear systems constitutes a linear difference inclusion for a nonlinear system. The method relies on formulating the verification of the inclusion as an optimization problem in a novel way. The sign of the optimal value of the proposed optimization problem determines whether the candidate set of vertices is a linear difference inclusion for the nonlinear dynamics or not. The result is a Yes/No certificate. Our method can be useful for reducing the conservatism of linear difference inclusions obtained by other conservative methods, or for providing a rigorous certification procedure for any possibly non-rigorous method that aims at finding a linear difference inclusion for a nonlinear system. We illustrate how the method can be useful in obtaining less conservative linear enclosures for nonlinear systems by using small numerical examples. We further demonstrate the applicability of our method to larger systems using an industry-relevant chemical reactor example.

title: Thin Magnetic Sensor for Angle Measurement in Folding Structures
authors: Boston McGarrahan, C. K. Harnett
abstract: Magnetic sensors are popular for rotation sensing in hinges and knobs because they do not require a mechanical or electrical connection between moving parts. However, the straightforward solution of spinning a permanent magnet about the rotation axis puts components directly on the hinge line, preventing its use in thin folded structures. Toward thin folding shells that can sense their own internal angles, this letter investigates a 1.5 mm thick flexible printed circuit board (PCB) design using a small permanent magnet and a magnetic vector sensor, with no components or PCB traces on the hinge line, covering an angular range of 10 to 360 degrees. Our method for calculating the hinge angle runs on a microcontroller and is insensitive to magnet strength, parametrized only by the PCB layout dimensions. The angle uncertainty is 5 degrees, limited chiefly by background from the Earth's magnetic field.

title: iFuzz-Meta: An Interpretable Fuzzy Learning Framework Bridging Top-Down and Bottom-Up Knowledge Integration_supp1-3683688.pdf
authors: Xiaowei Jiang
abstract: Interpretable representation learning remains a key challenge in modern neural computation, particularly when models are expected not only to perform but also to explain their reasoning. This paper introduces iFuzz-Meta, an inter pretable fuzzy rule-based learning framework that preserves human-understandable reasoning structures within modern neural architectures. Each fuzzy rule corresponds to a semantic and spatial prototype defined in the original feature space, enabling transparent inference and direct interpretability. Meta-learning is employed as an analytical paradigm to examine how these interpretable rules reorganize across tasks and domains, providing a principled means to link algorithmic adaptation with cognitive representation. A knowledge-guided regularization mechanism further enables a top-down–bottom-up integration, in which theoretical priors act as soft inductive biases while data-driven learning refines and extends them. This dual process ensures that adaptation proceeds along semantically and physiologically meaningful trajectories, rather than arbitrary parameter shifts. Evaluations demonstrate that iFuzz-Meta achieves interpretable reasoning and stable cross-domain generalization, establishing a potential general pathway toward explainable and knowledge aware fuzzy systems.

title: RUOD-R: A High-Fidelity Re-Annotated Benchmark for Underwater Object Detection
authors: Ali Awad, Ashraf Saleem, Yaman Aljnadi, Evan Lucas, Sidike Paheding, Timothy C. Havens
abstract: The reliability of object detection models fundamentally depends on training data quality, yet many benchmarks suffer from missing, confused, and inaccurate bounding boxes. This issue is particularly severe in the underwater domain, where visual degradations amplify annotation errors. The Real-world Underwater Object Detection (RUOD) dataset is a widely used benchmark that provides diverse real-world underwater scenes, but its annotations reflect the inherent difficulty of comprehensive annotation in such environments. To address this limitation, we present RUOD-R, a high-quality re-annotated version of RUOD developed using a rigorous protocol that combines image enhancement and quality-assured professional annotation. RUOD-R retains the original images, ensuring that performance differences stem solely from annotation quality. RUOD-R increases the total annotated instances by 3.5×, with small object annotations increasing by over 140×. Our comparative analysis reveals that many valid object instances, particularly small and visually degraded targets, were not annotated in the original dataset, and that matched boxes exhibit localization inaccuracy (mean IoU of 0.83 with RUOD-R).We evaluate detection performance using Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, YOLOv11, and DynYOLO, spanning general-purpose and underwater-domain-specific architectures, on both datasets. Results show that RUOD-R is a substantially more challenging benchmark due to the increased object density and the predominance of small, occluded, and visually degraded targets. A controlled evaluation on paired images where both annotation sets contain the same objects suggests that annotation density, rather than coordinate differences, is the primary factor behind the performance gap. A flip-rate analysis reveals that up to 38% of detections counted as false positives on the original dataset are correct detections of valid objects. RUOD-R thus provides a more reliable benchmark for advancing underwater object detection. The new annotations and related metadata can be found at https://github.com/RSSL-MTU/RUOD-R.
