title: Analysis and Synthesis of Fully Soft-Switching Flying-Capacitor based Quasi-Resonant DC–DC Converters
authors: Kumar Joy Nag, Aleksandar Prodić
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This article presents a generalized design methodology for a recently introduced class of soft-switching, flying-capacitor-based quasi-resonant (FC-QR) converters. This class achieves soft-switching across all transition edges, independent of the load and voltage conversion ratio (VCR) – highly desirable features for high-VCR and low-power supplies, where switching losses are dominant. Operating-condition-independent soft-switching is achieved by operating the flying capacitor (FC) in discontinuous voltage mode (DVM), i.e., allowing it a full voltage swing. At the same time, the inductor is operated in discontinuous conduction mode (DCM). This DVM–DCM operation is obtained through a specifically developed control scheme. These topologies achieve practically flat and high-efficiency curves that only drop slightly at very light loads. The foundation for the converter and controller synthesis and analysis is based on a developed set of generic assumptions (applicable for all dc–dc converters) and guidelines, covering the design of step-up/step-down, isolated/non-isolated, and single/multi-resonant topologies, as well as their complementary controllers. Consequently, the developed framework provides useful tools for the complete design and analysis of the entire class of FC-QR-based power supplies. Based on the developed guidelines, as a practical design example, an FC-QR boost converter is developed. The experimental prototype demonstrates class-leading peak efficiency of 88% for a 2–5 V input and a 30–60 V / 3 W output, in a small form factor, confirming the effectiveness of the introduced generalized synthesis and analysis framework.

title: TacMan-Turbo: Proactive Tactile Control for Robust and Efficient Articulated Object Manipulation_supp3-3683863.mp4
authors: Zihang Zhao
abstract: Adept manipulation of articulated objects is essential for robots to operate successfully in human environments. Such manipulation requires both effectiveness—reliable operation despite uncertain object structures—and efficiency—swift execution with minimal redundant steps and smooth trajectories. Existing approaches struggle to achieve both objectives simultaneously: methods relying on predefined kinematic models lack robustness when encountering structural variations, while the tactile-informed approach achieves robust manipulation but sacrifices efficiency through reactive, step-by-step execute-and-recover cycles. To address this challenge, this paper introduces TacMan-Turbo, a proactive tactile control framework that unifies the cycles into a continuous control loop. Our key insight is to interpret tactile signals temporally in addition to spatially: instead of treating contact deviations merely as instantaneous error signals requiring immediate compensation, we analyze sequential tactile observations to reveal local kinematic information. This temporal perspective enables our controller to predict future object states and proactively modulate manipulation velocities, eliminating the previous execute-and-recover cycle. In evaluations across 200 diverse simulated articulated objects and real-world experiments, our approach maintains a near-perfect performance while significantly enhancing time efficiency, action efficiency, and trajectory smoothness (all p-values < 0.0001). These results demonstrate that tactile feedback serves a dual purpose: providing not only spatial error signals for reactive control but also critical temporal structural information that enables proactive prediction and control. By enabling robots to manipulate articulated objects both reliably and efficiently, this work advances robot capabilities for seamless operation in dynamic, human-centric environments.

title: Metamaterial-Based Structures for Mutual Coupling Reduction Technique and Performance Enhancement in 5G Smartphone MIMO Antennas: A Systematic Review
authors: Noptin Harpawi, Muzammil Jusoh, Mohamed Nasrun Osman, Emansa Hasri Putra, Thennarasan Sabapathy, Akram Alomainy
abstract: Metamaterial based approaches have emerged as promising solutions for mitigating mutual coupling in compact multiple input multiple output antenna systems operating at fifth generation sub-6 GHz frequencies for smartphone devices. This systematic literature review analyzes some peer-reviewed publications published between 2020 and 2025 sourced from the Scopus database to evaluate the effectiveness of metamaterial strategies in reducing mutual coupling between closely spaced antenna elements. A structured search methodology employing the PRISMA framework identified studies focusing exclusively on sub-6 GHz implementations in handheld platforms, excluding base-station or millimeter-wave applications. Technical design analysis examined antenna configurations employing diverse metamaterial types, including split ring resonator, complementary split ring resonator, frequency selective surface, and electromagnetic bandgap structure. Results demonstrated that metamaterial-enhanced MIMO systems consistently achieved isolation levels ranging from 12 to 65 dB, envelope correlation coefficient values below 0.1, and radiation efficiency improvements exceeding 60%. Split ring resonators dominated implementations at 38.1%, while frequency selective surfaces provided dual benefits of isolation enhancement and 1.5 to 2.4 dB gain improvement. Advanced configurations combining parasitic metamaterials with metamaterial superstrates achieved 52.94% antenna miniaturization while maintaining performance specifications. Our synthesis reveals that while SRR and CSRR provide excellent compactness suitable for dense handset chassis environments, FSS and EBG structures offer superior broad-band isolation but often face spatial integration challenges due to their larger unit cell requirements. Consequently, from the authors' perspective, exploration of reconfigurable metamaterial structures, and machine learning-assisted design optimization as critical areas for future research advancement.

title: PI-YOLO: An Efficient Detector for Power Line Inspection using Aerial Images
authors: Di Jiang, Yuan Cao, Qiang Yang
abstract: Power line inspection, as a critical task of smart grid maintenance, has been empowered by Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) and neural detectors for automation and intelligence. The inspection task requires detectors to identify diverse equipment in high-resolution aerial images rapidly and accurately. However, existing detectors are still insufficient to meet these requirements, primarily due to their inadequate dataset scale, high-resolution adaptability, and network efficiency. To address these challenges, this paper first collects 99202 inspection images with a resolution of 4939×2987 and annotates 52 categories, thereby establishing the Power Inspection Unify Dataset (PIUD) as the data foundation. Then, PIUD is further processed by the proposed Power Inspection Multi-Scale (PIMS) framework to PIUD-MS to support high-resolution training and inference. Finally, this paper develops PI-YOLO based on YOLOv11 with improved performance in terms of multi-scale capability, network structure, and receptive field. PI-YOLO is publicly released as an efficient detector for inspection or annotation applications, whose advanced performance is validated by experiments on COCO, PIUD-MS, and InsPLAD. Compared with the baseline YOLOv11 and other detectors, PI-YOLO achieves both performance improvement and latency reduction. For example, PI-YOLO-Large improves AP by 0.7 while reducing latency by 1ms on PIUD-MS compared to YOLOv11.

title: Improving Satellite Imagery Segmentation Using Multiple Sentinel-2 Revisits
authors: Kartik Jindgar, Grace W. Lindsay
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In recent years, analysis of remote sensing data has benefited immensely from borrowing techniques from the broader field of computer vision, including the use of shared models pre-trained on large datasets. However, satellite imagery has unique features that are not accounted for in traditional computer vision, such as the existence of multiple revisits of the same location. Here, we explore the best way to use revisits in the framework of fine-tuning pre-trained remote sensing models. As a test case, we focus on the applied research question of power substation segmentation, which is representative of applied uses of pre-trained models more generally. Through extensive tests of different multi-temporal input schemes across diverse model architectures, we find that fusing representations from multiple revisits in the model's latent space is superior to other methods of using revisits, including as a form of data augmentation. We also find that a SWIN transformer-based architecture performs better than U-Nets and ViT-based models. We verify the generality of our results on a separate building density estimation task. In total, this work equips remote sensing scientists with a general method for utilizing the unique temporal properties of their images.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: A Distributed Plug-and-Play MCMC Algorithm for High-Dimensional Inverse Problems
authors: Maxime Bouton, Pierre-Antoine Thouvenin, Audrey Repetti, Pierre Chainais
abstract: Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms are standard approaches to solve imaging inverse problems and quantify estimation uncertainties, a key requirement in absence of ground-truth data. To improve estimation quality, Plug-and- Play MCMC algorithms, such as PnP-ULA, have been recently developed to accommodate priors encoded by a denoising neural network. Designing scalable samplers for high-dimensional imaging inverse problems remains a challenge: drawing and storing high-dimensional samples can be prohibitive, especially for highresolution images. To address this issue, this work proposes a distributed sampler based on approximate data augmentation and PnP-ULA to solve very large problems. The proposed sampler uses lightweight denoising convolutional neural network, to efficiently exploit multiple GPUs on a Single Program Multiple Data architecture. Reconstruction performance and scalability are evaluated on several imaging problems. Communication and computation overheads due to the denoiser are carefully discussed. The proposed distributed approach noticeably combines three very precious qualities: it is scalable, enables uncertainty quantification, for a reconstruction performance comparable to other PnP methods.

title: NASA Multi-Frequency Radar for Hydrometeor Phase Identification
authors: Liang Liao, Robert Meneghini, Gerald M. Heymsfield, Hyokyung Kim, Mei Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: This study investigates the potential of dual/multi-frequency Doppler radar for hydrometeor phase classification and develops a phase identification algorithm using NASA airborne radar observations. Theoretical model simulations confirm a clear distinction in both Doppler velocities (V) and dual-frequency Doppler velocities (DDV)—the difference in V at two radar frequencies—between snow and rain hydrometeors. This distinction provides a useful feature for phase identification. A statistical analysis of V and DDV was performed using airborne Doppler measurements from NASA's multi-frequency (X-, Ku-, Ka-, and W-band) radar collected during the IMPACTS field campaign. Results show that snow can be separated from rain using either V or DDV. However, the Linear Depolarization Ratio (LDR) data are required to differentiate snow/rain from mixedphase (or melting) hydrometeors, as Doppler data alone are insufficient. While the differences in V between snow and rain were found to be larger than in DDV, the air motion independence of DDV is a key advantage. Therefore, a technique leveraging both V and DDV is developed. By combining V, DDV, and LDR, this algorithm successfully classifies hydrometeor phases using the NASA airborne radar dataset.

title: Diffusion Distillation for Remote Sensing Salient Object Detection
authors: Ziran Gao, Huilan Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Salient object detection (SOD) in optical remote sensing images (ORSIs) is challenging due to limited spatial details, large scale variation, and cluttered backgrounds with strong look-alike interference. Although diffusion models can progressively refine object structures and boundaries, their multi-step sampling introduces high latency, which limits practical deployment over large areas. This paper proposes SGDDiff (Semantic-Guided Diffusion Distillation Network), which distills a high-capacity feature-space diffusion teacher into a compact student, thereby retaining diffusion-style refinement while enabling efficient inference. The student is trained under the supervision of a training-only pixel-semantic guidance mechanism and intermediate decoder feature alignment. To strengthen representation with low computational overhead, we further develop a lightweight VD encoder (VGRNN–DyT encoder), which integrates a VGRNN-based refinement unit with a DyT-enhanced Swin-style context block, where the VGRNN incorporates Vision Mamba-based spatial modeling and is further enhanced by a Gated Depthwise Block (GDB). A two-stage training pipeline is adopted, including encoder–decoder pretraining and feature-aligned diffusion distillation. Extensive experiments on multiple ORSI-SOD benchmarks demonstrate that SGDDiff achieves state-of-the-art performance among 18 competing methods while substantially reducing computational overhead, yielding a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off. Code and models are available at https://github.com/zizizirrr/SGDDIff.

title: List of Reviewers
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)

title: Investigating Multi-Channel Performance in Mesh Wireless Networks-on-Chip Design
authors: Asrani Lit, Rohana Sapawi, Nordiana Rajaee, Shamsiah Suhaili, Nur Alia Athirah Mohtadzar, Mohammed Sultan Mohammed
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The increasing complexity and performance demands of many-core systems have brought Networks-on-Chip (NoC) to the forefront of scalable interconnect solutions. Among these, wireless NoC (WNoC) offer promising advantages in latency and bandwidth by integrating wireless links into traditional mesh topologies. This paper investigates the performance impact of incorporating multiple wireless channels in a meshbased 64-core Wireless Network-on-Chip (WNoC) design under random traffic patterns. A cycle-accurate simulation using the Noxim framework evaluates three wireless transceiver subnet clustering strategies, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{WNoC}_{\text{subnet1 }}, \text{WNoC}_{\text{subnet4 }}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{WNoC}_{\text{subnet9 }}$</tex> across one to four wireless channels. The results demonstrate that multi-channel configurations can substantially reduce network latency and improve throughput, with the most significant gains observed when increasing from one to two channels. Among the architectures, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{WNoC}_{\text{subnet1 }}$</tex> consistently achieves the most stable and efficient performance, while <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{WNoC}_{\text{subnet9 }}$</tex> shows the highest responsiveness to increased channel count, achieving up to 10.49 flits/cycle throughput. Furthermore, the analysis of wireless channel utilization reveals that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{WNoC}_{\text{subnet9 }}$</tex> best exploits available bandwidth, indicating its suitability for bandwidth-intensive applications. These findings underscore the importance of tailored channel allocation and architecture-aware design for optimizing multi-channel WNoC performance in future manycore systems.

title: Multi-Stage Machine Learning Model for Classification and Prediction of Learners
authors: Toshiki Tsuji, Tomohiro Hayashida, Kento Tsutsumi, Shin Wakitani, Takuya Kinoshita, Shinya Sekizaki
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: In the one-device-per-student environment promoted by the GIGA School Initiative, personalized learning support utilizing learning history data has become increasingly important. Previous studies, such as those by Keser et al.,(2022) demonstrated the effectiveness of hybrid models using XGBoost for grade prediction, while Hirotani et al.,(2023), and Hirotani (2024) proposed a method to classify learners based on predictability, successfully identifying students who are difficult to predict. Although these studies highlight the utility of predictive modeling, conventional approaches face limitations when applied to unseen data, as they rely on actual performance values. To overcome this limitation, the present study proposes a multi-stage machine learning model that classifies learners without depending on actual performance values, thereby enabling predictions even for unseen learners. Specifically, the model integrates multiple classifiers and predictors, including Support Vector Regression (SVR) and Random Forest (RF), within a multi-stage framework. This design allows the selection of prediction models tailored to individual learner characteristics, improving predictive accuracy. Furthermore, the use of SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values enhances the interpretability of the model by quantifying the contribution of each feature to prediction outcomes, thereby offering insights that can support educational practice and feature selection. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through simulations using real-world data from junior high school students. Results demonstrate that the approach achieves high predictive accuracy without relying on actual performance values, while also identifying key features that influence academic achievement. This study provides not only a predictive framework but also an interpretable tool to support the realization of personalized learning environments.

title: Case Study: Improving the Robustness of Photonic Neural Networks Against Memory Faults
authors: Mauricio Gomes de Queiroz, Leonardo Alexandrino de Melo, Mateus Vidaletti Costa, Salvatore Pappalardo, Fabio Pavanello, Ian O'Connor, Alberto Bosio
abstract: Photonic Neural Networks (PNNs) still depend on electronic components for tasks such as memory storage and control, as a result, they are susceptible to faults that can compromise model robustness. In this study, we present the first robustness analysis of PNNs with respect to memory faults in their stored parameters. To mitigate these issues, we propose a hardening solution with zero memory overhead based on Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) that leads to increased accuracy under faulty conditions.

title: Does LLM Generated Code Smell?
authors: Debalina Ghosh Paul, Hong Zhu, Ian Bayley
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to generate program code. Much research has been reported on the functional correctness of generated code, but there is far less on code quality. In this study, we evaluate the code smells, an important indicator of code quality, of the Java programs generated in response to prompts given to four state-of-the-art LLMs: Gemini Pro, ChatGPT, Codex, and Falcon. We find that LLM-generated code has a higher incidence of code smells compared to reference solutions and this number increases with coding task complexity. We also find that the types of code smells vary with both programming topic and the choice of model. Falcon performed best, followed by Gemini Pro and ChatGPT. Codex performed worst, increasing by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 9 \%}$</tex> the average number of violations of coding rules. The quality of LLM-generated code tends to be worse in advanced topics such as encapsulation, abstract class and generics, polymorphism and inheritance, etc. The worst types of code smells in the baseline were also the worst in the LLM-generated code. On average across all LLMs, the implementation smells increase by 81 % and design smells by 64 %.

title: A Comprehensive Methodology for Synthesis of Cylindrical Leaky-Wave Metasurface Antennas
authors: Davorin Mikulić, Thibault Boisseau, Maxime Harnois, Marko Bosiljevac, Zvonimir Šipuš
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Numerous challenges are present in the design and realization of curved leaky-wave metasurface antennas, such as uncontrolled radiation of creeping waves, the requirement for amplitude and phase correction of excited leaky waves, and practical issues related to metallization of complex metasurface patterns on flexible substrates. Therefore, the aim of this paper is to offer a comprehensive methodology for the synthesis of such metasurface antennas on cylindrical substrates, covering analytical tools, design procedure, and fabrication method. As a first step, the theoretical foundation of creeping wave propagation and analytical methodology for extracting their properties on cylindrical metasurfaces is developed. Further, a method for retrieving the optimal metasurface reactance profile is discussed, which is needed to obtain the desired radiation properties from the curved structure. Finally, two cylindrical leaky-wave antenna designs with pencil-beam radiation pattern are developed based on the proposed methodology. The prototypes of the antennas were fabricated using a method called screen-printing, which was essential for realizing the metallization of designed metasurface patterns on flexible low-loss substrate. The antennas are experimentally characterized, and the measured results are compared with theoretical predictions, demonstrating strong agreement and validating the effectiveness of the proposed synthesis approach.

title: Machine Learning and Network Analysis for Cross‑Border Suspicious Transaction Detection: Evidence from the FinCEN Files
authors: Oghenefejiro M. Ejime, Abiola Kosemani, Shedrack Isioma Egede Onyeorueka
abstract: Traditional rule‑based anti‑money‑laundering (AML) systems raise far more alerts than humans can plausibly investigate. In the aftermath of the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists’ FinCEN Files revelations, compliance teams and regulators have begun to ask whether a data‑driven approach might allocate scarce attention more effectively. This study presents a holistic framework that combines descriptive statistics, time‑series modelling, geospatial analysis, network science and machine‑learning classification to analyse 4,507 aggregated transaction groups (more than 200,000 individual transaction records) from the FinCEN Files dataset. After cleaning and integrating the data, we explore the heavy‑tailed distribution of suspicious amounts, identify temporal spikes linked to economic crises, map cross‑border corridors between secrecy jurisdictions, quantify centrality in the bank‑to‑bank network, fit an ARIMA model to monthly volumes, and construct a risk‑scoring system. Logistic regression, Random Forest and XGBoost models are benchmarked against unsupervised isolation and one‑class support vector methods. Ensemble models achieve area‑under‑the‑ROC‑curve scores around 0.97–0.98, substantially outperforming rule‑based or unsupervised baselines. Our results demonstrate that network metrics, particularly degree and betweenness centrality—coupled with high‑risk corridor indicators are among the most important predictors of suspicious behaviour. We conclude by situating these findings within the emerging literature on graph neural networks and proposing avenues for continual learning, beneficial ownership integration and cross‑institutional transferability. Overall, the work illustrates that a judicious blend of machine learning and network analysis can reduce false positives and support risk‑based AML supervision.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Insertion-Withdrawal, Contact Behavior and Temperature Rise of a Cantilevered Reed Type Electrical Connector
authors: Jiang Bian, Wujun Wang, Zhanshu He
abstract: To achieve the smallest insertion-withdrawal force, the most reliable contact behavior and the lowest temperature rise simultaneously, the insertion-withdrawal, contact behavior and the temperature rise of a cantilever reed type electrical connector are studied through a combined method of theory, simulation, and experiment. First, establish theoretical models for insertion-withdrawal force, contact force, contact resistance, heat generation, and heat dissipation. Subsequently, obtain the transient change laws of insertion-withdrawal force, contact force, and temperature rise. Then, analyze the influence of the electrical connector's structural parameters on the insertion-withdrawal force and contact force. Finally, analyze the influence of wire cross-sectional area and current magnitude on the temperature rise. The results show that the accuracy of the theoretical models of the insertion-withdrawal force and the contact force and the simulation model of temperature rise are validated. The insertion force at the end of the initial insertion stage is the maximum insertion force, while the withdrawal force during the steady withdrawal stage is the maximum withdrawal force. The maximum insertion-withdrawal force and the maximum contact force increase as the initial inclination angle of the reed α0 and the distance from the initial contact point to the highest point H3 increase, and decrease as the gap H2 between the male terminal and the female terminal increases. The maximum insertion-withdrawal force increases with the increase of the friction coefficient μ. The maximum temperature rise of the electrical connector increases as the current rises and decreases as the wire cross-sectional area increases. The theoretical models for insertion-withdrawal force, contact force, contact resistance, heat generation, and heat dissipation can be extended to other type electrical connectors. The combined method of theory, simulation, and experiment can provide effective guidance for the structural optimization of electrical connectors.

title: Dual-Path Hybrid Network for Boundary-Aware Segmentation and Angle of Progression Measurement in Intrapartum Ultrasound
authors: Zhensen Chen, Yaosheng Lu, Ziduo Yang, Xiwen Zhang, Lei Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Accurate segmentation of the fetal head (FH) and pubic symphysis (PS) in intrapartum ultrasound (IU) images is a crucial step for automatic angle of progression (AoP) measurement, which plays an essential role in predicting delivery outcomes and reducing maternal and fetal complications. Existing CNN–Transformer hybrid models often suffer from attention collapse due to limited medical data and tend to overlook boundary details that are critical yet highly degraded by artifacts and noise in IU images. To address these issues, we propose a CNN-stylized dual-path CNN–Transformer hybrid network tailored for IU segmentation. The encoder integrates a parallel CNN branch and a CNN-stylized Transformer branch to balance local feature extraction and global dependency modeling while mitigating attention collapse. A Transformer-to-CNN fusion module further enhances cross-branch information interaction. In the decoder, a Boundary Attention Residual Module captures subtle foreground-background transitions and progressively refines boundary features. In addition, an Adaptive Boundary Enhancement strategy is designed to emphasize challenging boundary regions during CutMix augmentation. Experiments on three datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both overall accuracy and boundary precision. The automatic AoP measurement analysis further validates its potential for clinical translation. Code is available at https://github.com/SakuraKong/Dual-Path-CNN-Stylized-Hybrid-Network.

title: E 2 -Former: An Edge-Enhanced Transformer for UAV-Based Small Object Detection
authors: Jie Zhang, Yao Zhang, Said M. Easa, Boxiang Xie, Lingfeng Lin, Xiuzhuang Zhou, Nianyin Zeng, Wei Zhang, Mengmeng Song
abstract: The widespread deployment of low-altitude drones in Internet of Things (IoT) applications, such as smart transportation and urban security, demands efficient, real-time visual perception on resource-constrained edge devices. A critical challenge in this domain is the precise detection and localization of small objects against complex backgrounds. Low-altitude UAV imagery is particularly challenging due to blurred small-target edges, weak anti-interference for medium and large targets, and fragmented multi-scale features. Existing convolutional and general-purpose Transformer-based detectors exhibit significant limitations in localization accuracy and robustness under these conditions. To address these issues, we propose E2-Former, a novel object detection model based on the Detection Transformer (DETR) framework, designed for real-time performance on computational-edge devices. Our model integrates three core components: an Edge-Enhanced Backbone that augments sensitivity to contours while preserving deep semantics; a Polarized Dynamic Multi-Feature Fusion (PDMF) Transformer that leverages dual-path polarized attention and frequency-domain modulation to enhance local-global feature modeling and suppress background noise; and an Edge-aware Path Aggregation Network (E-PAN) that uses bidirectional gating and multi-level context interaction to resolve feature fragmentation and promote fine-grained, cross-scale integration. On three challenging low-altitude datasets—VisDrone2019, UAVDT, and CODrone—E2- Former achieves leading AP50 scores of 48.9%, 41.2%, and 33.0%, respectively, outperforming existing mainstream methods in both detection accuracy and robustness. Practical deployment and scene detection experiments further validate its impressive performance in real-world scenarios, establishing a new paradigm for building efficient and reliable low-altitude drone perception systems.

title: Trip Detection Algorithms for Healthy and Amputee Individuals
authors: Eugenio Anselmino, Tommaso Ciapetti, Michele Piazzini, Claudio Macchi, Alberto Mazzoni, Silvestro Micera, Arturo Forner-Cordero
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Amputation disrupts normal gait biomechanics, reducing strength, balance, and toe clearance. These factors contribute to an increase in falls in amputees, leading to both physical injury and psychological burden. Powered and microprocessor prosthetic legs can potentially react to external perturbations, but fast and reliable perturbation detection algorithms are needed. We developed three different algorithms for trip detection, each based on a different approach: thresholds, machine learning, or anomaly detection. Each trip detection method was validated offline with data acquired from 17 healthy subjects, 7 transtibial amputees, and 1 transfemoral amputee. Data was acquired using a modular tripping-eliciting platform based on multiple compliant trip blocks that deliver unanticipated tripping perturbations. All the detection approaches achieved satisfactory performances, with median sensitivities above 80% and specificities above 90%, depending on the sensors and feature combination used. Additionally, the algorithms were able to retain the same level of accuracy on the amputees dataset when trained on the healthy data only, suggesting a high generalization capability. The proposed trip detection algorithms demonstrated implementability under realistic operating conditions, achieving detection times below 150 ms. Among them, the threshold algorithm achieved the fastest detection, with an average time of approximately 40 ms. Overall, all the tested approaches proved to be viable solutions for the real-time detection of trip perturbations, with fast detection and high specificity. Additionally, the results of this work suggest a high generalization capability across healthy and amputees.

title: LADNet: A Lightweight Axial-Depthwise Network for Phase Unwrapping and Fading Suppression in ϕ-OTDR
authors: Zekai Li, Hao Zhou, Wen Zuo, Fan Yang, Yaojun Qiao
abstract: In this paper, we propose a lightweight axial-depthwise network (LADNet) to address interference fading and large-dynamic-range phase wrapping in phase-sensitive optical time-domain reflectometry (ϕ-OTDR). The lightweight design leverages axis-specific patterns and local spatial correlation in ϕ-OTDR waterfall data, and captures these features using axial depthwise convolutions. We design a U-Net architecture that integrates multi-branch axial depthwise convolutions and attention mechanisms to capture the anisotropic spatiotemporal correlations of ϕ-OTDR signals. To construct paired data for training and evaluation, we generate noisy wrapped-phase samples with fading and corresponding unwrapped-phase references using a physics-driven ϕ-OTDR simulator.On simulated test data (strain range 0–500 nε), LADNet achieves a strain MAE of 1.3 nε with only 1.70 M parameters. Experiments on a 5.6-km ϕ-OTDR system further verify robust phase recovery under large-amplitude vibration, yielding an SNR of 32.2 dB and a strong linear relationship between the recovered phase and the PZT driving voltage (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9974). Overall, the proposed method provides an effective solution for phase demodulation in ϕ-OTDR under large amplitude vibration conditions.

title: An Inertial and Array-Pressure Multimodal Fusion Sensor System for Robust Zero-Velocity Detection in Pedestrian Navigation
authors: Haonan Jia, Baoguo Yu, Hongsheng Li, Shuguo Pan, Denghui Du
abstract: Accurate counting of zero-velocity intervals is a critical issue for foot-mounted inertial sensor-based pedestrian navigation, which relies on zero-velocity corrections. In complex motion patterns, relying solely on inertial sensors for foot zero-velocity detection often leads to misdetections and missed detections. To address these challenges, we have developed a low-cost, intelligent, flexible insole sensor system and propose a multi-modal fusion algorithm for zero-velocity detection based on an array of pressure and inertial information. First, a Generalized Likelihood Ratio Test (GLRT)-based inertial information zero-velocity detection algorithm is applied for initial "coarse discrimination." Then, an innovative approach is introduced, where the distance between the foot pressure center and the zero-velocity region center is used for "fine discrimination" of zero-velocity detection. Furthermore, a multi-modal deep learning ensemble network model is designed to fuse the 6D inertial data and 8D foot pressure array data, outputting pedestrian motion patterns to adaptively adjust the detection threshold. Finally, experiments on pedestrian zero-velocity detection and positioning under complex motion patterns demonstrate that the classification accuracy of pedestrian motion modes reaches 99.3%, and the zero-velocity interval counting accuracy achieves 99.9%. Compared to the current state-of-the-art zero-velocity detectors, the accuracy has improved by at least 2.95%, and the 3D positioning error over continuous complex motion patterns lasting more than 30 minutes is reduced by at least 47.4%.

title: Response Surface-Based Optimization of Plasma-Activated Water Generated by Air Multipin-to-Water Discharge for Sustainable Microalgae Cultivation_supp1-3680788.pdf
authors: Khanit Matra
abstract: -Plasma-activated water (PAW) generated by an air multipin-to-water discharge operated in abnormal glow-like discharge mode was evaluated as a chemically tunable medium for microalgae cultivation. Response surface methodology (RSM) with a central composite design (CCD) quantified the effects of the electrode discharge gap (DG) (0.51.5 cm) and reaction time (15 min) on temperature, pH, oxidationreduction potential (ORP), electrical conductivity (EC), and reactive nitrogen species (RNSs) (NO2N and NO3N). Across 13 runs, pH decreased from 6.11 to 2.50, ORP increased from 33.8 to 234.7 mV, EC varied from 737 to 1274 $\mu $ S/cm, NO2N ranged from 2.83 to 69.78 $\mu $ M, and NO3N ranged from 13.36 to 32.75 $\mu $ M, while temperature remained within $30.1~^{\circ }$ C– $42.6~^{\circ }$ C. Reaction time primarily controlled acidification and ORP, whereas nonlinear and interaction effects contributed to the formation of nitrogen species. Freshly generated PAW was used immediately after generation and was subsequently diluted 1:1 with tap water (TW) before cultivation; in selected treatments, the diluted PAW was further combined with nitrogen-free BG-11 medium. Growth of Chlorella vulgaris over 12 days was assessed by chlorophyll-a content and cell density. PAW alone supported limited growth, whereas PAW combined with nitrogen-free BG-11 promoted higher growth under intermediate PAW conditions, while highly acidic PAW (pH <3) was unsuitable for direct cultivation. The results demonstrate a controllable PAW window for microalgal bioprocessing.

title: Task-Oriented Predict-then-Optimize Framework for Channel Prediction and Handover Decision
authors: Jia Miao, Li Zhu, Hongli Zhao, Yin Jia, Jinyu Fu
abstract: Reliable and real-time communication is essential for train autonomous control systems, yet ensuring seamless handover remains challenging in urban rail environments with rapidly varying channels and short overlap regions. To improve handover reliability, recent studies have introduced data-driven channel prediction models to anticipate signal variations and support proactive decision-making. However, these prediction-based methods are primarily evaluated using signal-level metrics—such as the signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) and reference signal received power (RSRP) prediction errors—which do not necessarily align with system-level objectives, including handover success rate and interruption time. This mismatch between prediction metrics and operational objectives leads to suboptimal decision outcomes. To address this gap, this paper proposes a Task-Oriented Predict–then–Optimize (TPO) framework that couples channel prediction and handover decision within a unified, differentiable learning pipeline. An Enhanced Informer predictor captures long-term temporal and boundary-aware signal dynamics, while an optimization-based solver generates handover actions under hysteresis and outage constraints. A learnable surrogate network bridges the non-differentiable task losses with gradient-based optimization, aligning forecasting behavior with decision quality. Simulations on urban rail scenarios demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces handover latency and interruption time while improving overall communication stability.

title: Anchor-SAM: Active Mining of Latent Anchors from SAM Encoder for Road Extraction
authors: Wenhai Li, Xiaohui Huang, Xiaofei Yang, Yicong Zhou, Jiangtao Peng, Yifang Ban, Nan Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Road extraction in high-resolution remote sensing imagery remains a persistent challenge due to occlusions and complex backgrounds, which lead to fragmented road topologies. However, existing road extraction methods constrained by limited pre-training remote sensing data often lack the generalization capability to distinguish roads from complex backgrounds. To address this issue, we propose Anchor-SAM, a novel framework that actively mines latent semantic anchors embedded in the SAM encoder to guide topological reconstruction. Our approach stems from a pivotal insight: the SAM encoder is able to abstract complex scenes into sparse semantic anchors at deep layers, thereby implicitly encoding the global structural skeleton. To harness these implicit cues, we introduce the Multi-scale Deformable Context Perceiver (MDCP) and the Deformable Bayesian Conditional Interaction Module (DBCIM). The MDCP explicitly utilizes spatial cues to aggregate global semantics across distributed anchors, establishing a robust initial context for the decoder. The DBCIM facilitates the diffusion of semantic cues to surrounding regions and effectively suppresses noise. Specifically, by leveraging the semantic certainty of anchors to guide deformable sampling trajectories, this mechanism proactively filters out background regions while precisely repairing fragmented road topologies. Our method achieves competitive performance on both the DeepGlobe and Massachusetts datasets. The source code will be publicly available at https://github.com/Hmbb0606/Anchor-SAM.

title: Cross-Phase Knowledge Transfer via Variable Space Alignment for Expensive Incremental Optimization_supp1-3684126.pdf
authors: Yuanchao Liu
abstract: One main feature of expensive incremental optimization problems (EIOPs) is the involvement of multiple costly related design phases. Based on the characteristics of EIOPs, solutions from previous design phases can be beneficial to find the optimum for the present design phase. However, extracting and reusing previous solutions is still a challenging task when solving EIOPs. To this end, this work proposes a surrogate-assisted differential evolution with variable space alignment (SADE-VSA) to effectively solve EIOPs. In SADE-VSA, a surrogate-assisted transfer strategy based on Wasserstein distance (SA-TWD) is first proposed to measure the similarity between different design phases. This strategy enables the pre-screening of high-quality solutions from previous design phases that exhibit high correlation with the present phase. Second, a surrogate-assisted variable space alignment (SA-VSA) strategy is proposed to align the variable space between previous and present design phases. Following this alignment, the highest quality solution is selected for transfer, which can help speed up the convergence of the algorithm. In order to verify the performance of the proposed SADE-VSA in solving EIOPs, six state-of-the-art methods are compared. The experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed SADE-VSA in solving EIOPs.

title: An FPGA-Efficient CNN Accelerator for Hybrid Model Compression With Scalable Bit-Serial and Bit-Parallel MAC
authors: Yuanmiao Lin, Zijian Zhang, Xueming Li, Hongmin Huang, Heng Mai, Ruidian Zhan, Xianghong Hu, Shuting Cai, Xiaoming Xiong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Mixed-precision quantization and unstructured pruning have emerged as two effective compression techniques, demonstrating great potential in reducing model size and computational cost in the deployment of convolutional neural networks (CNNs). However, their joint deployment still faces two major challenges: 1) The former introduces heterogeneous bit-widths in the bit-level, while the latter results in irregular sparsity in the value-level; their fundamentally incompatible data representations and computation patterns require two distinct types of hardware overhead to process them separately, which severely limits hardware execution efficiency. 2) Jointly applying both techniques often leads to notable accuracy degradation. In this paper, we propose a novel compression perspective that reinterprets zero-values generated by unstructured pruning as multiple consecutive 0-bits. We further introduce column-based bit-level sparsity, which provides a unified representation for weights after mixed-precision quantization and unstructured pruning, requiring only a single type of hardware overhead. Based on these techniques, we develop a hybrid compression framework that jointly optimizes model size, accuracy, and hardware implementation. Our method achieves weight/activation precision of 2.13b/4.06b on VGG16, delivering 7.40<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> compression and 2.74<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> speedup with 0.92% accuracy loss compared to the 8b baseline. Compared to state-of-the-art accelerators, our design achieves 1.12<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-6.23<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> and 1.31<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-6.60<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> improvements in energy efficiency and LUT efficiency when deploying VGG16 and ResNet50.

title: Dual-Mode Equilateral Triangular Resonator With Slit for Material Sensing
authors: Hamidreza Laribi, Ali-Reza Moznebi, Kambiz Moez, Rashid Mirzavand
abstract: This paper presents a dual-mode equilateral triangular resonator (DETR) sensor with a slit, which is theoretically analyzed and experimentally validated. In line with the theoretical analysis, the dual modes are the result of the rotation and superposition of the fundamental mode. A circuit model was developed tovalidate the theoretical analysis and good agreement was observed through a comprehensive comparison of results from full-wave simulations, theoretical calculations, and circuit model predictions. The proposed DETR operates in the industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) band (902-928 MHz) and offers a unique communication link (or calibration) capability, making it a promising candidate for various applications. As a proof-of-concept, a prototype was designed, fabricated, and experimentally measured that confirmed the reliability of the sensor for material characterization. The results show that the higher frequency remains unaffected by distance changes between the sensor and receiver, whereas the lower frequency exhibits robust responses to various materials.

title: Transmitter Placement Optimization Using 3D Ray Tracing and PSO in Metallic Environments
authors: Jonghee Kim, Minso Park, Youngchul Lee, Chi-Hyung Ahn, Seongwon Oh
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This paper proposes a transmitter (Tx) placement optimization method in metallic environments by combining the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with 3D ray tracingbased electromagnetic simulation. In particular, the study quantitatively analyzes the impact of the presence or absence of indoor objects on wireless propagation characteristics and optimal Tx placement. Simulations were conducted across four scenarios modeled on an actual factory layout using a detailed 3D environment. The results show that the presence of indoor objects affects signal quality and shadowing, and induces approximately 1 meter variation in the optimal Tx location. Based on these findings, this paper proposes a twostep optimization strategy to improve both computational efficiency and reliability. First, an initial PSO is performed in a simplified environment without objects to determine candidate Tx positions. Then, a localized re-optimization is conducted around the candidate area in a more complex environment that includes indoor structures. Since this method performs re-optimization only in the local region when the internal structure of the factory changes, it can search for the optimal Tx position with significantly reduced computational cost. This research provides a practical and scalable solution for achieving optimal Tx deployment in complex industrial indoor environments.

title: Optimization of Prediction Model for the Determination of Free Fatty Acid for Crude Palm Oil
authors: Katrul Nadia Basri, Nur Aisyah Athirah Mohd Zali, Mohd Hafizulfika Hisyam, Muhammad Syargawi Abdullah, Nur Azera Tuhaime, Zalhan Md Yusof, Mohd Norzaliman Mohd Zain
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Near infrared (NIR) spectroscopy is a reliable method to be adopted for in-line measurement in process analytical technology (PAT) in the agriculture industry due to the simplistic procedure, rapid result, and non-destructive method. NIR required chemometrics analysis to extract the information of the spectra to determine the free fatty acid (FFA) value of crude palm CPO. The standard titration method based on the Malaysian Palm Oil Board (MPOB) test method was utilized to determine the FFA value of the CPO sample as the reference value. The NIR spectra with a total of 211 were obtained through the inline measurement during the flow of CPO in the pipeline at the mill. Sample selection was performed by the sample distance calculation and 190 samples were retained. Feature selection using iPLS was applied to the data and the number of variables chosen was 140. Different types of regression algorithms (PLS, MLR, SVM, and XGBoost) were applied to build the prediction model. SVM has the optimum performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{P}$</tex> and RMSEP values of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 8 4 3 2}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 2 7 0 7}$</tex> respectively.

title: Modelling of Hot-Spot Temperature and Loss-of-Life for Non-Thermally and Thermally Upgraded Paper in ONAN Transformer Based on IEC 60076-7
authors: Mohd Fahmi Hussin, Najiyah Saleh, Nor Shafiqin Shariffuddin
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This paper presents the Loss-of-Life (LOL) of a 60 MVA Oil Natural Air Natural (ONAN) transformer subjected to thermal stress over time using Non-Thermally Upgraded Paper and Thermally-Upgraded Paper (TUP) as the cellulose insulation paper. The loading and ambient temperature data were first simulated and validated. The Hotspot Temperature (HST) was then calculated using the differential equation as per IEC 60076-7. The LOL was subsequently determined based on the relative ageing rate, considering the impact of temperature on the insulation paper over time. The results indicate a maximum HST of 67.3° C. Over a 15-day loading period, the LOL increases linearly for both NTUP and TUP-insulated transformers. Specifically, the transformer with NTUP recorded a LOL of 0.1049 day, while the TUP-insulated transformer showed a lower LOL of 0.0226 day. These findings indicate that the transformer with NTUP ages more rapidly, confirming the superior thermal endurance of TUP insulation.

title: Resilience Analysis of a Fault-Tolerant MPSoC Interconnection Architecture Under SEU Fault Injection
authors: Thiago H. Rausch, Wesley Grignani, Luigi Dilillo, Douglas R. Melo
abstract: The reliability of on-chip communication is a key enabler for dependable Multiprocessor System-on-Chip (MPSoC) platforms operating in radiation-harsh environments. This work investigates the fault-tolerance of the eXtensible Interconnect Network Architecture (XINA), a configurable Network-on-Chip (NoC) integrated with an AMBA AXI-compatible Network Interface (NI), both of which employ Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) in the control logic and Hamming Error-Correcting Codes (ECC) in the data buffers. The evaluation focuses on the interconnection fabric of a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0} \times \mathbf{1 0 ~ M P S o C}$</tex> prototype composed of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0}$</tex> routers and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0}$</tex> AXI-connected cores, where manager and subordinate IPs act as traffic generators and monitors data integrity under full load. A large-scale fault-injection campaign comprising <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1, 0 0 0}$</tex> simulations with Single-Event Upset (SEU) injections was conducted to assess the impact of fault-tolerant mechanisms across four hardening configurations. The results demonstrate improved reliability, with the fully protected architecture achieving an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 7. 2 \%}$</tex> reduction in observed errors relative to the baseline while maintaining stable throughput and data integrity across all tests.

title: Utilizing Deep Reinforcement Learning for Task Offloading and Resource Management in MEC-Enabled 6G Networks
authors: Mamoon M. Saeed, Rashid A. Saeed, Aisha A Hashim, Zeinab E. Ahmed, Mohammed S. Elbasheir
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Mobile edge computing (MEC) is an important emerging technology in recent years that offers energyconsuming services to mobile operators, who need a lot of storage and computing capabilities. Nevertheless, user devices (UDs) usually have limited resources, and satisfying such requirements can be problematic. In this work, the issue of computationally intensive services and the scarce resources of UDs are mentioned, and a strategy of partial task offloading and partial resource allocation is proposed to maximize job completion within reasonable time limits and minimize energy consumption. The approach uses the deep reinforcement learning algorithm, namely the deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) method, to define the optimization task as a Markov decision process (MDP). The performance outcomes show that the suggested method can greatly decrease energy consumption and raise the job completion rate compared to conventional techniques. Our approach minimizes overall energy consumption while maximizing job completion efficiency. Simulation results reveal that the proposed method reduces energy usage by approximately 84.96% compared to traditional approaches and enables a task completion rate of 95.9% under optimal conditions with a 16 GHz link bandwidth. Additionally, our method achieves a 42.08% increase in overall rewards. It outperforms existing strategies in energy efficiency and task execution speed, demonstrating its effectiveness in managing resources in dynamic 6G environments. Finally, this contribution proves that it is possible to efficiently conduct resource management in MEC-enabled 6G networks with the help of DDPG.

title: Validation of Spectroscopy Performance and Calibration in Compliance with ISO Standards
authors: Nur Ain Mohd Aziz, Abdur Rehman Laili, Hanys Syazwana Harun, Duratul Ain Rozli, Muhammad Hafiz Laili
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Spectroscopy plays a crucial role in various scientific and industrial domains by facilitating precise and dependable measurements of material properties. This study offers an exhaustive study on the validation of spectroscopy performance and the implementation of calibration in accordance with ISO standards. Data were collected using miniature LED-based spectrometers and conventional commercial instruments across 20 spectral tests at a fixed wavelength range of 415 nm to 1000 nm by using Spectralon reflectance standard and Vis-NIR calibration standard as sample for precise background data. Preliminary tests compared the absorbance values from the manufacturer to those from commercial and miniature spectrometers. Additional tests were conducted to assess the correlation and equivalence between results obtained from different instruments. Comparative analysis confirmed compliance with industry standards, reinforcing the importance of validation and calibration in maintaining consistent measurement quality. ISO guidelines help validate spectroscopy performance and implement calibration and allow researchers and practitioners to ensure spectroscopic measurements' accuracy, reliability, and traceability across different laboratory settings and use cases.

title: Resilient Autonomy: Securing Intelligent Systems Against Adversarial Attacks
authors: Pavan Nutalapati, Isan Sahoo, Deepak Kole, Rakesh Keshava, Milan Parikh, Girish Ashok Gajwani, Bindu Mohan Harve
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: As autonomous systems become central to mission-critical operations, their ability to withstand adversarial threats is crucial to ensuring trust, safety, and uninterrupted functionality. This paper introduces a comprehensive framework for resilient autonomy that proactively prevents, detects, and mitigates key adversarial challenges, including sensor spoofing, sensor manipulation, and machine learning model poisoning. We present a layered defense architecture that integrates adversarial robust learning, real-time anomaly detection, and secure control strategies to maintain reliable autonomous behavior in compromised environments. Our hybrid resilience model addresses both inference-time perturbations and upstream vulnerabilities in data acquisition and processing pipelines. The system employs adaptive decision logic and decentralized failover mechanisms to dynamically reconfigure operations upon detection of anomalies or suspicious behavior, thus preserving mission continuity. In addition, we embed explainable AI modules to enhance transparency, facilitate system diagnostics, and support operator trust. We validate the proposed framework using a suite of simulated adversarial scenarios across diverse autonomous agents, including drones, mobile robots, and vehicle models. Results demonstrate significant gains in threat resistance, operational stability, and recovery time when compared to conventional defenses. This work lays the foundation for building self-reliant, attack-aware autonomous systems that are capable of sustaining functionality under adversarial uncertainty.

title: Penetration Testing Analysis on IP Cameras in IoT Ecosystems
authors: Jin Qiuyu, Azana Hafizah Mohd Aman, Zainab S. Attarbashi, Yahya Tuhami, Zulhilmi Che Azizan
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: As Internet of Things (IoT) devices become more common in homes and businesses, it is important to make sure they are safe from hackers and other security threats. This study uses Internet Protocol (IP) cameras as an example to analyze the security of Internet of Things (IoT) devices. Employing the PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard) methodology for penetration testing, the aim was to assess the security performance of the devices without knowledge of the device passwords. The test results revealed that only Denial of Service (DoS) attacks were successful, indicating that IP cameras are relatively secure in other respects. Based on these findings, the paper concluded that while IP cameras can resist unauthorized access to some extent, there is a need to enhance the security of credentials, especially in terms of rapid response and repair work following credential breaches. The study highlights the importance of credential security in IoT devices and suggests directions for future work to improve overall IoT security protection.

title: Secure and Scalable Data Sharing in IoT Environments with Blockchain Integration
authors: Balakrishna Pothineni, Deepak Kole, Isan Sahoo, Nandagopal Seshagiri, Milan Parikh, Ashok Gadiparthi, Bindu Mohan Harve
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The rapid growth of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has revolutionized digital ecosystems, enabling real-time automation and decision-making. However, the large volume and sensitivity of data from distributed IoT networks pose serious challenges in ensuring secure, scalable, and trustworthy data sharing. Centralized architectures are prone to failures, breaches, and limited auditability. This paper proposes a blockchain-integrated framework to overcome these limitations in heterogeneous IoT environments. The model utilizes distributed ledger technology (DLT) to ensure tamper-proof data integrity, decentralized control, and verifiable audit trails. Smart contracts dynamically enforce access policies, while attribute-based encryption (ABE) provides fine-grained control over data access. Lightweight blockchain nodes are deployed at the edge to reduce latency and reliance on cloud infrastructure. A layered architecture ensures seamless interaction among IoT devices, edge nodes, and cloud systems. To evaluate the framework, a prototype was built using ESP32 sensors, Raspberry Pi 4 edge nodes, and a Hyperledger Fabric blockchain network. Data encryption and smart contract execution were tested under realistic conditions. Results show enhanced data security, access control, and system scalability with minimal performance overhead. This framework provides a practical solution for secure data sharing in IoT ecosystems and is well-suited for deployment in smart cities, healthcare, and industrial automation.

title: Blended Learning for Women's Business Development in Rural India: Enhancing Technology Adoption and Bridging the Gender Digital Divide
authors: Bhanu VR, Niksheeta Nayar, Reshma Ramesh, Aswathi Padmavilochanan, Amritha Natarajan, Srividya Sheshadri
abstract: The development of women-owned businesses is gaining increasing attention in the momentum toward women-led sustainable development. Skill development training is acknowledged to play a critical role in preparing individuals for business development and technology-enhanced skill development is in turn, recognized to be an effective mode of training. Despite increased attention being paid to technology based communication, women's access to and awareness of technology remain limited, especially in rural areas. Due to a widening gender digital divide, evidence of business development training programs and the adoption of technology-based business practices among women-owned businesses is lacking. It is within this context that a vocational education curriculum integrating both business and technology development, delivered through a blended learning format, was implemented across rural India. The study conducted semi-structured interviews with 10 women-owned business groups that emerged following their participation in the training program. The study examined the influence of the intervention on the participants' adoption of technology in their business practices. The study's findings bring to the forefront the experience of delivering blended-learning-based vocational and business development training in rural India, as well as factors associated with the adoption of technology following the training period. The findings indicate an association between knowledge, perceptions, and application of technology. These results are of particular relevance for policymakers, researchers, and practitioners seeking to promote women-led development and address the gender digital divide.

title: Enforcing Privacy-By-Design: A Policy-Guided Synthetic Data Generator for GenAI Workflows
authors: Dorababu Nadella, Satyanarayana Raju, Supraja Chinthala
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: This paper proposes policy-driven architecture for synthetic data creation for addressing regulatory compliance requirements of data processing pipelines. The architecture enables organizational and jurisdictional privacy rule invocation dynamically in creating synthetic datasets according to laws such as GDPR, HIPAA, and CPRA. By integrating with enterprise data catalogues and classification tools, the system infers context-specific approaches like redaction, transformation, pseudonymization, and anonymization in real-time. The approach attempts to maintain the analytical utility of synthetic data while constraining compliance risk across data processing pipelines. The architecture leverages open-source tools, including the Synthetic Data Vault (SDV) for synthesis, Azure Purview for the detection of sensitive data, and Apache Atlas for policy enforcement on metadata. Experimental tests on real-world datasets demonstrate that it is feasible to produce regulation-compliant synthetic data with substantial utility, upholding data integrity as well as model performance. This article focuses on the importance of embedding regulatory controls into data generation flows to make AI system development possible in a scalable and compliant manner.

title: Interactive Evolutionary Computation with User Preference Estimation Based on Gaze Information
authors: Akira Hara, Masato Tsuneda, Tomoko Kajiyama
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: Interactive evolutionary computation (IEC) suffers from the issue of user fatigue due to repeated evaluations. To address this, there have been attempts to automate the evaluation process by estimating user preferences from gaze information. However, previous studies have only considered the duration of gaze fixation on items, ignoring the temporal dynamics of gaze movements. In this study, we construct a model that estimates user preferences from gaze data using the Transformer model, which can process time-series data. We integrate the trained model into an IEC system and validate its effectiveness by comparing it to a conventional IEC system using manual evaluation. The experimental results show that the proposed system achieves a comparable level of satisfaction with the optimization outcomes while providing superior usability compared to conventional methods.
