title: Model Order Reduction for Three-Dimensional Inversion of Airborne Transient Electromagnetic Data
authors: Huake Cao, Jianmei Zhou, Jingru Xue, Yanfu Qi, KaiLiang Lu, Xiu Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Airborne transient electromagnetic (ATEM) methods offer high resolution and efficiency but face computational challenges in three-dimensional (3D) inversion. Conventional adjoint forward method for computing sensitivity matrices require computational time several times that of forward modeling, severely limiting inversion efficiency. This paper presents a fast 3D ATEM inversion method that integrates model order reduction (MOR) based on the rational Krylov subspace with finite volume discretization for central-loop configurations. An Octree-based finite volume method (FVM) is employed to discretize the magnetic field governing equations, transforming them into time-domain transfer functions that are solved via a single-pole rational Krylov subspace. By leveraging the subspace and orthogonal basis vectors obtained from forward modeling, spectral decomposition and differential approximation are used to represent the reduced-order model’s transfer function as a superposition of exponentially decaying modes. This enables space-time decoupling and equation-free computation, significantly reducing computational complexity compared to conventional adjoint forward method. The approach also exhibits favorable parallelizability, further enhancing computational efficiency. Numerical examples validate the accuracy of the proposed method and demonstrate significant potential for computational efficiency improvement under the described model and computational environment. Field data application further confirms the practical applicability of the method for imaging complex subsurface geological structures.

title: AI-Native O-RAN Architectures for 6G: Towards Real-Time Adaptation, Conflict Resolution, and Efficient Resource Management
authors: Sifeddine Salmi, Messaoud Ahmed Ouameur, Miloud Bagaa, George C. Alexandropoulos, Abdellah Tahenni, Daniel Massicotte, Adlen Ksentini
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN) enables modular and intelligent control of radio resources through open interfaces and programmable RAN components. As networks evolve toward sixth-generation (6G) systems, the proliferation of autonomous xApps and rApps introduces a critical challenge: Coordinating concurrent AI-driven control actions under tight near-real-time constraints while avoiding instability and conflicting decisions. This paper focuses on two tightly coupled enablers for AI-native O-RAN orchestration: Conflict-aware control and intent-driven automation. We propose an AI-native orchestration framework centered on a CME integrated into the Near-RT RIC, and a complementary LLM-based intent orchestration module deployed in the Non-RT RIC. The CME is designed to autonomously arbitrate conflicting xApp actions by learning adaptive mitigation policies from structured conflict signals, system context, and performance feedback, rather than relying on static priorities or predefined conflict classes. The LLM module translates high-level operator intents into policy constraints and control objectives that guide conflict resolution and xApp behavior. Overall, this work advances AI-native O-RAN orchestration by grounding conflict-aware control and LLM-assisted intent translation in practical measurements, and by outlining a clear path toward scalable, adaptive, and resilient control mechanisms required for future 6G RIC deployments.

title: Indoor Experimental Trial on AI-Based Pilotless Transmission
authors: Hiroto Yamamoto, Atsuya Nakamura, Daisei Uchida, Satoshi Suyama, Huiling Jiang, Dani Korpi, Jaime J.L. Quispe, Kyungpil Lee, Dongwook Kim
abstract: This paper presents a pilotless transmission scheme that combines a learned constellation with a neural receiver, and validates the approach through over-the-air indoor experiments. Motivated by the need for evidence beyond simulation when applying artificial intelligence/machine learning at the air interface, we first use a channel emulator to establish basic characteristics and to evaluate a wide range of parameters including channel-model family, delay spread, signal-to-noise ratio, and velocity. The channel emulator-based experiments show that the proposed pilotless scheme achieves a block error rate (BLER) generally comparable to a 5G New Radio baseline. As the BLERs converge, eliminating pilots (demodulation-reference signals (DMRSs)) overhead provides the expected throughput gain. Moreover, under strong time- and frequency-selective conditions, the conventional pipeline typically requires DMRS pattern optimization, whereas the proposed neural receiver maintains its performance level without such tuning. We then validate the approach in real indoor channels. Over-the-air measurements in an obstructed room, an open lobby, and a corridor enclosed by walls, floor, and ceiling with partial non-line-of-sight yield throughput gains of 6.5% to 18.2%.

title: Hybrid Data/Model-Driven Whole-System Admittance Identification via Single-Bus Injections
authors: Yue Zhu, Yunjie Gu, Yifan Zhang, Jessica S. Döhler, Xiaoyao Zhou, Lingling Fan, Timothy C. Green
abstract: Identifying the whole-system admittance model of an interconnected power system with multiple inverter-based resources (IBRs) typically requires injecting small voltage perturbations at each bus connected with an IBR, which is impractical in both simulations and real-world systems. To overcome this challenge, this paper presents a hybrid data/model-driven approach to identifying the whole-system admittance model through combinations of the measured data and the theoretical network model. By introducing voltage perturbations at a single bus in the system and measuring current responses at all buses, the measured data can be integrated with the known network nodal admittance matrix to reconstruct the whole-system admittance model. The whole-system admittance model, which is a closed-loop model, encompasses the dynamic characteristics of the entire system and facilitates oscillation analysis, root cause tracing, as well as system strength assessment. The proposed new approach is validated through simulations and power hardware-in-the-loop experiments, demonstrating a practical solution for implementing a limited number of injection sources to identify potential oscillations and their root causes for the entire power system.

title: Performance-Enhanced Adaptive Finite-Time Accurate Control of Uncertain Hydro Turbine Governing System with External Disturbances
authors: Chen Xu, Jiyu Zhu, Yadong Yang, Jianye Gong, Qikun Shen
abstract: The hydro turbine governing system (HTGS) plays a critical role in ensuring the overall stability and safe operation of hydropower stations. However, in the hydro turbine governing system, the rotor speed of the generator is the key factor affecting its performance. This paper proposes a finite time sliding mode control scheme aimed at achieving the accurate trajectory tracking of rotor speed to enhance the performance of the HTGS. To overcome the influence of external disturbances, a novel finite-time disturbance observer (FDO) is proposed. Unlike conventional high-order sliding-mode observers, the proposed FDO features a fully decoupled architecture that allows offline observer design and gain tuning independently of the controller synthesis. It requires only first-order differentiability of the lumped disturbances, and its estimation errors converge to zero strictly in finite time. This decoupled design enables exact trajectory tracking with stronger disturbance rejection, faster transient response, and lower online computational cost. Finally, a suite of extensive simulation outcomes is provided, which demonstrates the efficacy of the newly proposed control strategy. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves exact disturbance estimation within 1.2–1.5 s and drives the rotor speed tracking error to zero in 1.8–2.3 s, substantiating the finite-time convergence claim with quantitative metrics.

title: A Novel Angle Standard Using Time Grating With an Accuracy of ±0.005${}^{\prime\prime}$
authors: Bingnan Zhan, Chong Chen, Zhicheng Yu, Xiaokang Liu, Peiyu Yu, Changliang Wu
abstract: Angle, as a fundamental physical quantity, has been jointly proposed by several countries as the eighth fundamental unit in the International System of Units (SIs). However, angle standard for metrological traceability and traceback has stagnated in terms of resolution and uncertainty for 20 years. To address this, we proposed a novel associated constraint sensing principle featuring two concentric, coaxial time gratings within a common plane, replacing traditional dual optical gratings to eliminate concentricity variation during relative rotation. Building on this, a self-calibration and accuracy iterative evolution method was introduced, capitalizing on mutual reference and cross-verification characteristics to the dual time grating configuration. This enables continuous accuracy verification and improvement without higher accuracy reference instruments. Thus, the time grating angle standard device prototype has surpassed the current highest metrology level.

title: Stroke-Based Perception: Discover Novel Oracle Characters
authors: Tingzhang Luo, Yichao Liu, Yulin Xu, Ruizhong Liu, Xin Wang, Haijin Zeng, Shaoguang Huang, Hongyan Zhang
abstract: Oracle bone script is recognized as the oldest ancient Chinese character, which dates back to the Shang Dynasty, providing invaluable information in understanding ancient civilizations. Current automatic recognition methods of oracle characters are often designed in a closed-world setting, where labeled and unlabeled classes are identical. However, novel oracle character categories are often acquired during staged excavation, demanding a generalized ability to decipher the new ones. In this paper, we tackle a novel Oracle Character Category Discovery (OCCD) task, which aims to identify known oracle categories and cluster novel ones by using the knowledge learned from known classes. We propose a novel semi-supervised learning method for OCCD, which leverages the shared knowledge learned from known and unseen categories as guidance to distill informative features for classification. Specifically, we introduce a keypoint matrix to encode the shared knowledge, which is iteratively learned in a coarse-to-fine manner by gradually removing redundant components. To obtain generalized features, we decouple the character features into parts and aggregate relevant information with guidance from the shared keypoint matrix. In addition, we introduce a character memory bank to support the information exchange between the previous and current updates, enhancing the features' robustness. Moreover, we develop multi-level constraints at character, stroke and feature levels to improve the discriminativeness, sharpness and independence of the features while effectively avoiding the collapse of representation. Extensive experimental results on oracle datasets demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms the related state-of-the-art. Code is available at: https://github.com/Clarence-CV/Oracle_GCD.

title: COMET: Co-Optimization of CNN Models Using Efficient-Hardware OBC Techniques
authors: Boyang Chen, Mohd Tasleem Khan, George Goussetis, Mathini Sellathurai, Yuan Ding, João F. C. Mota, Jongeun Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) achieve remarkable accuracy in vision tasks, yet their computational complexity challenges low-power edge deployment. In this work, we present COMET, a framework of CNN models that employ efficient hardware offset-binary coding (OBC) techniques to enable co-optimization of performance and resource utilization. The approach formulates CNN inference using OBC representations applied separately to inputs (Scheme A) and weights (Scheme B), enabling exploitation of bit-width asymmetry. The shift–accumulate operation is modified by incorporating offset-term with the pre-scaled bias. Leveraging symmetries in Schemes A and B, we introduce four look-up table (LUT) techniques—parallel, shared, split, and hybrid—and evaluate their efficiency. Building on this foundation, we develop a general matrix multiplication core using the im2col transformation for efficient CNN acceleration. We consider LeNet-5 and All-CNN-C to demonstrate that the OBC-GEMM core efficiently supports modern workloads. Evaluation shows that COMET enables efficient FPGA deployment compared to state-of-the-art designs, with negligible accuracy loss, demonstrating its efficiency and scalability across diverse network architectures.

title: Parameter Estimation Based on Coupled Canonical Polyadic Decomposition for FDA-MIMO Radar With Unfolded Coprime Framework
authors: Wenshuai Wang, Xianpeng Wang, Yuehao Guo, Mingcheng Fu, Feifei Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The monostatic frequency diverse array multiple-input multiple-output (FDA-MIMO) radar with unfolded coprime framework has an enlarged array aperture and extended signal bandwidth, which can significantly improve the resolution of range and angle parameter estimation for FDA-MIMO radar. Furthermore, tensor analysis techniques can effectively exploit the multidimensional structural characteristics inherent in signals to achieve superior estimation performance. Therefore, this paper mainly employs tensor analysis to improve the parameter estimation performance of FDA-MIMO radar, and proposes a joint angle and range estimation algorithm based on coupled canonical polyadic decomposition (C-CPD). The proposed algorithm first models the received signal as a third-order tensor. Then, based on the structure of the Vandermonde-like matrix, a C-CPD model is designed. Subsequently, the alternating least squares (ALS) technique is utilized to obtain steering matrix estimates. Finally, the coprime characteristic is exploited to resolve phase ambiguity of the array framework, and least squares fitting is employed to obtain angle and range estimates. Simulation experiments demonstrate the superiority and effectiveness of the proposed algorithm.

title: A 95.86%-Effieient 48V/1.8V ZVS Voltage Regulator Based on Current Ninefold Rectifier
authors: Zehui Li, Qishan Pan, Junrui Liang, Minfan Fu, Haoyu Wang
abstract: In 48 V data-center voltage regulation, the current doubler rectifier (CDR) offers a high step-down ratio and structural simplicity but suffers from hard-switching, elevated voltage stress, and load-dependent voltage spikes. To overcome these limitations, this manuscript proposes a voltage regulator based on the current ninefold rectifier (CNR) topology. The proposed design achieves zero-voltage switching (ZVS) for all primary-side switches and employs Δ-connected primary windings to cancel circulating current, thereby reducing both switching loss and conduction loss while enhancing current-transfer capability. A 48 V/1.8 V, 180 A prototype operating at 500 kHz is built and tested. Experimental results confirm primary-side ZVS operation and demonstrate a peak efficiency of 95.86% and a full-load efficiency of 94.29%, verifying the efficiency benefits enabled by ZVS and reduced circulating current.

title: Comparative Performance Evaluation of Large Language Models for Domain-Specific Web Content Extraction
authors: Hendrik Hendrik, Silmi Fauziati, Adhistya Erna Permanasari
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative evaluation of seven Large Language Models (LLMs) for domain-specific web content extraction, addressing the gap in performance assessment across model families. We developed a web-based extraction tool supporting multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, DeepSeek) and evaluated their capabilities using halal tourism as a case study domain. Our evaluation framework assessed extraction quality through precision, recall, F1-score, accuracy, and processing time metrics across three Japanese halal tourism websites, focusing on restaurant and mosque entity extraction. Results reveal significant performance variations among model families, with GPT-4o-mini achieving the highest performance (F1-score: 0.973) while maintaining exceptional stability and computational efficiency. OpenAI models consistently outperformed other families across all metrics, while Anthropic models exhibited conservative extraction behavior with high precision but lower recall. The study demonstrates that model size does not guarantee superior performance and establishes evidence-based guidelines for LLM selection in domain-specific extraction tasks. Our findings provide practitioners with actionable insights for implementing robust knowledge extraction systems, contributing to the broader understanding of LLM capabilities in specialized applications.

title: Mitigation of SETs Under Process Variability Effects: Circuit-Level Strategies for Reliable Design
authors: Marina L. Dias, Bernardo B. Sandoval, Paulo F. Butzen, Ricardo A. L. Reis, Cristina Meinhardt
abstract: This paper investigates the combined effects of process variability and radiation on a set of circuit-level hardening techniques. We assess the radiation robustness of hardened inverter topologies by examining the critical Linear Energy Transfer Threshold (LETth) for different fault configurations and process variability scenarios. The simulations incorporate variations in the number of fins and statistical process parameters to evaluate how these factors influence the robustness, energy consumption, and delay of hardened topologies. The results discuss the trade-offs involved in designing reliable FinFET circuits that operate under both process variability and radiation environments. Additionally, the impact of implementing these strategies in a RISC-V processor is discussed, indicating that introducing these approaches in the arithmetic blocks has a negligible effect on area, power, and delay, while potentially enhancing the overall robustness of the arithmetic units.

title: Fast Statistical Estimation of Circuit-Level Cross Section Considering Logical Masking Effects
authors: Clayton R. Farias, Tiago R. Balen, Paulo F. Butzen
abstract: The technology scaling in the semiconductor industry increases the susceptibility of integrated circuits to radiation, especially to single-event effects in planar processes. The circuit vulnerability can be evaluated using the cross section metric. Determining the cross section requires an expensive radiation test. Therefore, predicting the circuit susceptibility, even at the cost of accuracy, enables early design choices to reduce the expected error rate of an electronic system intended for deployment in radiation environments. This work presents a fast statistical procedure for circuit-level cross section estimation. The procedure explores the sensitive areas of the circuit logic gates according to the input vectors. It also evaluates the impact of logical masking on the effectively estimated cross section of the combinational circuits. By exploring the impact of logical masking in the benchmarks ISCAS85, EPFL15, and ITC99, the results indicate an overestimation of the cross section of up to 50%.

title: Multi-Channel Chitosan Coated Quartz Crystal Microbalance Device for Continuous Detection of Isopropyl Alcohol
authors: Ahmad Fairuzabadi Mohd Mansor, Anis Nurashikin Nordin, M. Khairul Faisal Muhammad Atan, Aliza Aini Md Ralib, Lun Hao Tung, Zambri Samsudin
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) is a widely used volatile organic compound, yet its uncontrolled presence poses health and safety risks. This study presents the evaluation of a multichannel quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) sensor coated with chitosan for the real-time detection of IPA vapor. The sensor integrates three independent sensing units on a single substrate, fabricated using gold. A custom 3D-printed packaging system was also developed to enable gas flow channels for continuous monitoring. The chitosan coating, optimized between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1-10 \mu \mathrm{L}$</tex>, demonstrated effective interaction with IPA resulting in measurable frequency shifts. Experimental results revealed that sensor response was highly sensitive to IPA concentrations, with a linear detection range between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.5-15 \text{ppm}\left(\mathrm{R}^{2}=0.9806\right)$</tex> and an estimated limit of detection (LOD) of 10.7 ppm. At higher concentrations, a logarithmic calibration model (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{2} = 0.949$</tex>) captured saturation effects. The device exhibited rapid response (~10 s), reversibility, and stability, confirming its suitability for continuous air quality monitoring and industrial safety applications.

title: Gender Inclusivity in Technology- Enhanced Teacher Education in Ghana
authors: Gideon Yeboah, Madeline B. Gomez, Puneeta Pandey, Christopher Coley
abstract: The paper explores the issue of gender inclusion in technology-enhanced teacher education among trainee teachers in the Ashanti Region of Ghana, particularly about perceptions of ICT as essential to teaching and their levels of confidence. A descriptive quantitative design was adopted. Data were collected via an online questionnaire, with 200 trainees as participants, and SPSS was used for the data analysis. The results reflect the importance of ICTs in both male and female trainees but show more confidence on the part of males in using the ICTs. There was no statistical evidence showing that gender contributes to confidence in either male or female participants through one-way ANOVA, it may be inferred that such differences exist within the perceptions and not the participants' skills. This means that the policy thrust should focus on gender-sensitive policies on ICT training when boosting equal confidence for digitally literate teacher education in Ghana.

title: Gender Dynamics of Cyber Security Awareness in Kerala, India: Statistical and Machine Learning Approach
authors: Swapna M P, Devi Rajeev, J Ramkumar, Saranya Chandran
abstract: Gender-based cyber security awareness examines the varying levels of understanding and response to online threats among males, females, and others. This study offers insights into how demographic factors affect digital safety practices and awareness programs. In this study, we analyzed the relationship between cyber threat awareness score and demographic characteristics (age, gender, education, occupation and location) of 1000 plus participants. Males demonstrated slightly higher awareness levels (mean 3.6426) compared to females (3.5677) and other gender categories (3.4545). Machine learning algorithms like Logistic Regression, RandomForest, and Support Vector Machines, were applied for the prediction of cyber threat awareness. Results indicated that gender plays a role in creating awareness as there were variations in the different educational and occupational classes. Random Forests provided the highest accuracy of 98%, offering superior accuracy and insight into the most influential factors for predicting cyber security awareness. The findings emphasize the need for gender-sensitive, demographically tailored cyber security education programs, especially in rural areas, and provide a foundation for developing more effective awareness campaigns in Kerala and potentially other states of India.

title: Corrections to “Unbiased Estimation of Three-Dimensional Deformation From SAR Interferometry and GNSS Observations”
authors: Junichi Susaki, Yuka Teranishi
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In the above article [1], we made a mistake in (2). We correct it as follows. \begin{align*} \left[ {\begin{array}{c} {V_{SAR}^{ASC}}\\ {V_{SAR}^{DES}}\\ 0 \end{array}} \right] \!\!\!=\!\!\! & \left[ {\begin{array}{ccc} { - \cos {{\varphi }_{ASC}}\sin {{\theta }_{ASC}}}&{\sin {{\varphi }_{ASC}}\sin {{\theta }_{ASC}}}&{\cos {{\theta }_{ASC}}}\\ { - \cos {{\varphi }_{DES}}\sin {{\theta }_{DES}}}&{\sin {{\varphi }_{DES}}\sin {{\theta }_{DES}}}&{\cos {{\theta }_{DES}}}\\ 0&1&0 \end{array}}\! \right] \\ \left[ {\begin{array}{c} {{{V}^e}}\\ {{{V}^n}}\\ {{{V}^u}} \end{array}} \right] =& \left[ {\begin{array}{ccc} {u_e^{ASC}}&{u_n^{ASC}}&{u_u^{ASC}}\\ {u_e^{DES}}&{u_n^{DES}}&{u_u^{DES}}\\ 0&1&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{c} {{{V}^e}}\\ {{{V}^n}}\\ {{{V}^u}} \end{array}} \right]. \tag{2} \end{align*}
